nodes.title: Brouwer Fixed Point Theorem
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқҗ·рқ‘ӣ вҶ’ рқҗ·рқ‘ӣ be a continuous map from the (unit) disk in в„қрқ‘ӣ to itself. Then рқ‘“ has a fixed point, i.e., there is some point рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗ·рқ‘ӣ such that рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҙ) = рқ‘Ҙ.

For рқ‘ӣ = 1 this is a well-known result from calculus: The graph of any continuous map рқ‘“ вҲ¶ [0, 1] вҶ’ [0, 1] must cross the diagonal рқ‘Ұ = рқ‘Ҙ for some рқ‘ҘвҲ— вҲҲ [0, 1]. Hence, рқ‘“ (рқ‘ҘвҲ— ) = рқ‘ҘвҲ— . See Figure 1.4. The second theorem is the fundamental theorem of algebra.
nodes.formalStatement: Let $f : D^n \to D^n$ be a continuous map from the unit disk in $\mathbb{R}^n$ to itself. Then there exists $x \in D^n$ such that $f(x)=x$.
nodes.explanation: The theorem asserts that every continuous self-map of the closed unit disk has a fixed point. In the introduction it is used as a motivating example: in dimension one, the graph of a continuous map $[0,1]\to[0,1]$ must cross the diagonal.

nodes.title: Fundamental Theorem of Algebra
nodes.originalText:
A polynomial equation

рқ‘§рқ‘ӣ + рқ‘Һрқ‘ӣвҲ’1 рқ‘§рқ‘ӣвҲ’1 + вӢҜ + рқ‘Һ1 рқ‘§ + рқ‘Һ0 = 0

of degree рқ‘ӣ > 0 with complex coefficients has at least one complex root.

Chapter 1. Introduction 7

рқ‘Ұ (1, 1) вҲҷ

рқ‘Ұ=рқ‘Ҙ рқ‘Ұ = рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҙ) рқ‘Ҙ

рқ‘ҘвҲ— рқ‘Ҙ

Figure 1.4: The graph of any continuous map from [0, 1] to [0, 1] must cross the diagonal.

To prove it we will use the fact that the fundamental group of the circle is isomorphic to the group of integers. The fundamental theorem of algebra may be proved in many different ways, including using only algebraic techniques and analysis. However, the proof we will provide (based on [4, В§56]) is a fairly simple corollary of the computation of the fundamental group of the circle.

2. Continuous maps

2.1 Metric spaces From calculus we know what to mean by a continuous map from в„қрқ‘ӣ to в„қрқ‘ҡ : a map рқ‘“ вҲ¶ в„қрқ‘ӣ вҶ’ в„қрқ‘ҡ is continuous at рқ‘қ вҲҲ в„қрқ‘ӣ if for all рқң– > 0 there exists a рқӣҝ > 0 such that if вҖ–рқ‘қ вҲ’ рқ‘һвҖ–в„қрқ‘ӣ < рқӣҝ, then вҖ–рқ‘“ (рқ‘қ) вҲ’ рқ‘“ (рқ‘һ)вҖ–в„қрқ‘ҡ < рқң–. Here вҖ– вӢ… вҖ–в„қрқ‘ӣ denotes the Euclidean norm in в„қрқ‘ӣ . Similarly, вҖ– вӢ… вҖ–в„қрқ‘ҡ denotes the Euclidean norm in в„қрқ‘ҡ . Topological spaces provide the most general setting for which the concept of continuity makes sense. Before we get to the concept of a topological space, let us consider metric spaces. Metric spaces allow us to speak of distance between elements. Using the notion of distance between elements we can make sense of continuity of maps between metric spaces.
nodes.formalStatement: Every nonconstant polynomial with complex coefficients has at least one complex root. Equivalently, every polynomial equation $z^n+a_{n-1}z^{n-1}+\cdots+a_1z+a_0=0$ with $n>0$ has a solution in $\mathbb{C}$.
nodes.explanation: This introductory theorem is presented as a later application of topology: the text notes that it will be proved using the computation of the fundamental group of the circle.

nodes.title: Metric spaces
nodes.originalText:
A metric space (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘‘) is a non-empty set рқ‘Ӣ together with a map рқ‘‘ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ в„қ called a metric such that the following properties hold:

M1 рқ‘‘(рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) в©ҫ 0 for all рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ, and рқ‘‘(рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) = 0 if and only if рқ‘Ҙ = рқ‘Ұ;

M2 рқ‘‘(рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) = рқ‘‘(рқ‘Ұ, рқ‘Ҙ) for all рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ;

M3 рқ‘‘(рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘§) в©Ҫ рқ‘‘(рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) + рқ‘‘(рқ‘Ұ, рқ‘§) for all рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ, рқ‘§ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ.

The first condition says that the distance between two elements is always positive, and equal to zero if and only if the two elements are the same. The second condition says that distance is symmetric. The third condition says that the triangle inequality holds. The metric рқ‘‘ is sometimes also referred to as a distance function.

2.2 (в„қрқ‘ӣ seen as a metric space) Let рқ‘Ӣ = в„қ and рқ‘‘ be the map defined by рқ‘‘(рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) = Example вҲҡ |рқ‘Ҙ вҲ’ рқ‘Ұ|(= (рқ‘Ҙ вҲ’ рқ‘Ұ)2 ). The first two requirements for рқ‘‘ are clearly satisfied, and the third follows from the usual triangle inequality for real numbers,

рқ‘‘(рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘§) = |рқ‘Ҙ вҲ’ рқ‘§| = |(рқ‘Ҙ вҲ’ рқ‘Ұ) + (рқ‘Ұ вҲ’ рқ‘§)| в©Ҫ |рқ‘Ҙ вҲ’ рқ‘Ұ| + |рқ‘Ұ вҲ’ рқ‘§| = рқ‘‘(рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) + рқ‘‘(рқ‘Ұ, рқ‘§).

For рқ‘Ӣ = в„қрқ‘ӣ with рқ‘ӣ > 0 an integer, вҲҡ let рқ‘‘(рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) = вҖ–рқ‘Ҙ вҲ’ рқ‘ҰвҖ– where вҖ– вӢ… вҖ– is the Euclidean norm, e.g., for рқ‘ӣ = 2, рқ‘‘(рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) = вҖ–рқ‘Ҙ вҲ’ рқ‘ҰвҖ– = (рқ‘Ҙ1 вҲ’ рқ‘Ұ1 )2 + (рқ‘Ҙ2 вҲ’ рқ‘Ұ2 )2 . Again, the first two requirements for рқ‘‘ are clearly satisfied. The third requirement follows from the triangle inequality for vectors in в„қрқ‘ӣ .

We may equip в„қрқ‘ӣ with other metrics than the one described in Example 2.2. For instance, for рқ‘Ӣ = в„қ2 , let рқ‘‘(рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) = |рқ‘Ҙ1 вҲ’ рқ‘Ұ1 | + |рқ‘Ҙ2 вҲ’ рқ‘Ұ2 |.

This is known as the taxi cab metric.

Chapter 2. Continuous maps 9

We say that two metrics рқ‘‘1 and рқ‘‘2 on the same set рқ‘Ӣ are equivalent if there are constants рқҗҝ and рқ‘Җ such that рқ‘‘1 (рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) в©Ҫ рқҗҝрқ‘‘2 (рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) and рқ‘‘2 (рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) в©Ҫ рқ‘Җрқ‘‘1 (рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ)

for all рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ.
nodes.formalStatement: A metric space is a pair $(X,d)$ where $X$ is a nonempty set and $d:X\times X\to\mathbb{R}$ satisfies, for all $x,y,z\in X$: $d(x,y)\geq 0$ with equality if and only if $x=y$; $d(x,y)=d(y,x)$; and $d(x,z)\leq d(x,y)+d(y,z)$.
nodes.explanation: A metric space is a set equipped with a distance function. The three axioms give positivity, symmetry, and the triangle inequality, making metric spaces the starting point for continuity and open-set definitions.

nodes.title: Discrete metric spaces
nodes.originalText: For any set рқ‘Ӣ, let рқ‘‘ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ в„қ be the map given by { 1 рқ‘Ҙ вү  рқ‘Ұ, рқ‘‘(рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) = 0 рқ‘Ҙ = рқ‘Ұ. We call рқ‘‘ the discrete metric on рқ‘Ӣ.
nodes.formalStatement: For any set $X$, define $d:X\times X\to\mathbb{R}$ by $d(x,y)=0$ if $x=y$ and $d(x,y)=1$ if $x\neq y$. This is the discrete metric on $X$.
nodes.explanation: The discrete metric gives every distinct pair of points distance one. It is the simplest example of a metric space and later makes open balls especially easy to compute.

nodes.title: C[a,b] with the Sup Metric
nodes.originalText: Let рқ‘Ӣ = рқҗ¶[рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ], i.e., the set of continuous maps from the interval рқҗј = [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ] вҠҶ в„қ to в„қ, and let рқ‘‘(рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) = max |рқ‘Ҙ(рқ‘–) вҲ’ рқ‘Ұ(рқ‘–)|. рқ‘–вҲҲрқҗј
nodes.formalStatement: Let $X=C[a,b]$ be the set of continuous maps from $[a,b]\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ to $\mathbb{R}$. The function $d(x,y)=\max_{i\in[a,b]} |x(i)-y(i)|$ defines a metric on $X$.
nodes.explanation: This example puts a metric on a function space using maximum pointwise distance. It shows that metric spaces need not consist of points in Euclidean space.

nodes.title: Restricted Metric on a Subset
nodes.originalText:
If рқ‘‘ is a metric on a set рқ‘Ӣ, and рқҗҙ вҠҶ рқ‘Ӣ is any subset of рқ‘Ӣ, then рқ‘‘ is also a metric on рқҗҙ.

2.2 Continuous maps between metric spaces The definition of continuity of maps between metric spaces is completely analogous to the situation that we have from calculus.
nodes.formalStatement: If $d$ is a metric on a set $X$ and $A\subseteq X$, then the restriction of $d$ to $A\times A$ is a metric on $A$.
nodes.explanation: A metric can be restricted to any subset of its space. This is the metric-space precursor to later subspace constructions.

nodes.title: Continuous maps between metric spaces
nodes.originalText:
Let (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘‘рқ‘Ӣ ) and (рқ‘Ң , рқ‘‘рқ‘Ң ) be two metric spaces. A map рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң is continuous at рқ‘қ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ if for all рқң– > 0 there is a рқӣҝ > 0 such that if рқ‘‘рқ‘Ӣ (рқ‘қ, рқ‘һ) < рқӣҝ then рқ‘‘рқ‘Ң (рқ‘“ (рқ‘қ), рқ‘“ (рқ‘һ)) < рқң–. If рқ‘“ is continuous at every рқ‘қ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ, we say that рқ‘“ is continuous.

To get us to the setting of topological spaces we will need the concept of open and closed sets.
nodes.formalStatement: Let $(X,d_X)$ and $(Y,d_Y)$ be metric spaces. A map $f:X\to Y$ is continuous at $p\in X$ if for every $\epsilon>0$ there exists $\delta>0$ such that $d_X(p,q)<\delta$ implies $d_Y(f(p),f(q))<\epsilon$. The map $f$ is continuous if it is continuous at every point of $X$.
nodes.explanation: Continuity between metric spaces is the epsilon-delta condition expressed using the two metrics. This definition is the metric version that will later be reformulated using open sets.

nodes.title: Open and closed balls
nodes.originalText:
Let (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘‘) be a metric space, and let рқ‘Һ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘ҹ > 0 be real number. The open ball centered at рқ‘Һ with radius рқ‘ҹ is the subset

рқ–Ў(рқ‘Һ; рқ‘ҹ) = {рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ вҲЈ рқ‘‘(рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Һ) < рқ‘ҹ}

of рқ‘Ӣ. The closed ball centered at рқ‘Һ with radius рқ‘ҹ is the subset

рқ–Ў(рқ‘Һ; рқ‘ҹ) = {рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ вҲЈ рқ‘‘(рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Һ) в©Ҫ рқ‘ҹ}

of рқ‘Ӣ.

In Euclidean space with the usual metric (induced from Euclidean norm), a ball (as defined above) is precisely what we think of as a ball in everyday language. Open balls are sometimes referred to as simply balls, and closed balls are sometimes referred to as discs, e.g. Theorem 1.1.

10 2.2. Continuous maps between metric spaces
nodes.formalStatement: Let $(X,d)$ be a metric space, let $a\in X$, and let $r>0$. The open ball centered at $a$ with radius $r$ is $B(a;r)=\{x\in X\mid d(x,a)<r\}$, and the closed ball centered at $a$ with radius $r$ is $\overline{B}(a;r)=\{x\in X\mid d(x,a)\leq r\}$.
nodes.explanation: Open and closed balls are the basic local neighborhoods determined by a metric. They are used to define open sets and to express continuity at a point.

nodes.title: Open balls in discrete metric spaces
nodes.originalText: Let (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘‘) be the metric space defined in Example 2.3. Then рқ–Ў(рқ‘Ҙ; рқ‘ҹ1 ) = {рқ‘Ҙ} and рқ–Ў(рқ‘Ҙ; рқ‘ҹ2 ) = рқ‘Ӣ for all 0 < рқ‘ҹ1 в©Ҫ 1 and all рқ‘ҹ2 > 1.
nodes.formalStatement: In a discrete metric space $(X,d)$, $B(x;r)=\{x\}$ for $0<r\leq 1$, while $B(x;r)=X$ for $r>1$.
nodes.explanation: This example computes metric balls in the discrete metric. It shows how the radius controls whether an open ball isolates one point or contains the whole space.

nodes.title: Open and closed sets
nodes.originalText:
Let (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘‘) be a metric space. A subset рқҗҙ вҠҶ рқ‘Ӣ is open in рқ‘Ӣ if for every point рқ‘Һ вҲҲ рқҗҙ, there is an open ball рқ–Ў(рқ‘Һ; рқ‘ҹ) about рқ‘Һ contained in рқҗҙ. We say that рқҗҙ is closed in рқ‘Ӣ if the complement рқҗҙрқ‘җ = рқ‘Ӣ в§ө рқҗҙ = {рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ вҲЈ рқ‘Ҙ вҲү рқҗҙ} is open.

Most subsets are neither open nor closed. Subsets that are both open and closed are sometimes referred to as clopen. In particular, both вҲ… and рқ‘Ӣ are clopen in рқ‘Ӣ.
nodes.formalStatement: Let $(X,d)$ be a metric space. A subset $A\subseteq X$ is open in $X$ if for every $a\in A$ there exists an open ball $B(a;r)$ with $B(a;r)\subseteq A$. The subset $A$ is closed in $X$ if its complement $A^c=X\setminus A$ is open.
nodes.explanation: Open sets are subsets containing a metric ball around each of their points. Closed sets are defined by open complements, and this language prepares the transition from metric spaces to topological spaces.

nodes.title: Metric Balls Are Open or Closed
nodes.originalText:
Let (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘‘) be a metric space, рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘ҹ > 0 a real number. Then the open ball рқ–Ў(рқ‘Ҙ; рқ‘ҹ) вҠҶ рқ‘Ӣ is open in рқ‘Ӣ, and the closed ball рқ–Ў(рқ‘Ҙ; рқ‘ҹ) вҠҶ рқ‘Ӣ is closed in рқ‘Ӣ.

Proof. We prove the statement about open balls. The statement about closed balls follows from a similar argument. Assume that рқ‘Ұ вҲҲ рқ–Ў(рқ‘Ҙ; рқ‘ҹ). We need to prove that there is an open ball рқ–Ў(рқ‘Ұ; рқң–) about рқ‘Ұ that is contained in рқ–Ў(рқ‘Ҙ; рқ‘ҹ). Let рқң– = рқ‘ҹ вҲ’ рқ‘‘(рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ). By the triangle inequality of the metric рқ‘‘, M3, we have that for рқ‘§ вҲҲ рқ–Ў(рқ‘Ұ; рқң–),

рқ‘‘(рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘§) в©Ҫ рқ‘‘(рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) + рқ‘‘(рқ‘Ұ, рқ‘§) < рқ‘‘(рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) + рқң– = рқ‘‘(рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) + рқ‘ҹ вҲ’ рқ‘‘(рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) = рқ‘ҹ.

Hence, рқ–Ў(рқ‘Ұ; рқң–) вҠҶ рқ–Ў(рқ‘Ҙ; рқ‘ҹ).

For a metric space (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘‘), a subset рқҗҙ вҠҶ рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ, we say that: (i) рқ‘Ҙ is an interior point of рқҗҙ if there is an open ball рқ–Ў(рқ‘Ҙ; рқ‘ҹ) about рқ‘Ҙ which is contained in рқҗҙ, (ii) рқ‘Ҙ is an exterior point of рқҗҙ if there is an open ball рқ–Ў(рқ‘Ҙ; рқ‘ҹ) which is contained in рқҗҙрқ‘җ and (iii) рқ‘Ҙ is a boundary point if all open balls about рқ‘Ҙ contains points in рқҗҙ and in рқҗҙрқ‘җ . Hence, рқҗҙ is open in рқ‘Ӣ if and only if рқҗҙ only consists of its interior points. An interior point will always belong to рқҗҙ. An exterior point will never belong to рқҗҙ. A boundary point will some times belong to рқҗҙ, and some times to рқҗҙрқ‘җ .
nodes.formalStatement: Let $(X,d)$ be a metric space, let $x\in X$, and let $r>0$. Then the open ball $B(x;r)$ is open in $X$, and the closed ball $\overline{B}(x;r)$ is closed in $X$.
nodes.explanation: This lemma verifies that the basic metric balls behave as their names suggest. The proof for open balls uses the triangle inequality to fit a smaller ball around any chosen point.

nodes.title: Neighborhoods
nodes.originalText: Let (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘‘) be a metric space, рқҗҙ a subset of рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ. We say that рқҗҙ is a neighborhood of рқ‘Ҙ if there is an open ball about рқ‘Ҙ that is contained in рқҗҙ. We say that рқҗҙ is an open neighborhood (of рқ‘Ҙ) if рқҗҙ itself is open.
nodes.formalStatement: Let $(X,d)$ be a metric space, let $A\subseteq X$, and let $x\in X$. The set $A$ is a neighborhood of $x$ if there is an open ball about $x$ contained in $A$. It is an open neighborhood of $x$ if $A$ itself is open.
nodes.explanation: A neighborhood records the idea that a set contains some open ball around a point. This gives a pointwise way to discuss local behavior in metric spaces.

nodes.title: Continuity at a point
nodes.originalText:
Let (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘‘рқ‘Ӣ ) and (рқ‘Ң , рқ‘‘рқ‘Ң ) be two metric spaces, and let рқ‘қ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ. A map рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң is continuous at рқ‘қ if and only if for all neighborhoods рқҗө of рқ‘“ (рқ‘қ), there is a neighborhood рқҗҙ of рқ‘қ such that рқ‘“ (рқҗҙ) вҠҶ рқҗө.

Chapter 2. Continuous maps 11

Proof. Assume that рқ‘“ is continuous at рқ‘қ. If рқҗө is a neighborhood of рқ‘“ (рқ‘қ), then, by definition, there is an open ball рқ–Ўрқ‘Ң (рқ‘“ (рқ‘қ); рқң–) about рқ‘“ (рқ‘қ) that is contained in рқҗө. Since рқ‘“ is continuous at рқ‘қ, there is a рқӣҝ > 0 such that if рқ‘‘рқ‘Ӣ (рқ‘қ, рқ‘һ) < рқӣҝ, then рқ‘‘рқ‘Ң (рқ‘“ (рқ‘қ), рқ‘“ (рқ‘һ)) < рқң–. Hence, рқ‘“ (рқ–Ўрқ‘Ӣ (рқ‘қ; рқӣҝ)) вҠҶ рқ–Ўрқ‘Ң (рқ‘“ (рқ‘қ); рқң–) вҠҶ рқҗө. That is, if we let рқҗҙ = рқ–Ўрқ‘Ӣ (рқ‘қ, рқӣҝ), then for all neighborhoods рқҗө of рқ‘“ (рқ‘қ), we have that рқ‘“ (рқҗҙ) вҠҶ рқҗө where рқҗҙ is a neighborhood of рқ‘қ. Assume that for all neighborhoods рқҗө of рқ‘“ (рқ‘қ), there is a neighborhood рқҗҙ of рқ‘қ such that рқ‘“ (рқҗҙ) вҠҶ рқҗө. We need to prove that for all рқң– > 0, there is a рқӣҝ > 0 such that if рқ‘‘рқ‘Ӣ (рқ‘қ, рқ‘һ) < рқӣҝ, then рқ‘‘рқ‘Ң (рқ‘“ (рқ‘қ), рқ‘“ (рқ‘һ)) < рқң–. By utilizing the fact that рқҗө = рқ–Ўрқ‘Ң (рқ‘“ (рқ‘қ); рқң–) is a neighborhood of рқ‘“ (рқ‘қ), then, by assumption, there must be a neighborhood рқҗҙ of рқ‘қ such that рқ‘“ (рқҗҙ) вҠҶ рқҗө. Since рқҗҙ is a neighborhood of рқ‘қ, there is an open ball рқ–Ўрқ‘Ӣ (рқ‘қ; рқӣҝ) about рқ‘қ that is contained in рқҗҙ. Now assume that рқ‘‘рқ‘Ӣ (рқ‘қ, рқ‘қвҖІ ) < рқӣҝ. Then рқ‘қвҖІ вҲҲ рқ–Ўрқ‘Ӣ (рқ‘қ; рқӣҝ) вҠҶ рқҗҙ. Thus рқ‘“ (рқ‘қвҖІ ) вҲҲ рқҗө = рқ–Ўрқ‘Ң (рқ‘“ (рқ‘қ); рқң–), and hence, рқ‘‘рқ‘Ң (рқ‘“ (рқ‘қ), рқ‘“ (рқ‘қвҖІ )) < рқң–. Thus рқ‘“ is continuous at рқ‘қ.

The following theorem gives an alternative description of continuous maps between metric spaces.
nodes.formalStatement: Let $(X,d_X)$ and $(Y,d_Y)$ be metric spaces, let $p\in X$, and let $f:X\to Y$. Then $f$ is continuous at $p$ if and only if for every neighborhood $B$ of $f(p)$ there exists a neighborhood $A$ of $p$ such that $f(A)\subseteq B$.
nodes.explanation: This theorem recasts epsilon-delta continuity using neighborhoods. The proof translates neighborhoods into open balls and then applies the metric definition of continuity.

nodes.title: Preimage Criterion for Metric Continuity
nodes.originalText:
Let (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘‘рқ‘Ӣ ) and (рқ‘Ң , рқ‘‘рқ‘Ң ) be two metric spaces. A map рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң is continuous if and only if for every subset рқҗө вҠҶ рқ‘Ң open in рқ‘Ң , the preimage of рқҗө under рқ‘“ ,

рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқҗө) = {рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ вҲЈ рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҙ) вҲҲ рқҗө} вҠҶ рқ‘Ӣ,

is open in рқ‘Ӣ.

Proof. Assume that рқ‘“ is continuous. For рқҗө вҠҶ рқ‘Ң open in рқ‘Ң , we have to prove that рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқҗө) вҠҶ рқ‘Ӣ is open in рқ‘Ӣ. Let рқ‘Һ вҲҲ рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқҗө). We want to prove that there is an open ball about рқ‘Һ in рқ‘Ӣ that is contained in рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқҗө). By assumption, рқҗө вҠҶ рқ‘Ң is open in рқ‘Ң . Hence, there is an рқң– > 0 such that рқ–Ўрқ‘Ң (рқ‘“ (рқ‘Һ); рқң–) вҠҶ рқҗө. From the assumption that рқ‘“ is continuous there is a рқӣҝ > 0 such that рқ–Ўрқ‘Ӣ (рқ‘Һ; рқӣҝ) вҠҶ рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ–Ўрқ‘Ң (рқ‘“ (рқ‘Һ); рқң–)) вҠҶ рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқҗө). We now prove the opposite implication. Assume that for every subset рқҗө вҠҶ рқ‘Ң open in рқ‘Ң , the preimage рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқҗө) of рқҗө under рқ‘“ is open in рқ‘Ӣ. Let рқ‘Һ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ and рқң– > 0 be a real number. From the first assumption it follows that рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ–Ўрқ‘Ң (рқ‘“ (рқ‘Һ); рқң–)) вҠҶ рқ‘Ӣ is open in рқ‘Ӣ. As рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ–Ўрқ‘Ң (рқ‘“ (рқ‘Һ); рқң–)) is open and contains рқ‘Һ, there is a рқӣҝ > 0 such that рқ–Ўрқ‘Ӣ (рқ‘Һ; рқӣҝ) вҠҶ рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ–Ўрқ‘Ң (рқ‘“ (рқ‘Һ); рқң–)). Thus рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ–Ўрқ‘Ӣ (рқ‘Һ; рқӣҝ) implies that рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҙ) вҲҲ рқ–Ўрқ‘Ң (рқ‘“ (рқ‘Һ); рқң–). Hence, рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң is continuous.

Let рқҗҙ and рқҗө be sets, and let рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқҗҙ вҶ’ рқҗө. Then рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқҗө) will always exist even if there is no inverse map. In the cases where рқ‘“ has an inverse there is no ambiguity. If рқ‘Ҳ and рқ‘ү are both subsets of рқҗө then

рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҳ вҲӘ рқ‘ү ) = рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҳ ) вҲӘ рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘ү ) and рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҳ вҲ© рқ‘ү ) = рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҳ ) вҲ© рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘ү ),

and furthermore, if рқ‘Ҳ вҠҶ рқ‘ү then рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҳ ) вҠҶ рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘ү ). Let рқ‘Ҳ вҠҶ рқҗҙ and рқ‘ү вҠҶ рқҗө, then ( ) рқ‘Ҳ вҠҶ рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҳ )) and рқ‘“ рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘ү ) вҠҶ рқ‘ү .

We also note that if рқ‘Ҳ is a subset of рқҗө then

рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқҗө в§ө рқ‘Ҳ ) = рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҳ рқ‘җ ) = (рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҳ ))рқ‘җ = рқҗҙ в§ө рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҳ ).

12 2.3. Exercises

2.3 Exercises

Exercise 2.1 Does рқ‘‘(рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) = (рқ‘Ҙ вҲ’ рқ‘Ұ)2 define a metric on рқ‘Ӣ = в„қ?

Exercise 2.2 Show that в„қ2 equipped with the taxi cab metric is a metric space.

Exercise 2.3 Let (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘‘) be a metric space. Show that the map рқ‘‘ вҖІ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ в„қ given by

рқ‘‘(рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) рқ‘‘ вҖІ (рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) = 1 + рқ‘‘(рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) is also a metric on рқ‘Ӣ.

Exercise 2.4 Draw a picture of the open ball рқ–Ў((0, 0); 1) in the metric space (в„қ2 , рқ‘‘) with

(a) рқ‘‘(рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) = рқ‘‘1 (рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) = |рқ‘Ҙ1 вҲ’ рқ‘Ұ1 | + |рқ‘Ҙ2 вҲ’ рқ‘Ұ2 |; вҲҡ (b) рқ‘‘(рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) = рқ‘‘2 (рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) = (рқ‘Ҙ1 вҲ’ рқ‘Ұ1 )2 + (рқ‘Ҙ2 вҲ’ рқ‘Ұ2 )2 ;

(c) рқ‘‘(рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) = рқ‘‘вҲһ (рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) = max{|рқ‘Ҙ1 вҲ’ рқ‘Ұ1 |, |рқ‘Ҙ2 вҲ’ рқ‘Ұ2 |}.

Exercise 2.5 Show that рқ‘‘1 , рқ‘‘2 and рқ‘‘вҲһ (as defined in Exercise 2.4) are equivalent on рқ‘Ӣ = в„қ2 .

Exercise 2.6 Show that in a discrete metric space (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘‘), cf. Example 2.3, every subset is both open and closed in рқ‘Ӣ.

Exercise 2.7 Show that for equivalent metrics рқ‘‘ and рқ‘‘ вҖІ on the set рқ‘Ӣ, the open sets are the same.

Exercise 2.8 Let (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘‘рқ‘Ӣ ) and (рқ‘Ң , рқ‘‘рқ‘Ң ) be metric spaces, and let рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң be a map. Show that рқ‘“ is continuous if and only if for every subset рқҗө вҠҶ рқ‘Ң closed in рқ‘Ң , the preimage рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқҗө) is closed in рқ‘Ӣ.

3. Topological spaces

3.1 Definition and examples Topological spaces are spaces constructed to support continuous maps. The definition is as follows.
nodes.formalStatement: Let $(X,d_X)$ and $(Y,d_Y)$ be metric spaces. A map $f:X\to Y$ is continuous if and only if for every subset $B\subseteq Y$ that is open in $Y$, the preimage $f^{-1}(B)=\{x\in X\mid f(x)\in B\}$ is open in $X$.
nodes.explanation: This theorem identifies continuity with preservation of open sets under preimage. It is the bridge from metric continuity to the topological definition of continuity.

nodes.title: Topological spaces
nodes.originalText:
A topological space is a set рқ‘Ӣ together with a collection оүҖ of subsets of рқ‘Ӣ that are called open in рқ‘Ӣ, such that the following properties hold.

T1 The subsets вҲ… and рқ‘Ӣ are in оүҖ .

T2 The union of the elements of any subcollection of оүҖ is in оүҖ .

T3 The intersection of the elements of any finite subcollection of оүҖ is in оүҖ .

A topological space is strictly speaking an ordered pair (рқ‘Ӣ, оүҖ ). We refer to оүҖ as the topology on рқ‘Ӣ. We will often omit specific mention of оүҖ if no confusion will arise.

The following theorem states that every metric space (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘‘) is a topological space with the metric topology оүҖрқ‘‘ on рқ‘Ӣ.
nodes.formalStatement: A topological space is a pair $(X,\mathcal{T})$ where $X$ is a set and $\mathcal{T}$ is a collection of subsets of $X$ such that $\emptyset,X\in\mathcal{T}$, arbitrary unions of elements of $\mathcal{T}$ lie in $\mathcal{T}$, and finite intersections of elements of $\mathcal{T}$ lie in $\mathcal{T}$. The elements of $\mathcal{T}$ are called open sets.
nodes.explanation: A topology abstracts the open-set behavior seen in metric spaces. The three axioms make open sets stable under the union and finite-intersection operations used throughout the subject.

nodes.title: Metric spaces are topological spaces
nodes.originalText:
Let (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘‘) be a metric space. Let оүҖрқ‘‘ be the collection of subsets рқ‘Ҳ вҠҶ рқ‘Ӣ with the property that рқ‘Ҳ вҲҲ оүҖрқ‘‘ if and only if for each рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ҳ there is an рқ‘ҹ > 0 such that рқ–Ў(рқ‘Ҙ; рқ‘ҹ) вҠҶ рқ‘Ҳ . Then оүҖрқ‘‘ defines a topology on рқ‘Ӣ.

Proof. Clearly, вҲ… вҲҲ оүҖрқ‘‘ . To show that рқ‘Ӣ вҲҲ оүҖрқ‘‘ , note that for any рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ, рқ–Ў(рқ‘Ҙ; 1) вҠҶ рқ‘Ӣ. Hence, рқ‘Ӣ вҲҲ оүҖрқ‘‘ . Thus T1 is satisfied. вӢғ Let {рқ‘ҲрқңҶ }рқңҶвҲҲОӣ be any subcollection of оүҖрқ‘‘ . We need to prove that рқ‘ү = рқңҶвҲҲОӣ рқ‘ҲрқңҶ вҲҲ оүҖрқ‘‘ . Let рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘ү . вӢғ From рқ‘ү = рқңҶвҲҲОӣ рқ‘ҲрқңҶ there is рқңҶ вҲҲ Оӣ such that рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘ҲрқңҶ . By the property of рқ‘ҲрқңҶ satisfied by the рқ‘ҲрқңҶ in оүҖрқ‘‘ there is an рқ‘ҹ > 0 such that рқ–Ў(рқ‘Ҙ; рқ‘ҹ) вҠҶ рқ‘ҲрқңҶ . Hence, рқ–Ў(рқ‘Ҙ; рқ‘ҹ) вҠҶ рқ‘ү . Thus рқ‘ү вҲҲ оүҖрқ‘‘ , and T2 is satisfied. We prove that the intersection of the elements of any finite subcollection of оүҖрқ‘‘ is also an element of оүҖрқ‘‘ . Let рқ‘Ҳ1 , рқ‘Ҳ2 , вҖҰ , рқ‘Ҳрқ‘ӣ вҲҲ оүҖрқ‘‘ . We need to prove that рқ‘Ҳ1 вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ2 вҲ© вӢҜ вҲ© рқ‘Ҳрқ‘ӣ вҲҲ оүҖрқ‘‘ . Let рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ҳ1 вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ2 вҲ© вӢҜ вҲ© рқ‘Ҳрқ‘ӣ . Since рқ‘Ҳ1 вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ2 вҲ© вӢҜ вҲ© рқ‘Ҳрқ‘ӣ вҠҶ рқ‘Ҳрқ‘– , we have that рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ҳрқ‘– for рқ‘– = 1, 2, вҖҰ , рқ‘ӣ. By the defining property of оүҖрқ‘‘ there is an рқ‘ҹрқ‘– > 0 such that рқ–Ў(рқ‘Ҙ; рқ‘ҹрқ‘– ) вҠҶ рқ‘Ҳрқ‘– for рқ‘– = 1, 2, вҖҰ , рқ‘ӣ. Let рқ‘ҹ = min{рқ‘ҹ1 , рқ‘ҹ2 , вҖҰ , рқ‘ҹрқ‘ӣ }. Then рқ–Ў(рқ‘Ҙ; рқ‘ҹ) вҠҶ рқ–Ў(рқ‘Ҙ; рқ‘ҹрқ‘– ) вҠҶ рқ‘Ҳрқ‘– for рқ‘– = 1, 2, вҖҰ , рқ‘ӣ. Thus рқ–Ў(рқ‘Ҙ; рқ‘ҹ) вҠҶ рқ‘Ҳ1 вҲ©рқ‘Ҳ2 вҲ©вӢҜвҲ©рқ‘Ҳрқ‘ӣ , and so рқ‘Ҳ1 вҲ©рқ‘Ҳ2 вҲ©вӢҜвҲ©рқ‘Ҳрқ‘ӣ вҲҲ оүҖрқ‘‘ . Hence, T3 is satisfied.

The following theorem relates the metric topologies for two equivalent metrics.
nodes.formalStatement: Let $(X,d)$ be a metric space. Let $\mathcal{T}_d$ be the collection of subsets $U\subseteq X$ such that for each $x\in U$ there exists $r>0$ with $B(x;r)\subseteq U$. Then $\mathcal{T}_d$ is a topology on $X$.
nodes.explanation: Every metric determines a topology by declaring the metric-open subsets to be open. The proof checks the topology axioms using open balls and the metric definition of open sets.

nodes.title: Theorem 3.3
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be any set, and let рқ‘‘1 and рқ‘‘2 be two equivalent metrics on рқ‘Ӣ. Then оүҖрқ‘‘1 = оүҖрқ‘‘2 .

14 3.1. Definition and examples

This follows from Exercise 2.7.

nodes.title: Discrete topology
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be any set. The collection оүҖ of all subsets of рқ‘Ӣ, i.e. the power set оҲј(рқ‘Ӣ) of рқ‘Ӣ, is a topology on рқ‘Ӣ. We refer to this topology as the discrete topology. A set рқ‘Ӣ equipped with the discrete topology is referred to as a discrete topological space.

The discrete topology is the unique topology where the singletons are open. We can think of a discrete topological space as a space of separate, isolated points, with no close interaction between different points. For any set рқ‘Ӣ, the discrete topology is the largest topology we may equip рқ‘Ӣ with. The smallest topology is called the indiscrete topology.
nodes.formalStatement: For any set $X$, the power set $\mathcal{P}(X)$ is a topology on $X$. This topology is called the discrete topology.
nodes.explanation: The discrete topology makes every subset open. It is the largest topology on a fixed set and models spaces whose points are completely separated.

nodes.title: Indiscrete topology
nodes.originalText: Let рқ‘Ӣ be any set. The collection оүҖ consisting of вҲ… and рқ‘Ӣ is a topology on рқ‘Ӣ, referred to as the indiscrete topology on рқ‘Ӣ. A set рқ‘Ӣ equipped with the indiscrete topology is referred to as an indiscrete topological space.
nodes.formalStatement: For any set $X$, the collection $\{\emptyset,X\}$ is a topology on $X$. This topology is called the indiscrete topology.
nodes.explanation: The indiscrete topology has only the two open sets required by the topology axioms. It is the smallest topology on a fixed set.

nodes.title: Example 3.6
Comparable topologies
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be any set and suppose that оүҖ1 and оүҖ2 are two topologies on рқ‘Ӣ. If оүҖ1 вҠҶ оүҖ2 , we say that оүҖ1 is coarser than оүҖ2 and that оүҖ2 is finer than оүҖ1 . We say that оүҖ1 and оүҖ2 are comparable if either оүҖ1 вҠҶ оүҖ2 or оүҖ2 вҠҶ оүҖ1 .

Clearly, for any set рқ‘Ӣ, the discrete topology оүҖdisc contains the indiscrete topology оүҖind : оүҖdisc вҠҮ оүҖind . Hence, the discrete topology is finer than the indiscrete topology and the indiscrete topology is coarser than the discrete topology.

nodes.title: Cofinite topology
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be any set. The collection оүҖ of subsets of рқ‘Ӣ consisting of subsets рқ‘Ҳ вҠҶ рқ‘Ӣ such that рқ‘Ҳ рқ‘җ = рқ‘Ӣ в§ө рқ‘Ҳ is either finite or all of рқ‘Ӣ is a topology on рқ‘Ӣ. We refer to this topology as the cofinite topology on рқ‘Ӣ.

Chapter 3. Topological spaces 15

If рқ‘Ӣ is a finite set, the cofinite topology is equal to the discrete topology. If рқ‘Ӣ is an infinite set, the cofinite topology is strictly coarser than the discrete topology, in the sense that the cofinite topology is properly contained in the discrete topology. We end this section with a theorem that we can use to prove that some set is open. To state the theorem we need the following definition.

nodes.title: Neighborhoods
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space, рқ‘Ҳ a subset of рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ. We say that рқ‘Ҳ is a neighborhood of рқ‘Ҙ if рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ҳ and рқ‘Ҳ is open in рқ‘Ӣ.

A neighborhood in the sense of the previous definition is sometimes referred to as an open neigh- borhood, cf. Definition 2.11.
nodes.formalStatement: Let $X$ be a topological space, let $U\subseteq X$, and let $x\in X$. The set $U$ is a neighborhood of $x$ if $x\in U$ and $U$ is open in $X$.
nodes.explanation: In a topological space, a neighborhood is simply an open set containing the point. This is the topological analogue of metric neighborhoods.

nodes.title: Open Sets via Neighborhoods
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space. A subset рқ‘Ҳ of рқ‘Ӣ is open in рқ‘Ӣ if and only if for every рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ҳ there is a neighborhood рқ‘Ҳрқ‘Ҙ of рқ‘Ҙ such that рқ‘Ҳрқ‘Ҙ вҠҶ рқ‘Ҳ .

Proof. Assume that рқ‘Ҳ is open in рқ‘Ӣ. Then for every рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ҳ , рқ‘Ҳ is a neighborhood of рқ‘Ҙ that is contained in рқ‘Ҳ . We prove the other implication. Assume that for every рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ҳ there is a рқ‘Ҳрқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ оүҖ such that рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ҳрқ‘Ҙ вҠҶ рқ‘Ҳ , i.e., that рқ‘Ҳрқ‘Ҙ is a neighborhood of рқ‘Ҙ such that рқ‘Ҳрқ‘Ҙ вҠҶ рқ‘Ҳ . To prove that рқ‘Ҳ вҲҲ оүҖ , we will вӢғ вӢғ prove that рқ‘Ҳ = рқ‘ҘвҲҲрқ‘Ҳ рқ‘Ҳрқ‘Ҙ . Assume that рқ‘ҘвҖІ вҲҲ рқ‘Ҳрқ‘ҘвҖІ . Then рқ‘ҘвҖІ вҲҲ рқ‘Ҳрқ‘ҘвҖІ вҠҶ рқ‘ҘвҲҲрқ‘Ҳ рқ‘Ҳрқ‘Ҙ . Furthermore, any вӢғ point in рқ‘ҘвҲҲрқ‘Ҳ рқ‘Ҳрқ‘Ҙ is in рқ‘Ҳрқ‘Ҙ for some рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ҳ so by assumption, рқ‘Ҳрқ‘Ҙ вҠҶ рқ‘Ҳ and рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ҳрқ‘Ҙ вҠҶ рқ‘Ҳ . Hence, вӢғ рқ‘Ҳ = рқ‘ҘвҲҲрқ‘Ҳ рқ‘Ҳрқ‘Ҙ . As рқ‘Ҳ is the union of open sets it must be an open set as well by T2.

3.2 Continuous maps We know from Theorem 2.13 that a map between metric spaces is continuous if and only if the preimage of an open set is open. This motivates the following definition.
nodes.formalStatement: Let $X$ be a topological space. A subset $U\subseteq X$ is open in $X$ if and only if for every $x\in U$ there exists a neighborhood $U_x$ of $x$ such that $U_x\subseteq U$.
nodes.explanation: This theorem characterizes open sets pointwise by neighborhoods. The proof uses the fact that an arbitrary union of open neighborhoods is open.

nodes.title: Continuous maps between topological spaces
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ң be topological spaces. A map рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң is said to be continuous if preimages of open sets are open, i.e., if рқ‘ү is an open set in рқ‘Ң then the preimage рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘ү ) of рқ‘ү under рқ‘“ is open in рқ‘Ӣ.

Hence, all continuous maps between metric spaces (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘‘рқ‘Ӣ ) and (рқ‘Ң , рқ‘‘рқ‘Ң ) are also continuous maps between the corresponding topological spaces рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ң with the metric topologies оүҖрқ‘‘рқ‘Ӣ and оүҖрқ‘‘рқ‘Ң , re- spectively.
nodes.formalStatement: Let $X$ and $Y$ be topological spaces. A map $f:X\to Y$ is continuous if for every open set $V\subseteq Y$, the preimage $f^{-1}(V)$ is open in $X$.
nodes.explanation: Topological continuity is defined by the preimage of open sets. This definition is motivated by the metric preimage criterion and no longer requires distances.

nodes.title: Example 3.12
nodes.originalText: Let рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ң be topological spaces. Then all constant maps from рқ‘Ӣ to рқ‘Ң are continuous: the preimages are either empty or the entire space, and these are always open, cf. T1.

nodes.title: Example 3.13
nodes.originalText: Let рқ‘Ӣ be a discrete topological space and рқ‘Ң a topological space. Then all maps from рқ‘Ӣ to рқ‘Ң are continuous.

nodes.title: Example 3.14
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be any topological space and рқ‘Ң be an indiscrete topological space. Then all maps from рқ‘Ӣ to рқ‘Ң are continuous.

16 3.2. Continuous maps

nodes.title: Example 3.15
nodes.originalText:
Let в„қdisc be the discrete topological space consisting of the real numbers with the discrete topology, and let в„қ be the topological space consisting of the the real numbers with the usual (Euclidean) metric topology. Then the identity map id в„қdisc вҶҗвҶҗвҶҗвҶҗвҶ’ в„қ

is continuous by Example 3.13, while the identity map id в„қ вҶҗвҶҗвҶҗвҶҗвҶ’ в„қdisc

is not continuous: singletons are open in the discrete topology but not in the (Euclidean) metric topology.

The following theorem says that the composition of two continuous maps is a continuous map.

nodes.title: Composition of continuous maps
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘Ң and рқ‘Қ be topological spaces. If рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң and рқ‘” вҲ¶ рқ‘Ң вҶ’ рқ‘Қ are continuous maps, then the composite рқ‘”в—Ұрқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Қ is continuous.

Proof. Let рқ‘Ҡ вҠҶ рқ‘Қ be open in рқ‘Қ. We need to prove that (рқ‘”в—Ұрқ‘“ )вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҡ ) is open in рқ‘Ӣ. Since

(рқ‘”в—Ұрқ‘“ )вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҡ ) = {рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ вҲЈ рқ‘”(рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҙ)) вҲҲ рқ‘Ҡ } = {рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ вҲЈ рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҙ) вҲҲ рқ‘” вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҡ )} = {рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ вҲЈ рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘” вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҡ ))} = рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘” вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҡ ))

and that рқ‘” вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҡ ) is open in рқ‘Ң and рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘” вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҡ )) is open in рқ‘Ӣ (by continuity of рқ‘” and рқ‘“ ), it follows that (рқ‘”в—Ұрқ‘“ )вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҡ ) is open in рқ‘Ӣ. Hence, рқ‘”в—Ұрқ‘“ is continuous.

We can express continuity at a point for maps between topological spaces using neighborhoods. (See Theorem 2.12 for the case of metric spaces.)
nodes.formalStatement: Let $X$, $Y$, and $Z$ be topological spaces. If $f:X\to Y$ and $g:Y\to Z$ are continuous maps, then the composite $g\circ f:X\to Z$ is continuous.
nodes.explanation: Continuous maps are closed under composition. The proof uses the identity $(g\circ f)^{-1}(W)=f^{-1}(g^{-1}(W))$ for an open set $W\subseteq Z$.

nodes.title: Continuity at a point
nodes.originalText: Let рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ң be topological spaces, and let рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ. A map рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң is continuous at рқ‘Ҙ if for all neighborhoods рқ‘ү of рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҙ) there is a neighborhood рқ‘Ҳ of рқ‘Ҙ such that рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҳ ) вҠҶ рқ‘ү .

nodes.title: Theorem 3.18
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ң be topological spaces. A map рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң is continuous if and only if it is continuous at each рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ.

Proof. Assume that рқ‘“ is continuous, and let рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘ү be a neighborhood of рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҙ). Then the set рқ‘Ҳ = рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘ү ) is a neighborhood of рқ‘Ҙ such that рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҳ ) вҠҶ рқ‘ү . Assume that рқ‘“ is continuous at each рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ. Let рқ‘ү вҠҶ рқ‘Ң be open in рқ‘Ң . Choose рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘ү ). Since рқ‘“ is continuous at рқ‘Ҙ there is neighborhood рқ‘Ҳрқ‘Ҙ of рқ‘Ҙ such that рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҳрқ‘Ҙ ) вҠҶ рқ‘ү . Hence, рқ‘Ҳрқ‘Ҙ вҠҶ рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘ү ). It follows that рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘ү ) can be written as the union of the open sets рқ‘Ҳрқ‘Ҙ , and hence, it is open in рқ‘Ӣ. Thus рқ‘“ is continuous.

Chapter 3. Topological spaces 17

3.3 Homeomorphisms We now introduce the notion of topological equivalence, also known as homeomorphism.

nodes.title: Homeomorphisms
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ң be topological spaces. A bijective map рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң with the property that both рқ‘“ and рқ‘“ вҲ’1 вҲ¶ рқ‘Ң вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ are continuous, is called a homeo- morphism. If there exists a homeomorphism рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң , we say that рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ң are homeomor- phic and write рқ‘Ӣ вү… рқ‘Ң .

A homeomorphism рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң gives a one-to-one correspondence between open sets in рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ң . As a result, any property of a topological space that can be expressed in terms of its elements and its open subsets is preserved by homeomorphisms. Such a property is called a topological property.

nodes.title: Example 3.20
nodes.originalText:
Let в„қ be the topological space of the real numbers with the (Euclidean) metric topology. The map рқ‘“ вҲ¶ в„қ вҶ’ в„қ given by

рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҙ) = 2рқ‘Ҙ вҲ’ 1

is a homeomorphism. Let рқ‘” вҲ¶ в„қ вҶ’ в„қ be given by

рқ‘”(рқ‘Ұ) = (рқ‘Ұ + 1) then, clearly, рқ‘”(рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҙ)) = рқ‘Ҙ and рқ‘“ (рқ‘”(рқ‘Ұ)) = рқ‘Ұ for all real numbers рқ‘Ҙ and рқ‘Ұ. Thus рқ‘“ is a bijection and рқ‘“ вҲ’1 = рқ‘”. From calculus we know that рқ‘“ and рқ‘” are continuous. Hence, рқ‘“ is a homeomorphism.

nodes.title: Example 3.21
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ = {рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ}, and let оүҖ1 = {вҲ…, {рқ‘Һ}, рқ‘Ӣ} and оүҖ2 = {вҲ…, {рқ‘Ҹ}, рқ‘Ӣ} be two topolo- gies on рқ‘Ӣ. The map рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ given by рқ‘“ (рқ‘Һ) = рқ‘Ҹ and рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҹ) = рқ‘Һ is clearly a continuous bijection (with the domain given оүҖ1 as topology, and the codomain given оүҖ2 as topology). Also, рқ‘“ is its own inverse: рқ‘“ = рқ‘“ вҲ’1 . Hence, рқ‘“ is a homeomorphism and (рқ‘Ӣ, оүҖ1 ) вү… (рқ‘Ӣ, оүҖ2 ).

Homeomorphisms are continuous bijections, but the converse is not true.

nodes.title: Example 3.22
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ = {рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ}. The identity map id вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ where the domain is given the discrete topology and the codomain is given the indiscrete topology is a continuous bijection but not a homeomorphism: the inverse map is not continuous.

The following theorem says that being homeomorphic is an equivalence relation on any set of topological spaces.

nodes.title: Theorem 3.23
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘Ң and рқ‘Қ be topological spaces.

Reflexivity The identity map id вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ (where the domain and the codomain are equipped with the same topology), given by id(рқ‘Ҙ) = рқ‘Ҙ for рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ, is a homeomorphism.

Symmetry If рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң is a homeomorphism, then рқ‘“ вҲ’1 вҲ¶ рқ‘Ң вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ is also a homeomor- phism.

Transitivity If рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң and рқ‘” вҲ¶ рқ‘Ң вҶ’ рқ‘Қ are homeomorphisms, then рқ‘”в—Ұрқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Қ is also

18 3.4. Closed sets

a homeomorphism.

Proof. The identity map id вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ (where the domain and the codomain are equipped with the same topology) is clearly continuous and bijective. As the identity map is its own inverse, then it is also a homeomorphism. Hence, рқ‘Ӣ вү… рқ‘Ӣ and so вү… satisfies the reflexivity condition for an equivalence relation. If рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң is a homeomorphism, then рқ‘“ вҲ’1 вҲ¶ рқ‘Ң вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ is also a homeomorphism: рқ‘“ вҲ’1 is a continuous bijection with continuous inverse (рқ‘“ вҲ’1 )вҲ’1 = рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң . Hence, рқ‘Ӣ вү… рқ‘Ң if and only if рқ‘Ң вү… рқ‘Ӣ. Thus вү… satisfies the symmetry condition for an equivalence relation.

nodes.title: Theorem 3.16
nodes.originalText:
tells us that the composition of two homeomorphisms рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң and рқ‘” вҲ¶ рқ‘Ң вҶ’ рқ‘Қ is continuous. The composition of two bijective maps is always bijective. Hence, рқ‘”в—Ұрқ‘“ is a continuous bijection. We need to prove that its inverse, (рқ‘”в—Ұрқ‘“ )вҲ’1 , is continuous. Since

(рқ‘”в—Ұрқ‘“ )вҲ’1 = рқ‘“ вҲ’1 в—Ұрқ‘” вҲ’1

is a composition of continuous maps, then by Theorem 3.16 so is (рқ‘”в—Ұрқ‘“ )вҲ’1 . Thus рқ‘”в—Ұрқ‘“ is a homeomor- phism. Hence, if рқ‘Ӣ вү… рқ‘Ң and рқ‘Ң вү… рқ‘Қ, then рқ‘Ӣ вү… рқ‘Қ. Thus вү… satisfies the transitivity condition for an equivalence relation.

3.4 Closed sets Recall that in a topological space рқ‘Ӣ, a subset рқҗҙ of рқ‘Ӣ is an open subset if and only if рқҗҙ is an element of the topology of рқ‘Ӣ, i.e., рқҗҙ вҲҲ оүҖ .

nodes.title: Closed subsets
nodes.originalText:
A subset рқҗҫ of a topological space рқ‘Ӣ is closed in рқ‘Ӣ if and only if the complement рқҗҫ рқ‘җ = рқ‘Ӣ в§ө рқҗҫ is open in рқ‘Ӣ.

This is completely analogous to how we defined closed subsets in metric spaces, cf. Definition 2.9.

nodes.title: Example 3.25
nodes.originalText: Let рқ‘Ӣ be a discrete topological space. Since every subset of рқ‘Ӣ is open in рқ‘Ӣ, it follows that every subset of рқ‘Ӣ is also closed in рқ‘Ӣ.

nodes.title: Example 3.26
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be an indiscrete topological space. The only subsets of рқ‘Ӣ that are closed in рқ‘Ӣ are вҲ… and рқ‘Ӣ (which are also the only subsets that are open in рқ‘Ӣ).

Recall that in a discrete topological space, all the singletons are open sets. This is usually not the case.

nodes.title: Example 3.27
nodes.originalText:
Let в„қ be the topological space of the real numbers with the (Euclidean) metric topology. Then every subset [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ] = {рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ в„қ вҲЈ рқ‘Һ в©Ҫ рқ‘Ҙ в©Ҫ рқ‘Ҹ} вҠҶ в„қ is closed in в„қ: the complement [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ]рқ‘җ = в„қ в§ө [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ] = (вҲ’вҲһ, рқ‘Һ) вҲӘ (рқ‘Ҹ, вҲһ) is a union of open sets in в„қ, and hence, is open in в„қ. Furthermore, all the singletons are closed: the complement {рқ‘Һ}рқ‘җ = в„қ в§ө {рқ‘Һ} = (вҲ’вҲһ, рқ‘Һ) вҲӘ (рқ‘Һ, вҲһ) is a union of open sets in в„қ, and hence, is open in в„қ.

By passing to complements we get the following theorem.

Chapter 3. Topological spaces 19

nodes.title: Theorem 3.28
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space.

(1) Both вҲ… and рқ‘Ӣ are closed (as subsets) in рқ‘Ӣ.

(2) The intersection of any subcollection of closed sets in рқ‘Ӣ is closed in рқ‘Ӣ.

(3) The union of any finite subcollection of closed sets in рқ‘Ӣ is closed in рқ‘Ӣ.

It follows that we could have defined a topological space рқ‘Ӣ by specifying a collection of subsets of рқ‘Ӣ satisfying the three statements in Theorem 3.28 where we would say that a subset of рқ‘Ӣ is open in рқ‘Ӣ if its complement is closed in рқ‘Ӣ. We end this section with a theorem describing the connection between continuous maps and closed sets. We will need the following definition.

nodes.title: Closure
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space, and let рқҗҙ be a subset of рқ‘Ӣ. The closure of рқҗҙ, written рқҗҙ, is the intersection of all subsets of рқ‘Ӣ that contain рқҗҙ and which are closed in рқ‘Ӣ.

From the definition it follows that рқҗҙ is the smallest subset of рқ‘Ӣ that contains рқҗҙ and which is closed in рқ‘Ӣ. Furthermore, if рқҗҙ is closed in рқ‘Ӣ, then рқҗҙ = рқҗҙ. There is an analogous definition for open sets where we take union instead of intersection. We can define the interior of рқҗҙ, written Int(рқҗҙ), to be the union of all subsets of рқ‘Ӣ that are contained in рқҗҙ and which are open in рқ‘Ӣ. It follows that Int(рқҗҙ) is the largest subset of рқ‘Ӣ that is contained in рқҗҙ and which is open in рқ‘Ӣ. Furthermore, Int(рқҗҙ) вҠҶ рқҗҙ вҠҶ рқҗҙ.

nodes.title: Example 3.30
nodes.originalText: Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space consisting of the set {рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ, рқ‘җ} and the topology оүҖ = {вҲ…, {рқ‘Һ}, {рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ}, рқ‘Ӣ}. Then the closed subsets in рқ‘Ӣ are вҲ…, {рқ‘Ҹ, рқ‘җ}, {рқ‘җ} and рқ‘Ӣ. Thus the intersection of all of the closed subsets that contain {рқ‘Ҹ} is simply {рқ‘Ҹ, рқ‘җ}вҲ©рқ‘Ӣ = {рқ‘Ҹ, рқ‘җ}, and hence, {рқ‘Ҹ} = {рқ‘Ҹ, рқ‘җ}.

nodes.title: Example 3.31
nodes.originalText:
Let в„қ be the topological space of the real numbers with the (Euclidean) metric topology. Assume that рқ‘Һ < рқ‘Ҹ are real numbers. Then (рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ] = [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ] and Int((рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ]) = (рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ).

Let (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘‘) be a metric space. If we consider рқ‘Ӣ as a topological space with the metric topology оүҖрқ‘‘ , the closure рқ–Ў(рқ‘Ҙ; рқ‘ҹ) of an open ball рқ–Ў(рқ‘Ҙ; рқ‘ҹ) about рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ is, in general, not the same as the closed ball рқ–Ў(рқ‘Ҙ; рқ‘ҹ). If рқ‘‘ is the discrete metric and рқ‘Ӣ has at least two elements, then рқ–Ў(рқ‘Ҙ; 1) = {рқ‘Ҙ} while рқ–Ў(рқ‘Ҙ; 1) = рқ‘Ӣ. It is always the case that рқ–Ў(рқ‘Ҙ; рқ‘ҹ) вҠҶ рқ–Ў(рқ‘Ҙ; рқ‘ҹ).

nodes.title: Dense
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space, and let рқҗҙ be a subset of рқ‘Ӣ. We say that рқҗҙ is dense in рқ‘Ӣ if рқҗҙ = рқ‘Ӣ.

From the definition it follows that рқҗҙ is dense in рқ‘Ӣ if and only if рқҗҙ вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ вү  вҲ… for every nonempty subset рқ‘Ҳ of рқ‘Ӣ which is open in рқ‘Ӣ.

nodes.title: Example 3.33
nodes.originalText: Let в„қ be the topological space of the real numbers with the (Euclidean) metric topology. Then the subset в„ҡ of rational numbers is dense in в„қ: в„ҡ = в„қ.

nodes.title: Example 3.34
nodes.originalText:
For any topological space рқ‘Ӣ, the subset рқ‘Ӣ is dense in рқ‘Ӣ. If рқ‘Ӣ is a discrete topological space, then the subset рқ‘Ӣ is the only dense subset in рқ‘Ӣ.

20 3.5. Exercises

nodes.title: Theorem 3.35
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң be a map between topological spaces. Then the following are equivalent:

(1) рқ‘“ is continuous;

(2) for every subset рқҗҙ of рқ‘Ӣ, we have рқ‘“ (рқҗҙ) вҠҶ рқ‘“ (рқҗҙ);

(3) for every closed subset рқҗө of рқ‘Ң , the preimage рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқҗө) of рқҗө under рқ‘“ is closed in рқ‘Ӣ.

Proof. By passing to complements, it follows readily that (1) and (3) are equivalent. We will prove that (2) is equivalent to (3). Assume (2). Let рқҗө be a subset of рқ‘Ң that is closed in рқ‘Ң , and let рқҗҙ = рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқҗө). We must show that рқҗҙ is closed. We have рқ‘“ (рқҗҙ) вҠҶ рқҗө. If рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗҙ, then рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҙ) вҲҲ рқ‘“ (рқҗҙ) вҠҶ рқ‘“ (рқҗҙ) вҠҶ рқҗө. Hence, рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқҗө) = рқҗҙ. In other words, рқҗҙ вҠҶ рқҗҙ. Thus рқҗҙ = рқҗҙ, and hence, рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқҗө) is closed in рқ‘Ӣ. Now assume (3). Let рқҗҙ be a subset of рқ‘Ӣ. We must show that рқ‘“ (рқҗҙ) вҠҶ рқ‘“ (рқҗҙ). Since рқ‘“ (рқҗҙ) is closed in рқ‘Ң , it follows by assumption that рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘“ (рқҗҙ)) is closed in рқ‘Ӣ. Furthermore, рқҗҙ вҠҶ рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘“ (рқҗҙ)) вҠҶ рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘“ (рқҗҙ)). Since рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘“ (рқҗҙ)) is closed in рқ‘Ӣ, it follows that рқҗҙ вҠҶ рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘“ (рқҗҙ)). Hence, рқ‘“ (рқҗҙ) вҠҶ рқ‘“ (рқҗҙ).

3.5 Exercises

Exercise 3.1 Let рқ‘Ӣ = {рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ, рқ‘җ, рқ‘‘}. Show that оүҖ = {вҲ…, {рқ‘Һ}, {рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ}, {рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ, рқ‘җ}, {рқ‘Ҹ, рқ‘җ, рқ‘‘}, рқ‘Ӣ} is not a topology on рқ‘Ӣ. Find a topology оүҖ вҖІ (different from the discrete topology) on рқ‘Ӣ such that оүҖ вҠҶ оүҖ вҖІ.

Exercise 3.2 Let рқ‘Ӣ be a non-empty set, and let рқ‘Ҙ0 be an element of рқ‘Ӣ. Show that

оүҖ = {рқ‘Ҳ вҠҶ рқ‘Ӣ вҲЈ рқ‘Ҙ0 вҲү рқ‘Ҳ or рқ‘Ӣ в§ө рқ‘Ҳ is finite}

is a topology on рқ‘Ӣ.

Exercise 3.3 Let рқ‘Ӣ be a set, and let рқҗҙ be a subset of рқ‘Ӣ. Define the coarsest topology on рқ‘Ӣ such that рқҗҙ is open in рқ‘Ӣ.

Exercise 3.4 Show that the discrete topology оүҖdisc is finer than the cofinite topology оүҖcof on any set рқ‘Ӣ.

Exercise 3.5 Let рқ‘Ӣ = {рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ, рқ‘җ, рқ‘‘}. Find two topologies оүҖ1 and оүҖ2 with оүҖ1 вү  оүҖ2 such that a bijection рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ is a homeomorphism (where the domain is given оүҖ1 as topology and the codomain is given оүҖ2 as topology).

Exercise 3.6 Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space, and let рқҗҙ and рқҗө be subsets of рқ‘Ӣ.

(a) Assume that рқҗҙ вҠҶ рқҗө. Show that рқҗҙ вҠҶ рқҗө.

(b) Show that рқҗҙ вҲӘ рқҗө = рқҗҙ вҲӘ рқҗө.

Chapter 3. Topological spaces 21

We say that a set рқҗҙ intersects or meets a set рқҗө if рқҗҙ вҲ© рқҗө вү  вҲ….

Exercise 3.7 Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space, and let рқҗҙ be a subset of рқ‘Ӣ. Show that рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗҙ if and only if every neighborhood of рқ‘Ҙ intersects рқҗҙ.

Exercise 3.8 Let рқ‘Ӣ = {рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ, рқ‘җ, рқ‘‘, рқ‘’}, and let

оүҖ = {вҲ…, {рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ}, {рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ, рқ‘җ}, {рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ, рқ‘‘, рқ‘’}, {рқ‘Ҹ}, {рқ‘Ҹ, рқ‘җ}, {рқ‘Ҹ, рқ‘‘, рқ‘’}, {рқ‘Ҹ, рқ‘җ, рқ‘‘, рқ‘’}, {рқ‘‘, рқ‘’}, рқ‘Ӣ}

be a topology on рқ‘Ӣ. Is the subset {рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ} dense in рқ‘Ӣ?

4. Generating topologies

4.1 Generating topologies from subsets The following theorem tells us how we may extract a third topology from two other topologies on the same set.

nodes.title: The intersection of two topologies is a topology
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a set, and let оүҖ1 and оүҖ2 be two topologies on рқ‘Ӣ. Then оүҖ1 вҲ© оүҖ2 is also a topology on рқ‘Ӣ.

Proof. Clearly, вҲ… and рқ‘Ӣ are in оүҖ1 вҲ© оүҖ2 , so T1 is satisfied. Let {рқ‘ҲрқңҶ }рқңҶвҲҲОӣ be a collection of sets such that рқ‘ҲрқңҶ вҲҲ оүҖ1 вҲ© оүҖ2 for each рқңҶ вҲҲ Оӣ where Оӣ is some вӢғ index set. Then, for рқ‘– = 1, 2, рқ‘ҲрқңҶ вҲҲ оүҖрқ‘– for each рқңҶ вҲҲ Оӣ. Thus рқңҶвҲҲОӣ рқ‘ҲрқңҶ вҲҲ оүҖрқ‘– for рқ‘– = 1, 2. Hence, вӢғ рқңҶвҲҲОӣ рқ‘ҲрқңҶ вҲҲ оүҖ1 вҲ© оүҖ2 , and so, T2 is satisfied. Let {рқ‘Ҳ1 , рқ‘Ҳ2 , вҖҰ , рқ‘Ҳрқ‘ӣ } be a finite subcollection of оүҖ . We need to prove that T3 is satisfied, i.e., рқ‘Ҳ1 вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ2 вҲ© вӢҜ вҲ© рқ‘Ҳрқ‘ӣ вҲҲ оүҖ . Note that if рқ‘Ҳ1 , рқ‘Ҳ2 , вҖҰ , рқ‘Ҳрқ‘ӣ вҲҲ оүҖрқ‘– then рқ‘Ҳ1 вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ2 вҲ© вӢҜ вҲ© рқ‘Ҳрқ‘ӣ вҲҲ оүҖрқ‘– for рқ‘– = 1, 2. Hence, рқ‘Ҳ1 вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ2 вҲ© вӢҜ вҲ© рқ‘Ҳрқ‘ӣ вҲҲ оүҖ1 вҲ© оүҖ2 . Thus T3 is satisfied.

Theorem вӢӮ 4.1 may be extended to hold for a family of topologies: if {оүҖрқңҶ }рқңҶвҲҲОӣ is a family of topologies on рқ‘Ӣ, then рқңҶвҲҲОӣ оүҖрқңҶ is also a topology on рқ‘Ӣ. If we follow the convention that for subsets рқ‘Ҷ of a fixed (large) set рқ‘Ҳ , вӢӮ рқ‘Ҷ = рқ‘Ҳ, рқ‘ҶвҲҲвҲ…

then the extended version of Theorem 4.1 may also include an empty family {оүҖрқңҶ }рқңҶвҲҲвҲ… of topologies with вӢӮ оүҖрқңҶ = оҲј(рқ‘Ӣ), рқңҶвҲҲвҲ…

i.e., the discrete topology on рқ‘Ӣ (with our fixed (large) set рқ‘Ҳ being equal to оҲј(рқ‘Ӣ)). However, not all mathemati- cians follow this convention. Thus we will in general not define the intersection of an empty family.

The union of two topologies is not necessarily a topology.

nodes.title: Example 4.2
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ = {рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ, рқ‘җ}, and let оүҖ1 = {вҲ…, {рқ‘Һ}, {рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ}, рқ‘Ӣ} and оүҖ2 = {вҲ…, {рқ‘җ}, рқ‘Ӣ} be two topologies on рқ‘Ӣ. Then оүҖ1 вҲ© оүҖ2 = {вҲ…, рқ‘Ӣ} is the indiscrete topology on рқ‘Ӣ while

оүҖ1 вҲӘ оүҖ2 = {вҲ…, {рқ‘Һ}, {рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ}, {рқ‘җ}, рқ‘Ӣ}

is not a topology on рқ‘Ӣ: оүҖ1 вҲӘ оүҖ2 does not satisfy T2.

Chapter 4. Generating topologies 23

Recall from Section 3.1 that for any set рқ‘Ӣ the discrete topology оүҖdisc is the largest topology we may equip рқ‘Ӣ with, and the indiscrete topology оүҖind is the smallest topology we may equip рқ‘Ӣ with. For any topology оүҖ on рқ‘Ӣ we have оүҖind вҠҶ оүҖ вҠҶ оүҖdisc .

That is, we have partially ordered topologies on рқ‘Ӣ by inclusion. Let рқ‘Ӣ be a set. We often want to have a collection of subsets оҲҝ of рқ‘Ӣ to be the open subsets of a topology on рқ‘Ӣ.

nodes.title: Topology generated by a collection of subsets
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a set, and let оҲҝ be a collection of subsets of рқ‘Ӣ. The topology generated by оҲҝ is the topology вӢӮ вҹЁоҲҝвҹ© = оүҖ оүҖ topology оҲҝвҠҶоүҖ

on рқ‘Ӣ.

In other words, вҹЁоҲҝвҹ© contains оҲҝ and for any other topology оүҖ вҖІ containing оҲҝ, we have вҹЁоҲҝвҹ© вҠҶ оүҖ вҖІ . Thus вҹЁоҲҝвҹ© is unique.

nodes.title: Example 4.4
nodes.originalText: Let рқ‘Ӣ be a set, and let оҲҝ = вҲ…. Then вҹЁоҲҝвҹ© is the same as the indiscrete topology on рқ‘Ӣ, i.e., вҹЁоҲҝвҹ© = оүҖind = {вҲ…, рқ‘Ӣ}.

nodes.title: Example 4.5
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a set, and let оҲҝ be the collection of all the singletons of рқ‘Ӣ, i.e., оҲҝ = {{рқ‘Ҙ} вҲЈ рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ}. Then вҹЁоҲҝвҹ© is the same as the discrete topology on рқ‘Ӣ, i.e.,

вҹЁоҲҝвҹ© = оүҖdisc = оҲј(рқ‘Ӣ).

4.2 Basis for a topology It is often convenient to define a topology оүҖ on a set рқ‘Ӣ by only specifying a subcollection оҲ® of оүҖ satisfying certain properties. The open subsets of рқ‘Ӣ are then precisely the unions of subcollections of оҲ®. In this way, we say the basis determines, or generates, the topology.

nodes.title: Basis
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a set. A basis for a topology on рқ‘Ӣ is a collection оҲ® of subsets of рқ‘Ӣ such that

B1 for each рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ, there is a рқҗө вҲҲ оҲ® such that рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗө;

B2 if рқҗө1 , рқҗө2 вҲҲ оҲ® and рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗө1 вҲ© рқҗө2 , then there is a рқҗө3 вҲҲ оҲ® such that рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗө3 вҠҶ рқҗө1 вҲ© рқҗө2 .

The elements of оҲ® are sometimes referred to as basis elements.

nodes.title: Example 4.7
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a set, and let оҲ® be the collection of all the singletons of рқ‘Ӣ. Then оҲ® is a basis for the discrete topology on рқ‘Ӣ.

24 4.2. Basis for a topology

nodes.title: Example 4.8
nodes.originalText:
Let (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘‘) be a metric space. Then the collection of (open) рқң–-balls

оҲ® = {рқ–Ў(рқ‘Ҙ; рқң–) вҲЈ рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ, рқң– > 0}

is a basis for the metric topology оүҖрқ‘‘ , as defined in Theorem 3.2, on рқ‘Ӣ.

The following theorem describes a topology generated by a basis.

nodes.title: Theorem 4.9
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a set, and let оҲ® be basis for a topology on рқ‘Ӣ. The collection оүҖ generated by оҲ® of subsets рқ‘Ҳ of рқ‘Ӣ with the property that for each рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ҳ there is a basis element рқҗө вҲҲ оҲ® with рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗө вҠҶ рқ‘Ҳ is a topology on рқ‘Ӣ.

Proof. Clearly, вҲ… and рқ‘Ӣ are both in оүҖ . Hence, T1 is satisfied. вӢғ Let {рқ‘ҲрқңҶ }рқңҶвҲҲОӣ be a subcollection of оүҖ . Let рқ‘ү = рқңҶвҲҲОӣ рқ‘ҲрқңҶ . We need to prove that рқ‘ү вҲҲ оүҖ . Let рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘ү . Then there is a рқңҶ вҲҲ Оӣ such that рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘ҲрқңҶ . Since рқ‘ҲрқңҶ вҲҲ оүҖ , there is a basis element рқҗө вҲҲ оҲ® such that рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗө вҠҶ рқ‘ҲрқңҶ . As рқ‘ҲрқңҶ вҠҶ рқ‘ү , it follows that рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗө вҠҶ рқ‘ү . Hence, рқ‘ү вҲҲ оүҖ , and so, T2 is satisfied. We need to prove that T3 is satisfied, that is, for a finite subcollection {рқ‘Ҳ1 , рқ‘Ҳ2 , вҖҰ , рқ‘Ҳрқ‘ӣ } of оүҖ , we have рқ‘Ҳ1 вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ2 вҲ© вӢҜ вҲ© рқ‘Ҳрқ‘ӣ вҲҲ оүҖ . We will prove the case for рқ‘ӣ = 2. The general result then follows by an induction argument. Let рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ҳ1 вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ2 . Since рқ‘Ҳ1 вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ2 вҠҶ рқ‘Ҳрқ‘– we have рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ҳрқ‘– , and thus there is a basis element рқҗөрқ‘– вҲҲ оҲ® with рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗөрқ‘– вҠҶ рқ‘Ҳрқ‘– for рқ‘– = 1, 2. Hence, рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗө1 вҲ© рқҗө2 вҠҶ рқ‘Ҳ1 вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ2 . By B2 there is a basis element рқҗө3 вҲҲ оҲ® with рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗө3 вҠҶ рқҗө1 вҲ© рқҗө2 . Thus рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗө3 вҠҶ рқ‘Ҳ1 вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ2 , and so, рқ‘Ҳ1 вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ2 вҲҲ оүҖ .

The topology generated by a basis may also be described using the following theorem.

nodes.title: Theorem 4.10
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a set, and let оҲ® be a basis for a topology оүҖ on рқ‘Ӣ. Then оүҖ is equal to the collection of all unions of elements of оҲ®.

Proof. Let рқҗө вҲҲ оҲ® be any basis element. Then for each рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗө we obviously have рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗө and рқҗө вҠҶ рқҗө. Thus рқҗө вҲҲ оүҖ . It follows that any union of basis elements is a union of elements of оүҖ , and hence, is in оүҖ. Conversely, let рқ‘Ҳ вҲҲ оүҖ . For each рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ҳ there is a рқҗөрқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ оҲ® with рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗөрқ‘Ҙ and рқҗөрқ‘Ҙ вҠҶ рқ‘Ҳ . Then вӢғ рқ‘Ҳ = рқ‘ҘвҲҲрқ‘Ҳ рқҗөрқ‘Ҙ , and thus, рқ‘Ҳ is the union of elements of оҲ®.

We end this section with a theorem describing a criterion for whether one topology is finer than another when both topologies are described using bases.

nodes.title: Theorem 4.11
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a set, and let оҲ®1 and оҲ®2 be bases for topologies оүҖ1 and оүҖ2 , respec- tively, on рқ‘Ӣ. Then the following are equivalent:

(1) оүҖ2 is finer than оүҖ1 , i.e., оүҖ1 вҠҶ оүҖ2 .

(2) For each рқҗө1 вҲҲ оҲ®1 and each рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗө1 there is a рқҗө2 вҲҲ оҲ®2 such that рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗө2 вҠҶ рқҗө1 .

Proof. Assume (1). Let рқҗө1 вҲҲ оҲ®1 and рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗө1 . Since рқҗө1 вҲҲ оүҖ1 and оүҖ1 вҠҶ оүҖ2 we have рқҗө1 вҲҲ оүҖ2 . Furthermore, as оүҖ2 is the topology generated by оҲ®2 there is a рқҗө2 вҲҲ оҲ®2 such that рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗө2 where рқҗө2 вҠҶ рқҗө1 . Hence, (2) is satisfied.

Chapter 4. Generating topologies 25

Now assume (2). Let рқ‘Ҳ вҲҲ оүҖ1 . We must prove that рқ‘Ҳ вҲҲ оүҖ2 . Since оҲ®1 generates оүҖ1 , then for each рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ҳ there is a рқҗө1 вҲҲ оҲ®1 such that рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗө1 вҠҶ рқ‘Ҳ . By assumption there is a рқҗө2 вҲҲ оҲ®2 such that рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗө2 вҠҶ рқҗө1 . Hence, рқҗө2 вҠҶ рқ‘Ҳ , and so, рқ‘Ҳ вҲҲ оүҖ2 . Thus (1) is satisfied.

In order to have оүҖ1 вҠҶ оүҖ2 it is not necessary to have оҲ®1 вҠҶ оҲ®2 , i.e., each basis element in оҲ®1 need not be a basis element in оҲ®2 . However, for each basis element рқҗө1 вҲҲ оҲ®1 and each point рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗө1 there should be some (possibly) smaller basis element рқҗө2 вҲҲ оҲ®2 such that рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗө2 вҠҶ рқҗө1 .

4.3 Subbasis for a topology Let рқ‘Ӣ be a set, and let оҲҝ be a collection of subsets of рқ‘Ӣ. We can form a basis оҲ® for a topology by simply taking all finite intersections рқ‘ӣ вӢӮ рқҗө= рқ‘Ҷрқ‘– рқ‘–=1 of elements of оҲҝ. Thus the open sets in the topology generated by this basis are all unions of such basis elements рқҗө, cf. Theorem 4.10. Hence, the open sets are all unions of all finite intersections of elements of оҲҝ. The collection оҲҝ is then referred to as a subbasis. We will impose the restriction that the union of the elements of оҲҝ is equal to рқ‘Ӣ to avoid ambiguity about the intersection of an empty collection.

nodes.title: Subbasis
nodes.originalText: Let рқ‘Ӣ be a set. A subbasis for a topology on рқ‘Ӣ is a collection оҲҝ of subsets of рқ‘Ӣ whose union equals рқ‘Ӣ.

nodes.title: Lemma 4.13
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a set, and let оҲҝ be a subbasis for a topology on рқ‘Ӣ. The collection оҲ® consisting of all finite intersections of elements of оҲҝ is a basis for a topology on рқ‘Ӣ and is called the basis associated to оҲҝ.

Proof. Each рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ must lie in some рқ‘Ҷ вҲҲ оҲҝ. Hence, рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ҷ. Thus рқ‘Ҙ is an element of the basis element рқ‘Ҷ in оҲ®, and so, B1 is satisfied. вӢӮ вӢӮрқ‘ӣ вҖІ Let рқҗө1 = рқ‘ҡ рқ‘–=1 рқ‘Ҷрқ‘– and рқҗө2 = рқ‘–=1 рқ‘Ҷрқ‘– be two basis elements of оҲ®, and let рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗө1 вҲ© рқҗө2 . We must prove that there is a basis element рқҗө3 вҲҲ оҲ® such that рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗө3 вҠҶ рқҗө1 вҲ© рқҗө2 . Let (рқ‘ҡ ) ( рқ‘ӣ ) вӢӮ вӢӮ вҖІ рқҗө3 = рқ‘Ҷрқ‘– вҲ© рқ‘Ҷрқ‘– . рқ‘–=1 рқ‘–=1

Then рқҗө3 is also a finite intersection of elements of оҲҝ, and hence, рқҗө3 вҲҲ оҲ® with рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗө3 . Thus B2 is satisfied.

By combining the previous lemma with Theorem 4.10, we get the following lemma.

nodes.title: Lemma 4.14
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a set, and let оҲҝ be a subbasis for a topology on рқ‘Ӣ. The collection оүҖ generated by оҲҝ consisting of all unions of all basis elements of the associated basis оҲ® is a topology on рқ‘Ӣ.

When referring to the topology оүҖ generated by the subbasis оҲҝ we mean the topology generated by the associated basis оҲ®. We have оҲҝ вҠҶ оҲ® вҠҶ оүҖ .

26 4.4. Exercises

The following theorem provides an explicit description of the topology generated by a collection of subsets оҲҝ of a set рқ‘Ӣ.

nodes.title: Theorem 4.15
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a set, and let оҲҝ be a subbasis for a topology on рқ‘Ӣ. Then there is a unique topology вҹЁоҲҝвҹ© generated by оҲҝ which is coarser than any other topology containing оҲҝ, where { } вӢғвӢӮ рқ‘ӣрқңҶ | | вҹЁоҲҝвҹ© = рқ‘ҶрқңҶ,рқ‘– | рқ‘ҶрқңҶ,рқ‘– вҲҲ оҲҝ . | рқңҶвҲҲОӣ рқ‘–=1 |

In other words, вҹЁоҲҝвҹ© is the topology for which оҲҝ is a subbasis. Proof. Since the discrete topology оүҖdisc = оҲј(рқ‘Ӣ), there is at least one topology on рқ‘Ӣ that contains оҲҝ. We know from Theorem 4.1 that taking the intersection of all topologies that contain оҲҝ is again a topology which clearly still contains оҲҝ. By construction, вҹЁоҲҝвҹ© is then contained in any other topology containing оҲҝ. Thus вҹЁоҲҝвҹ© is the unique topology with this property. Let { } вӢғвӢӮ рқ‘ӣрқңҶ | | оүҖоҲҝ = рқ‘ҶрқңҶ,рқ‘– | рқ‘ҶрқңҶ,рқ‘– вҲҲ оҲҝ . | рқңҶвҲҲОӣ рқ‘–=1 | Then оүҖоҲҝ вҠҶ вҹЁоҲҝвҹ©. We need to prove that they are equal. To do this we will prove that оүҖоҲҝ is a topology on рқ‘Ӣ that contains оҲҝ. Hence, by the first part вҹЁоҲҝвҹ© = оүҖоҲҝ . Since оҲҝ is a subbasis for a topology on рқ‘Ӣ, by Lemma 4.14 we know that the topology generated by оҲҝ is equal to the collection of all unions of basis elements of the associated оҲ® to оҲҝ. Hence, оүҖоҲҝ is a topology on рқ‘Ӣ. We end this section with a theorem about continuity and (sub)basis.

nodes.title: Theorem 4.16
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ң be topological spaces, and let оҲ® (resp., оҲҝ) be a basis (resp., subbasis) for the topology on рқ‘Ң . Then a map рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң is continuous if and only if for each рқҗө вҲҲ оҲ® (resp. рқ‘Ҷ вҲҲ оҲҝ) the preimage рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқҗө) (resp., рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҷ)) is open in рқ‘Ӣ.

Proof. We prove the statement about basis. Assume that рқ‘“ is continuous. Since each basis element рқҗө вҲҲ оҲ® is open in рқ‘Ң , then by continuity рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқҗө) is open in рқ‘Ӣ. Assume that for each рқҗө вҲҲ оҲ® the preimage рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқҗө) is open in рқ‘Ӣ. Let оүҖрқ‘Ң be the topology on рқ‘Ң . вӢғ Since every рқ‘ү вҲҲ оүҖрқ‘Ң is a union рқ‘ү = рқңҶвҲҲОӣ рқҗөрқңҶ of basis elements рқҗөрқңҶ вҲҲ оҲ®, we have вӢғ рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘ү ) = рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқҗөрқңҶ ). рқңҶвҲҲОӣ

Thus if each рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқҗөрқңҶ ) is open in рқ‘Ӣ, so is рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘ү ).

4.4 Exercises

Exercise 4.1 Let рқ‘Ӣ = {рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ, рқ‘җ, рқ‘‘, рқ‘’}, and let

оүҖ = {вҲ…, {рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ}, {рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ, рқ‘‘, рқ‘’}, {рқ‘Ҹ}, {рқ‘Ҹ, рқ‘‘, рқ‘’}, {рқ‘Ҹ, рқ‘җ, рқ‘‘, рқ‘’}, {рқ‘җ, рқ‘‘, рқ‘’}, {рқ‘‘, рқ‘’}, рқ‘Ӣ}

be a topology on рқ‘Ӣ. Show that оҲҝ = {{рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ}, {рқ‘Ҹ, рқ‘‘, рқ‘’}, {рқ‘җ, рқ‘‘, рқ‘’}} is a subbasis for оүҖ . Is оҲҝ вҖІ = {{рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ}, {рқ‘Ҹ, рқ‘җ, рқ‘‘, рқ‘’}, {рқ‘‘, рқ‘’}} a subbasis for оүҖ ?

Chapter 4. Generating topologies 27

Exercise 4.2 Let оҲ® be the collection of all open intervals (рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ) = {рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ в„қ вҲЈ рқ‘Һ < рқ‘Ҙ < рқ‘Ҹ} in в„қ.

(a) Show that оҲ® is a basis for a topology on в„қ. The topology generated by оҲ® is called the standard topology on в„қ denoted by оүҖstd .

(b) Show that оүҖstd = оүҖрқ‘‘ where оүҖрқ‘‘ is the metric topology obtained from the metric рқ‘‘(рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) = |рқ‘Ҙ вҲ’ рқ‘Ұ|.

Exercise 4.3 Show that

оҲҝ = {(рқ‘Һ, вҲһ) вҲЈ рқ‘Һ вҲҲ в„қ} вҲӘ {(вҲ’вҲһ, рқ‘Ҹ) вҲЈ рқ‘Ҹ вҲҲ в„қ}

is a subbasis for the standard topology on в„қ.

Exercise 4.4 Let в„ҡ deonte the set of rational numbers, and let в„қ denote the set of real numbers. Show that оҲ® = {(рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ) вҲЈ рқ‘Һ < рқ‘Ҹ, рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ вҲҲ в„ҡ} is a basis for the standard topology on в„қ where (рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ) = {рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ в„қ вҲЈ рқ‘Һ < рқ‘Ҙ < рқ‘Ҹ}.

Exercise 4.5 Let оҲ® be the collection of all half-open intervals of the form [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ) = {рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ в„қ вҲЈ рқ‘Һ в©Ҫ рқ‘Ҙ < рқ‘Ҹ} in в„қ.

(a) Show that оҲ® is a basis for a topology on в„қ. The topology generated by оҲ® is called the lower limit topology on в„қ.

(b) Find the closure of the subset (0, 1) of в„қ given the lower limit topology.

Exercise 4.6 For each рқ‘ӣ вҲҲ в„Ө, let { {рқ‘ӣ} if рқ‘ӣ is odd, рқҗө(рқ‘ӣ) = {рқ‘ӣ вҲ’ 1, рқ‘ӣ, рқ‘ӣ + 1} if рқ‘ӣ is even.

Show that the collection оҲ® = {рқҗө(рқ‘ӣ) вҲЈ рқ‘ӣ вҲҲ в„Ө} is a basis for a topology on в„Ө. The topology gen- erated by оҲ® is known as the digital line topology on в„Ө. See [1, pp. 44вҖ“46] for some applications of this topology.

Exercise 4.7 Let оҲ® be the collection of all subsets of the form рқҗҙрқ‘Һ,рқ‘Ҹ = {рқ‘Һрқ‘§ + рқ‘Ҹ вҲЈ рқ‘§ вҲҲ в„Ө} of в„Ө, where рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ вҲҲ в„Ө and рқ‘Һ вү  0. (The set рқҗҙрқ‘Һ,рқ‘Ҹ is known as an arithmetic progression.)

(a) Show that оҲ® is a basis for a topology on в„Ө.

(b) Show that there are infinitely many primes by using the topology generated by оҲ®. (This topology is known as the arithmetic progression topology on в„Ө and it was used originally by Furstenberg [3] to show that there are infinitely many primes.)

28 4.4. Exercises

Exercise 4.8 Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space, and let оҲ® be a basis for the topology on рқ‘Ӣ. Show that a subset рқҗҙ of рқ‘Ӣ is dense in рқ‘Ӣ if and only if every non-empty basis element in оҲ® intersects рқҗҙ. (Recall that a set рқ‘Ҳ intersects a set рқ‘ү if рқ‘Ҳ вҲ© рқ‘ү вү  вҲ….)

5. Constructing topological spaces

5.1 Subspaces Let рқҗҙ be a subset of a topological space рқ‘Ӣ. There is a natural way to define a topology on рқҗҙ that is based on the topology on рқ‘Ӣ.

nodes.title: Subspace topology
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space, and let рқҗҙ be a subset of рқ‘Ӣ. The collection оүҖрқҗҙ = {рқҗҙ вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ вҲЈ рқ‘Ҳ is open in рқ‘Ӣ} of subsets of рқҗҙ is called the subspace topology on рқҗҙ.

The subspace topology is indeed a topology.

nodes.title: Lemma 5.2
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space, and let рқҗҙ be a subset of рқ‘Ӣ. Then the collection оүҖрқҗҙ = {рқҗҙ вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ вҲЈ рқ‘Ҳ is open in рқ‘Ӣ} is a topology on рқҗҙ.

Proof. Let оүҖ denote the topology on рқ‘Ӣ. Since вҲ…, рқ‘Ӣ вҲҲ оүҖ , вҲ… = рқҗҙ вҲ© вҲ… and рқҗҙ = рқҗҙ вҲ© рқ‘Ӣ, then, clearly, вҲ…, рқҗҙ вҲҲ оүҖрқҗҙ . Hence, T1 is satisfied. Let {рқ‘үрқңҶ }рқңҶвҲҲОӣ be a collection of subsets of рқҗҙ who are open in рқҗҙ, i.e., рқ‘үрқңҶ вҲҲ оүҖрқҗҙ . We must show that вӢғ рқңҶвҲҲОӣ рқ‘үрқңҶ вҲҲ оүҖрқҗҙ . For each рқңҶ вҲҲ Оӣ there is a рқ‘ҲрқңҶ вҲҲ оүҖ such that рқ‘үрқңҶ = рқҗҙ вҲ© рқ‘ҲрқңҶ . Thus вӢғ вӢғ( ) вӢғ рқ‘үрқңҶ = рқҗҙ вҲ© рқ‘ҲрқңҶ = рқҗҙ вҲ© рқ‘ҲрқңҶ . рқңҶвҲҲОӣ рқңҶвҲҲОӣ рқңҶвҲҲОӣ вӢғ вӢғ Since рқңҶвҲҲОӣ рқ‘ҲрқңҶ вҲҲ оүҖ , it follows that рқңҶвҲҲОӣ рқ‘үрқңҶ вҲҲ оүҖрқҗҙ . Hence, T2 is satisfied. Let рқ‘ү1 , рқ‘ү2 , вҖҰ , рқ‘үрқ‘ӣ be subsets of рқҗҙ that are open in рқҗҙ, i.e., рқ‘үрқ‘– вҲҲ оүҖрқҗҙ for рқ‘– = 1, 2, вҖҰ , рқ‘ӣ. We must вӢӮ show that рқ‘ӣрқ‘–=1 рқ‘үрқ‘– вҲҲ оүҖрқҗҙ . For each рқ‘– вҲҲ {1, 2, вҖҰ , рқ‘ӣ} there is a рқ‘Ҳрқ‘– вҲҲ оүҖ such that рқ‘үрқ‘– = рқҗҙ вҲ© рқ‘Ҳрқ‘– . Thus

рқ‘ӣ рқ‘ӣ рқ‘ӣ вӢӮ вӢӮ ( ) вӢӮ рқ‘үрқ‘– = рқҗҙ вҲ© рқ‘Ҳрқ‘– = рқҗҙ вҲ© рқ‘Ҳрқ‘– . рқ‘–=1 рқ‘–=1 рқ‘–=1

вӢӮрқ‘ӣ вӢӮрқ‘ӣ Since рқ‘–=1 рқ‘Ҳрқ‘– вҲҲ оүҖ , it follows that рқ‘–=1 рқ‘үрқ‘– вҲҲ оүҖрқҗҙ . Hence, T3 is satisfied.

Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space (with topology оүҖ ), and let рқҗҙ be a subspace of рқ‘Ӣ. If рқ‘ү is a subset of рқҗҙ, there are two possible meanings to the statement вҖңрқ‘ү is open.вҖқ We can either take рқ‘ү to be open in рқ‘Ӣ, i.e., рқ‘ү вҲҲ оүҖ , or we can take рқ‘ү to be open in рқҗҙ, i.e., рқ‘ү вҲҲ оүҖрқҗҙ . In general, these do not mean the same thing. We say that рқ‘ү is closed in рқҗҙ if рқ‘ү is closed in the subspace topology of рқҗҙ. In other words, if рқ‘ү is closed in рқҗҙ, then рқҗҙ в§ө рқ‘ү is open in рқҗҙ.

30 5.1. Subspaces

nodes.title: Theorem 5.3
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space, and let рқҗҙ be a subspace of рқ‘Ӣ. Then a subset рқҗҫ of рқҗҙ is closed in рқҗҙ if and only if there is a closed subset рқҗҝ of рқ‘Ӣ with рқҗҫ = рқҗҙ вҲ© рқҗҝ.

Proof. Assume that рқҗҫ is a closed subset of рқҗҙ. Then рқ‘ү = рқҗҙ в§ө рқҗҫ is open in рқҗҙ, i.e., there is an open subset рқ‘Ҳ of рқ‘Ӣ with рқ‘ү = рқҗҙ вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ . Moreover, рқҗҝ = рқ‘Ӣ в§ө рқ‘Ҳ is a closed subset of рқ‘Ӣ and

рқҗҙ вҲ© рқҗҝ = рқҗҙ вҲ© (рқ‘Ӣ в§ө рқ‘Ҳ ) = рқҗҙ в§ө (рқҗҙ вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ ) = рқҗҙ в§ө рқ‘ү = рқҗҫ.

See Figure 5.1.

рқ‘Ӣ

рқҗҙ рқ‘Ҳ

рқҗҫ

рқ‘ү

Figure 5.1: рқҗҫ = рқҗҙ вҲ© рқҗҝ

Now assume that рқҗҝ is a closed subset of рқ‘Ӣ with рқҗҫ = рқҗҙ вҲ© рқҗҝ. Then рқ‘Ҳ = рқ‘Ӣ в§ө рқҗҝ is an open subset of рқ‘Ӣ, and so, рқ‘ү = рқҗҙ вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ is an open subset of рқҗҙ. Furthermore,

рқҗҙ в§ө рқҗҫ = рқҗҙ в§ө (рқҗҙ вҲ© рқҗҝ) = рқҗҙ вҲ© (рқ‘Ӣ в§ө рқҗҝ) = рқҗҙ вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ = рқ‘ү .

See Figure 5.2. Hence, рқҗҫ is a closed subset of рқҗҙ.

рқ‘Ӣ рқҗҝ рқҗҙ

рқ‘ү

рқҗҫ

Figure 5.2: рқ‘ү = рқҗҙ в§ө рқҗҫ

The next example illustrates the fact that we may have subsets of a topological space рқ‘Ӣ that are open in the subspace рқҗҙ but which are not open in рқ‘Ӣ.

Chapter 5. Constructing topological spaces 31

nodes.title: Example 5.4
nodes.originalText:
Let в„қ denote the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology, cf. Exercise 4.2, and let рқҗј = [0, 1] be a subspace of в„қ. Then sets of the form [0, рқ‘Һ) and (рқ‘Һ, 1] with 0 < рқ‘Һ < 1 are open in рқҗј but not in в„қ.

The following theorem describes how we may extract a basis for the subspace topology on рқҗҙ from the basis of a topology on рқ‘Ӣ.

nodes.title: Theorem 5.5
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space, and let оҲ® be a basis for the topology on рқ‘Ӣ. If рқҗҙ is a subset of рқ‘Ӣ, then the collection

оҲ®рқҗҙ = {рқҗҙ вҲ© рқҗө вҲЈ рқҗө вҲҲ оҲ®}

is a basis for the subspace topology on рқҗҙ.

Proof. We need to prove that оҲ®рқҗҙ is a basis for the subspace topology on рқҗҙ. We first prove that оҲ®рқҗҙ is a basis for a topology on рқҗҙ, and then that the topology generated by оҲ®рқҗҙ equals the subspace topology on рқҗҙ. First note that each рқҗө вҲҲ оҲ® is open in рқ‘Ӣ, and so, each рқҗҙ вҲ© рқҗө вҲҲ оҲ®рқҗҙ is open in рқҗҙ. For each рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ there is a basis element рқҗө вҲҲ оҲ® such that рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗө. Let рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗҙ. Since рқҗҙ = рқҗҙ вҲ© рқ‘Ӣ, there must be a basis element рқҗө вҲҲ оҲ® such that рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗҙ вҲ© рқҗө. Hence, B1 holds. Now let рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗҙ вҲ© (рқҗө1 вҲ© рқҗө2 ) where рқҗө1 and рқҗө2 are basis elements of оҲ®. Since оҲ® is a basis for the topology on рқ‘Ӣ, it follows that there is a basis element рқҗө3 of оҲ® such that рқҗө3 вҠҶ рқҗө1 вҲ© рқҗө2 and рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗҙ вҲ© рқҗө3 вҠҶ рқҗҙ вҲ© (рқҗө1 вҲ© рқҗө2 ). Hence, B2 is satisfied. Thus оҲ®рқҗҙ is a basis for a topology on рқҗҙ. Let оүҖрқҗҙ be the subspace topology on рқҗҙ. We want to prove that the topology оүҖ вҖІ generated by оҲ®рқҗҙ is equal to оүҖрқҗҙ . If рқҗҙ вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ вҲҲ оүҖрқҗҙ and рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗҙ вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ , then, using the fact that оҲ® is a basis for the topology on рқ‘Ӣ, we have рқҗө вҲҲ оҲ® such that рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗҙ вҲ© рқҗө вҠҶ рқҗҙ вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ . Thus рқҗҙ вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ вҲҲ оүҖ вҖІ , cf. Theorem 4.9. By Theorem 4.10 we know that оүҖ вҖІ is equal to the collection of all unions of elements of оҲ®рқҗҙ . Hence, if рқ‘Ҡ вҲҲ оүҖ вҖІ then рқ‘Ҡ equals a union of elements of оҲ®рқҗҙ . Since each element of оҲ®рқҗҙ belongs to оүҖрқҗҙ and оүҖрқҗҙ is a topology, рқ‘Ҡ also belongs to оүҖрқҗҙ .

We end this section with an alternative description of the subspace topology. Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space, and let рқ‘Ү be a set. There do exist topologies on рқ‘Ү that make рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ү вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ continuous, e.g., the discrete topology. If оүҖрқ‘“ is the intersection of all topologies on рқ‘Ү such that рқ‘“ is continuous, then оүҖрқ‘“ is the coarsest topology for which рқ‘“ is continuous and

оүҖрқ‘“ = {рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҳ ) вҲЈ рқ‘Ҳ is open in рқ‘Ӣ}.

From this we may define the subspace topology as follows: Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space, and let рқҗҙ be subset of рқ‘Ӣ. The subspace topology on рқҗҙ is then the coarsest topology on рқҗҙ for which the inclusion рқ‘– вҲ¶ рқҗҙ вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ, given by рқ‘–(рқ‘Ҙ) = рқ‘Ҙ for рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗҙ, is continuous. This coincides with our previous definition as рқ‘–вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҳ ) = рқҗҙ вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ for any subset рқ‘Ҳ of рқ‘Ӣ. Thus

оүҖрқ‘– = {рқ‘–вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҳ ) вҲЈ рқ‘Ҳ is open in рқ‘Ӣ} = {рқҗҙ вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ вҲЈ рқ‘Ҳ is open in рқ‘Ӣ},

and hence, оүҖрқ‘– = оүҖрқҗҙ . The following theorem describes a universal property for the subspace topology.

32 5.1. Subspaces

nodes.title: Theorem 5.6
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space, and let рқҗҙ be a subset of рқ‘Ӣ. Then the subspace topology on рқҗҙ is the only topology on рқҗҙ with the following universal property: for every topo- logical space рқ‘Ң and every map рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ң вҶ’ рқҗҙ, рқ‘“ is continuous if and only if рқ‘–в—Ұрқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ң вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ is continuous where рқ‘– вҲ¶ рқҗҙ вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ is the inclusion map given by рқ‘–(рқ‘Ҙ) = рқ‘Ҙ for рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗҙ.

рқ‘Ӣ рқ‘–в—Ұрқ‘“ рқ‘–

рқ‘Ң рқҗҙ рқ‘“

Proof. We will first prove that the subspace topology оүҖрқҗҙ has the universal property that for every topological space рқ‘Ң and every map рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ң вҶ’ рқҗҙ, рқ‘“ is continuous if and only if рқ‘–в—Ұрқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ң вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ is continuous. Then we will prove that оүҖрқҗҙ is the only topology on рқҗҙ with this property. Consider рқҗҙ as a subspace of рқ‘Ӣ. Assume that рқ‘“ is continuous. Since the inclusion map рқ‘– is con- tinuous (with рқҗҙ given the subspace topology), and the composition of two continuous maps is again continuous, cf. Theorem 3.16, it follows that рқ‘–в—Ұрқ‘“ is continuous. Now assume that рқ‘–в—Ұрқ‘“ is continuous. Let рқ‘ү be an open set in рқҗҙ, i.e., рқ‘ү = рқҗҙ вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ for some open set рқ‘Ҳ in рқ‘Ӣ. Since

рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘ү ) = рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқҗҙ вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ ) = рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘–вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҳ )) = (рқ‘–в—Ұрқ‘“ )вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҳ )

is open in рқ‘Ң by continuity of рқ‘–в—Ұрқ‘“ , it follows that рқ‘“ is continuous. Thus the subspace topology оүҖрқҗҙ has the desired property. Let оүҖ вҖІ be a topology on рқҗҙ with the universal property that for every topological space рқ‘Ң and every map рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ң вҶ’ рқҗҙ, рқ‘“ is continuous if and only if рқ‘–в—Ұрқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ң вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ is continuous. We must show that оүҖрқҗҙ = оүҖ вҖІ . Let оүҖ be the topology on рқ‘Ӣ, and let рқҗҙ be given the topology оүҖ вҖІ . First let рқ‘Ң = рқҗҙ with the subspace topology. Then for рқ‘“ = id вҲ¶ (рқҗҙ, оүҖрқҗҙ ) вҶ’ (рқҗҙ, оүҖ вҖІ ), we have рқ‘–в—Ұ id = рқ‘– вҲ¶ (рқҗҙ, оүҖрқҗҙ ) вҶ’ (рқ‘Ӣ, оүҖ ) which is continuous. Hence, by the universal property id is continuous.

(рқ‘Ӣ, оүҖ ) рқ‘–в—Ұ id рқ‘–

(рқҗҙ, оүҖрқҗҙ ) (рқҗҙ, оүҖ вҖІ ) id

Thus any рқ‘ү вҲҲ оүҖ вҖІ must also be an element of оүҖрқҗҙ , and so, оүҖ вҖІ вҠҶ оүҖрқҗҙ . Secondly let рқ‘Ң = рқҗҙ with оүҖ вҖІ as its topology. Then, clearly, рқ‘“ = id вҲ¶ (рқҗҙ, оүҖ вҖІ ) вҶ’ (рқҗҙ, оүҖ вҖІ ) is continu- ous. Thus by the universal property it follows that рқ‘–в—Ұ id = рқ‘– вҲ¶ (рқҗҙ, оүҖ вҖІ ) вҶ’ (рқ‘Ӣ, оүҖ ) is continuous.

(рқ‘Ӣ, оүҖ ) рқ‘–в—Ұ id рқ‘–

(рқҗҙ, оүҖ вҖІ ) (рқҗҙ, оүҖ вҖІ ) id

Thus for any рқ‘Ҳ вҲҲ оүҖ , we have рқ‘–вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҳ ) = рқҗҙ вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ вҲҲ оүҖ вҖІ . That is, оүҖрқҗҙ вҠҶ оүҖ вҖІ . Hence, оүҖрқҗҙ = оүҖ вҖІ .

Chapter 5. Constructing topological spaces 33

5.2 Products Let рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ң be topological spaces. If we want to give the product рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң a topology, a first approach might be to take the collection оҲҜ = {рқ‘Ҳ Г— рқ‘ү вҲЈ рқ‘Ҳ is open in рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘ү is open in рқ‘Ң } as the topology on рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң . However, оҲҜ is not a topology. The union of two elements of оҲҜ is not necessarily of the form рқ‘Ҳ Г— рқ‘ү for some рқ‘Ҳ open in рқ‘Ӣ and some рқ‘ү open in рқ‘Ң . See Figure 5.3.

рқ‘Ң

рқ‘ү1 рқ‘Ҳ1 Г— рқ‘ү1

рқ‘ү2 рқ‘Ҳ2 Г— рқ‘ү2

рқ‘Ӣ рқ‘Ҳ1 рқ‘Ҳ2

Figure 5.3: The collection оҲҜ of all products of open sets in рқ‘Ӣ and in рқ‘Ң is not a topology on рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң .

We can remedy the situation by taking оҲҜ as a basis instead. The topology generated from this basis is what we will take to be the product topology on рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң .

nodes.title: Product topology
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ң be topological spaces. The product topology on рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң is the topology generated by the basis

оҲ® = {рқ‘Ҳ Г— рқ‘ү вҲЈ рқ‘Ҳ is open in рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘ү is open in рқ‘Ң }.

nodes.title: Lemma 5.8
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ң be topological spaces. Then the collection

оҲ® = {рқ‘Ҳ Г— рқ‘ү вҲЈ рқ‘Ҳ is open in рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘ү is open in рқ‘Ң }

is a basis for a topology on рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң .

Proof. Let (рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң . We need to show that there is a basis element рқ‘Ҳ Г— рқ‘ү вҲҲ оҲ® such that (рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) вҲҲ рқ‘Ҳ Г— рқ‘ү вҠҶ рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң . Since рқ‘Ӣ is open in рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ң is open in рқ‘Ң , we simply take рқ‘Ҳ = рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘ү = рқ‘Ң . Thus B1 is satisfied. Now let (рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) вҲҲ (рқ‘Ҳ1 Г— рқ‘ү1 ) вҲ© (рқ‘Ҳ2 Г— рқ‘ү2 ) where рқ‘Ҳ1 Г— рқ‘ү1 , рқ‘Ҳ2 Г— рқ‘ү2 вҲҲ оҲ®. Since (рқ‘Ҳ1 Г— рқ‘ү1 ) вҲ© (рқ‘Ҳ2 Г— рқ‘ү2 ) = (рқ‘Ҳ1 вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ2 ) Г— (рқ‘ү1 вҲ© рқ‘ү2 ), and рқ‘Ҳ1 вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ2 and рқ‘ү1 вҲ© рқ‘ү2 are open in рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ң , respectively, it follows by letting рқ‘Ҳ3 = рқ‘Ҳ1 вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ2 and рқ‘ү3 = рқ‘ү1 вҲ©рқ‘ү2 that there is a basis element рқ‘Ҳ3 Г—рқ‘ү3 вҲҲ оҲ® such that (рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) вҲҲ рқ‘Ҳ3 Г—рқ‘ү3 вҠҶ (рқ‘Ҳ1 Г—рқ‘ү1 )вҲ©(рқ‘Ҳ2 Г—рқ‘ү2 ). Hence, B2 holds. Thus оҲ® is a basis for a topology on рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң .

34 5.2. Products

The basis described in Lemma 5.8 is relatively large; it consists of pairs of every open set рқ‘Ҳ in рқ‘Ӣ and every open set рқ‘ү in рқ‘Ң . The following theorem describes a smaller basis for the product topology based on bases rather than whole topologies.

nodes.title: Theorem 5.9
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ң be topological spaces. If оҲ®рқ‘Ӣ is a basis for the topology on рқ‘Ӣ and оҲ®рқ‘Ң is a basis for the topology on рқ‘Ң , then the collection

оҲ®рқ‘ӢГ—рқ‘Ң = {рқҗөрқ‘Ӣ Г— рқҗөрқ‘Ң вҲЈ рқҗөрқ‘Ӣ вҲҲ оҲ®рқ‘Ӣ and рқҗөрқ‘Ң вҲҲ оҲ®рқ‘Ң }

is a basis for the product topology on рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң .

Proof. We follow the arguments for the proof of Theorem 5.5 and adapt them to our current setting. First note that each рқҗөрқ‘Ӣ Г— рқҗөрқ‘Ң вҲҲ оҲ®рқ‘Ӣ Г— оҲ®рқ‘Ң is open in рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң as each рқҗөрқ‘Ӣ is open in рқ‘Ӣ and each рқҗөрқ‘Ң is open in рқ‘Ң . Let (рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң . Using the fact that оҲ®рқ‘Ӣ and оҲ®рқ‘Ң are bases for topologies on рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ң , respectively, there are basis elements рқҗөрқ‘Ӣ вҲҲ оҲ®рқ‘Ӣ and рқҗөрқ‘Ң вҲҲ оҲ®рқ‘Ң such that (рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) вҲҲ рқҗөрқ‘Ӣ Г— рқҗөрқ‘Ң вҠҶ рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң . Thus B1 is satisfied. Now let (рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) вҲҲ (рқҗөрқ‘Ӣ,1 Г— рқҗөрқ‘Ң ,1 ) вҲ© (рқҗөрқ‘Ӣ,2 Г— рқҗөрқ‘Ң ,2 ) where рқҗөрқ‘Ӣ,1 , рқҗөрқ‘Ӣ,2 вҲҲ оҲ®рқ‘Ӣ and рқҗөрқ‘Ң ,1 , рқҗөрқ‘Ң ,2 вҲҲ оҲ®рқ‘Ң . Since (рқҗөрқ‘Ӣ,1 Г— рқҗөрқ‘Ң ,1 ) вҲ© (рқҗөрқ‘Ӣ,2 Г— рқҗөрқ‘Ң ,2 ) = (рқҗөрқ‘Ӣ,1 вҲ© рқҗөрқ‘Ӣ,2 ) Г— (рқҗөрқ‘Ң ,1 вҲ© рқҗөрқ‘Ң ,2 ) and оҲ®рқ‘Ӣ and оҲ®рқ‘Ң are bases for the topologies on рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ң , respectively, it follows that there are basis elements рқҗөрқ‘Ӣ,3 вҲҲ оҲ®рқ‘Ӣ and рқҗөрқ‘Ң ,3 вҲҲ оҲ®рқ‘Ң such that рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗөрқ‘Ӣ,3 вҠҶ рқҗөрқ‘Ӣ,1 вҲ© рқҗөрқ‘Ӣ,2 , рқ‘Ұ вҲҲ рқҗөрқ‘Ң ,3 вҠҶ рқҗөрқ‘Ң ,1 вҲ© рқҗөрқ‘Ң ,2 and (рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) вҲҲ рқҗөрқ‘Ӣ,3 Г— рқҗөрқ‘Ң ,3 вҠҶ (рқҗөрқ‘Ӣ,1 вҲ© рқҗөрқ‘Ӣ,2 ) Г— (рқҗөрқ‘Ң ,1 вҲ© рқҗөрқ‘Ң ,2 ). Thus B2 holds. Hence, оҲ®рқ‘ӢГ—рқ‘Ң is a basis for a topology on рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң . Let оүҖрқ‘ӢГ—рқ‘Ң be the product topology on рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң . We want to prove that the topology оүҖ вҖІ generated by оҲ®рқ‘ӢГ—рқ‘Ң is equal to оүҖрқ‘ӢГ—рқ‘Ң . Let рқ‘Ҡ вҲҲ оүҖрқ‘ӢГ—рқ‘Ң , and let (рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) вҲҲ рқ‘Ҡ . Then there is an open set рқ‘Ҳ in рқ‘Ӣ and an open set рқ‘ү in рқ‘Ң such that (рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) вҲҲ рқ‘Ҳ Г— рқ‘ү вҠҶ рқ‘Ҡ , cf. Theorem 4.9. Since рқ‘Ҳ is open in рқ‘Ӣ and оҲ®рқ‘Ӣ is a basis for the topology on рқ‘Ӣ, it follows that there is a basis element рқҗөрқ‘Ӣ вҲҲ оҲ®рқ‘Ӣ such that рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗөрқ‘Ҙ вҠҶ рқ‘Ҳ . Likewise, there is a basis element рқҗөрқ‘Ң вҲҲ оҲ®рқ‘Ң such that рқ‘Ұ вҲҲ рқҗөрқ‘Ң вҠҶ рқ‘ү . Thus (рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) вҲҲ рқҗөрқ‘Ӣ Г— рқҗөрқ‘Ң вҠҶ рқ‘Ҡ , and so, рқ‘Ҡ вҲҲ оүҖ вҖІ , cf. Theorem 4.9. By Theorem 4.10, we know that оүҖ вҖІ is equal to the collection of all unions of elements of оҲ®рқ‘ӢГ—рқ‘Ң . Hence, if рқ‘Ҡ вҲҲ оүҖ вҖІ then рқ‘Ҡ equals a union of elements of оҲ®рқ‘ӢГ—рқ‘Ң . Since each element of оҲ®рқ‘ӢГ—рқ‘Ң belongs to оүҖрқ‘ӢГ—рқ‘Ң and оүҖрқ‘ӢГ—рқ‘Ң is a topology, рқ‘Ҡ also belongs to оүҖрқ‘ӢГ—рқ‘Ң .

nodes.title: Theorem 5.13
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ң be topological spaces. Let pr 1 вҲ¶ рқ‘ӢГ—рқ‘Ң вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ and pr 2 вҲ¶ рқ‘ӢГ—рқ‘Ң вҶ’ рқ‘Ң be the projections of рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң onto its first and second factors, respectively. The product topology is the only topology on рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң with the following universal property: for every topological space рқ‘Қ and every map рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Қ вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң , рқ‘“ is continuous if and only if pr 1 в—Ұрқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Қ вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ and pr 2 в—Ұрқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Қ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң are continuous.

рқ‘ӢГ—рқ‘Ң рқ‘ӢГ—рқ‘Ң рқ‘“ рқ‘“ pr 1 pr 2

рқ‘Қ рқ‘Ӣ рқ‘Қ рқ‘Ң pr 1 в—Ұрқ‘“ pr 2 в—Ұрқ‘“

Proof. We follow the arguments for the proof of Theorem 5.6 and adapt them to our current setting. We first prove that the product topology оүҖрқ‘ӢГ—рқ‘Ң has the universal property that for every topolog- ical space рқ‘Қ and every map рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Қ вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң , рқ‘“ is continuous if and only if pr 1 в—Ұрқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Қ вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ and pr 2 в—Ұрқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Қ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң are continuous. Let рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң be given the product topology, and let рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Қ вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң be continuous. Since pr вҲ’1 1 (рқ‘Ҳ ) = рқ‘Ҳ Г— рқ‘Ң for an open set рқ‘Ҳ in рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ң is open in рқ‘Ң , it follows that pr 1 is continuous. Likewise, pr 2 вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ Г—

рқ‘Ң вҶ’ рқ‘Ң is continuous. Thus by Theorem 3.16 both pr 1 в—Ұрқ‘“ and pr 2 в—Ұрқ‘“ are continuous. Now assume that pr 1 в—Ұрқ‘“ and pr 2 в—Ұрқ‘“ are continuous. Let рқ‘Ҳ Г— рқ‘ү be a subset of рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң where рқ‘Ҳ is an open set in рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘ү is an open set in рқ‘Ң . Since рқ‘Ҳ Г— рқ‘ү = pr вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҳ ) вҲ© pr вҲ’1 (рқ‘ү ) and рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҳ Г— рқ‘ү ) = рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (pr вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҳ ) вҲ© pr вҲ’1 (рқ‘ү )) = рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (pr вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҳ )) вҲ© рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (pr вҲ’1 (рқ‘ү )) = (pr 1 в—Ұрқ‘“ )вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҳ ) вҲ© (pr 2 в—Ұрқ‘“ )вҲ’1 (рқ‘ү ) is open in рқ‘Қ by continuity of pr 1 в—Ұрқ‘“ and pr 2 в—Ұрқ‘“ , and subsets of the form рқ‘Ҳ Г— рқ‘ү form a basis for the product topology on рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң , cf. Lemma 5.8, it follows that рқ‘“ is continuous. Let оүҖ be a topology on рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң with the universal property that for every topological space рқ‘Қ and every map рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Қ вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң , рқ‘“ is continuous if and only if pr 1 в—Ұрқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Қ вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ and pr 2 в—Ұрқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Қ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң are continuous. We must show that оүҖрқ‘ӢГ—рқ‘Ң = оүҖ . Let оүҖрқ‘Ӣ be the topology on рқ‘Ӣ and оүҖрқ‘Ң be the topology on рқ‘Ң , and let рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң be given the topology оүҖ . First let рқ‘Қ = рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң with the product topology. Then for рқ‘“ = id вҲ¶ (рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң , оүҖрқ‘ӢГ—рқ‘Ң ) вҶ’ (рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң , оүҖ ), we have pr 1 в—Ұ id = pr 1 вҲ¶ (рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң , оүҖрқ‘ӢГ—рқ‘Ң ) вҶ’ (рқ‘Ӣ, оүҖрқ‘Ӣ ) and pr 2 в—Ұ id = pr 2 вҲ¶ (рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң , оүҖрқ‘ӢГ—рқ‘Ң ) вҶ’ (рқ‘Ң , оүҖрқ‘Ң ) which are both continuous. Thus by the universal property id is continuous.
