(рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң , оүҖрқ‘ӢГ—рқ‘Ң ) (рқ‘Ӣ, оүҖрқ‘Ӣ ) (рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң , оүҖрқ‘ӢГ—рқ‘Ң ) (рқ‘Ң , оүҖрқ‘Ң ) pr 1 в—Ұ id pr 2 в—Ұ id

Hence, any рқ‘Ҡ вҲҲ оүҖ must also be an element of оүҖрқ‘ӢГ—рқ‘Ң , and so, оүҖ вҠҶ оүҖрқ‘ӢГ—рқ‘Ң . Secondly let рқ‘Қ = рқ‘Ӣ Г—рқ‘Ң with оүҖ as its topology. Then, clearly, рқ‘“ = id вҲ¶ (рқ‘Ӣ Г—рқ‘Ң , оүҖ ) вҶ’ (рқ‘Ӣ Г—рқ‘Ң , оүҖ ) is continuous. Thus by the universal property it follows that both pr 1 в—Ұ id = pr 1 вҲ¶ (рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң , оүҖ ) вҶ’ (рқ‘Ӣ, оүҖрқ‘Ӣ ) and pr 2 в—Ұ id = pr 2 вҲ¶ (рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң , оүҖ ) вҶ’ (рқ‘Ң , оүҖрқ‘Ң ) are continuous.

(рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң , оүҖ ) (рқ‘Ӣ, оүҖрқ‘Ӣ ) (рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң , оүҖ ) (рқ‘Ң , оүҖрқ‘Ң ) pr 1 в—Ұ id pr 2 в—Ұ id

Thus for each рқ‘Ҳ вҲҲ оүҖрқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘ү вҲҲ оүҖрқ‘Ң , we have pr вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҳ ) = рқ‘Ҳ Г— рқ‘Ң вҲҲ оүҖ and pr вҲ’1 (рқ‘ү ) = рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘ү вҲҲ оүҖ , and so, рқ‘Ҳ Г— рқ‘ү = pr вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҳ ) вҲ© pr вҲ’1 (рқ‘ү ) вҲҲ оүҖ . Hence, оүҖрқ‘ӢГ—рқ‘Ң вҠҶ оүҖ . Thus оүҖрқ‘ӢГ—рқ‘Ң = оүҖ .

We can extend our discussion of the product topology from рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң to рқ‘Ӣ1 Г— рқ‘Ӣ2 Г— вӢҜ Г— рқ‘Ӣрқ‘ӣ where each рқ‘Ӣрқ‘– вҲҸ is a topological space. If we are to extend to the product рқңҶвҲҲОӣ рқ‘ӢрқңҶ , which we can think of as the set of maps вӢғ рқ‘“ вҲ¶ Оӣ вҶ’ рқңҶвҲҲОӣ рқ‘ӢрқңҶ where рқ‘“ (рқңҶ) вҲҲ рқ‘ӢрқңҶ for each рқңҶ вҲҲ Оӣ, of an indexed family {рқ‘ӢрқңҶ }рқңҶвҲҲОӣ of topological spaces we вҲҸ вҲҸ may proceed in two ways. We may equip рқңҶвҲҲОӣ рқ‘ӢрқңҶ with the topology generated by the basis рқңҶвҲҲОӣ рқ‘ҲрқңҶ where вҲҸ рқ‘ҲрқңҶ is open in рқ‘ӢрқңҶ for each рқңҶ вҲҲ Оӣ. This is known as the box topology. We may also equip рқңҶвҲҲОӣ рқ‘ӢрқңҶ with the вӢғ topology generated by the subbasis оҲҝ = рқңҮвҲҲОӣ {pr вҲ’1 рқңҮ (рқ‘ҲрқңҮ ) вҲЈ рқ‘ҲрқңҮ is open in рқ‘ӢрқңҮ }. This is known as the product вҲҸрқ‘ӣ topology. For finite products рқ‘–=1 рқ‘Ӣрқ‘– the two topologies are the same. Also, the box topology is, in general, finer than the product topology. Finally, several results regarding finite products may be extended to arbitrary products when using the product topology but not the box topology.

Chapter 5. Constructing topological spaces 37

5.3 Quotient spaces Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space. In Section 5.1, we discussed how to define the coarsest possible topology on a subset рқҗҙ of рқ‘Ӣ such that the inclusion map рқ‘– вҲ¶ рқҗҙ вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ is continuous. This is known as the subspace topology. If we let рқҗҙ be a set which is not necessarily a subset of рқ‘Ӣ and we consider a surjective map рқңӢ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқҗҙ, the quotient topology is the finest topology on рқҗҙ such that рқңӢ is continuous. The torus рқ‘Ү 2 = рқ‘Ҷ 1 Г— рқ‘Ҷ 1 (see Figure 5.4) can be constructed by taking a rectangle and вҖңgluingвҖқ its edges together in an appropriate way as shown in Figure 5.5. Such a construction involves the concept of quotient topology.

nodes.title: Equivalence classes
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a set, and let вҲј be an equivalence relation on рқ‘Ӣ. The equivalence class of рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ is the subset

[рқ‘Ҙ] = {рқ‘Ұ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ вҲЈ рқ‘Ҙ вҲј рқ‘Ұ}

of рқ‘Ӣ. Let рқ‘ӢвҲ•вҲј = {[рқ‘Ҙ] вҲЈ рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ} be the set of equivalence classes.

By definition, рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ [рқ‘Ҙ] for each рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ and [рқ‘Ҙ] = [рқ‘Ұ] if and only if рқ‘Ҙ вҲј рқ‘Ұ. Moreover, two equivalence classes [рқ‘Ҙ1 ] and [рқ‘Ҙ2 ] are either disjoint or equal. Finally, the union of all equivalence classes equal рқ‘Ӣ.

nodes.title: Lemma 5.15
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ and рқҗҙ be sets, and let рқңӢ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқҗҙ be a surjective map. Then the map

рқң‘ вҲ¶ рқ‘ӢвҲ•вҲј вҶ’ рқҗҙ

given by рқң‘([рқ‘Ҙ]) = рқңӢ(рқ‘Ҙ), where рқ‘Ҙ1 вҲј рқ‘Ҙ2 if and only if рқңӢ(рқ‘Ҙ1 ) = рқңӢ(рқ‘Ҙ2 ), is a bijection.

Proof. The map is well-defined since [рқ‘Ҙ1 ] = [рқ‘Ҙ2 ] only if рқ‘Ҙ1 вҲј рқ‘Ҙ2 , and so, рқңӢ(рқ‘Ҙ1 ) = рқңӢ(рқ‘Ҙ2 ) by definition of the equivalence relation. It is injective since рқң‘([рқ‘Ҙ1 ]) = рқң‘([рқ‘Ҙ2 ]) implies рқңӢ(рқ‘Ҙ1 ) = рқңӢ(рқ‘Ҙ2 ), and so, рқ‘Ҙ1 вҲј рқ‘Ҙ2 , i.e., [рқ‘Ҙ1 ] = [рқ‘Ҙ2 ]. Finally, it is surjective since рқңӢ is; every element of рқҗҙ is of the form рқңӢ(рқ‘Ҙ) = рқң‘([рқ‘Ҙ]) for some рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ.

Thus by Lemma 5.15 we can, up to a bijection, go back and forth between equivalence relations on рқ‘Ӣ and surjective maps рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқҗҙ.

38 5.3. Quotient spaces

nodes.title: Quotient space
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space, let рқҗҙ be a set, and let рқңӢ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқҗҙ be a surjective map. The quotient topology on рқҗҙ induced by рқңӢ is the collection of subsets рқ‘Ҳ of рқҗҙ such that рқңӢ вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҳ ) is open in рқ‘Ӣ. We say that рқңӢ is a quotient map if рқҗҙ is given the quotient topology, and we call рқҗҙ the quotient space.

In other words, рқңӢ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқҗҙ is quotient map if it is surjective and a subset рқ‘Ҳ of рқҗҙ is open in рқҗҙ if and only if рқңӢ вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҳ ) is open in рқ‘Ӣ. Equivalently, рқңӢ is a quotient map if it is surjective and рқ‘Ҳ is closed in рқҗҙ if and only if рқңӢ вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҳ ) is closed in рқ‘Ӣ. Clearly, a quotient map is continuous.

nodes.title: Lemma 5.17
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space, let рқҗҙ be a set, and let рқңӢ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқҗҙ be a surjective map. Then the quotient topology on рқҗҙ induced by рқңӢ is a topology and it is the finest topology on рқҗҙ such that рқңӢ is continuous.

Proof. Since рқңӢ вҲ’1 (вҲ…) = вҲ… and рқңӢ вҲ’1 (рқҗҙ) = рқ‘Ӣ, and both вҲ… and рқ‘Ӣ are open in рқ‘Ӣ, it follows that вҲ… and рқҗҙ are open in рқҗҙ. Thus T1 holds. Let {рқ‘ҲрқңҶ }рқңҶвҲҲОӣ be a collection of subsets of рқҗҙ that are open in рқҗҙ. Then рқңӢ вҲ’1 (рқ‘ҲрқңҶ ) is open in рқ‘Ӣ for each рқңҶ вҲҲ Оӣ. Since ( ) вӢғ вӢғ вҲ’1 рқңӢ рқ‘ҲрқңҶ = рқңӢ вҲ’1 (рқ‘ҲрқңҶ ) рқңҶвҲҲОӣ рқңҶвҲҲОӣ вӢғ is a union of open sets in рқ‘Ӣ, it must be open in рқ‘Ӣ. Hence, рқңҶвҲҲОӣ рқ‘ҲрқңҶ is open in рқҗҙ. Thus T2 is satisfied. We prove that the intersection of the elements of any finite subcollection of the quotient topology on рқҗҙ induced by рқңӢ is also an element of the quotient topology on рқҗҙ induced by рқңӢ. Let рқ‘Ҳ1 , рқ‘Ҳ2 , вҖҰ , рқ‘Ҳрқ‘ӣ be subsets of рқҗҙ that are open in рқҗҙ. Then рқңӢ вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҳрқ‘– ) is open in рқ‘Ӣ for рқ‘– = 1, 2, вҖҰ , рқ‘ӣ. Since ( рқ‘ӣ ) рқ‘ӣ вӢӮ вӢӮ рқңӢ вҲ’1 рқ‘Ҳрқ‘– = рқңӢ вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҳрқ‘– ) рқ‘–=1 рқ‘–=1

is a (finite) intersection of open sets in рқ‘Ӣ, it must be open in рқ‘Ӣ. Hence, рқ‘Ҳ1 вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ2 вҲ© вӢҜ вҲ© рқ‘Ҳрқ‘ӣ is open in рқҗҙ, and so, T3 is satisfied. Thus the quotient topology is a topology. Let оүҖ be a topology on рқҗҙ such that рқңӢ is continuous. We must show that оүҖ is coarser than the quotient topology. Since рқңӢ is continuous when рқҗҙ is given оүҖ as its topology, we have for each рқ‘ү вҲҲ оүҖ that рқңӢ вҲ’1 (рқ‘ү ) is open in рқ‘Ӣ, and so, рқ‘ү is in the quotient topology. Hence, оүҖ is coarser than the quotient topology.

nodes.title: Example 5.18
nodes.originalText:
Let в„қ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology, let рқҗҙ = {рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ, рқ‘җ}, and let рқңӢвҲ¶ в„қ вҶ’ рқҗҙ be the map given by вҺ§рқ‘Һ рқ‘Ҙ = 0, вҺӘ рқңӢ(рқ‘Ҙ) = вҺЁрқ‘Ҹ рқ‘Ҙ < 0, вҺӘрқ‘җ рқ‘Ҙ > 0. вҺ© Then the quotient topology on рқҗҙ induced by рқңӢ is the collection {вҲ…, {рқ‘Ҹ}, {рқ‘Ҹ, рқ‘җ}, {рқ‘җ}, рқҗҙ} of subsets of рқҗҙ.

Chapter 5. Constructing topological spaces 39

nodes.title: Open and closed maps
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ң be topological spaces, and let рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң be a continuous map. We say that рқ‘“ is an open map if for each subset рқ‘Ҳ of рқ‘Ӣ that is open in рқ‘Ӣ the image рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҳ ) is open in рқ‘Ң . Likewise, we say that рқ‘“ is a closed map if for each subset рқ‘ү of рқ‘Ӣ that is closed in рқ‘Ӣ the image рқ‘“ (рқ‘ү ) is closed in рқ‘Ң .

If рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң is an open (closed) map and рқҗҙ is a subset of рқ‘Ӣ then it is not true in general that рқ‘“ |рқҗҙ вҲ¶ рқҗҙ вҶ’ рқ‘“ (рқҗҙ) is an open (closed) map. However, if we also assume that рқҗҙ is an open (closed) subset of рқ‘Ӣ, then рқ‘“ |рқҗҙ вҲ¶ рқҗҙ вҶ’ рқ‘“ (рқҗҙ) is an open (closed) map. Consider the case where рқҗҙ is open in рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘“ is an open map. Let рқ‘Ҳ be a subset of рқҗҙ that is open in рқҗҙ, i.e., рқ‘Ҳ = рқҗҙ вҲ© рқ‘ү for some open subset рқ‘ү of рқ‘Ӣ. Then рқ‘Ҳ is also open in рқ‘Ӣ, and hence, рқ‘“ |рқҗҙ (рқ‘Ҳ ) = рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҳ ) is an open subset of рқ‘Ң . Furthermore, since рқ‘“ (рқҗҙ) is open in рқ‘Ң and рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҳ ) вҠҶ рқ‘“ (рқҗҙ), рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҳ ) must be open in рқ‘“ (рқҗҙ).

nodes.title: Example 5.20
nodes.originalText: Any homeomorphism is both open and closed. However, the converse is, in general, not true. Let в„қ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology, and let вҲ— be the space consisting of a single element. Then в„қ вҶ’вҲ— is both an open and a closed map but it is clearly not a homeomorphism.

nodes.title: Theorem 5.21
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ң be two topological spaces, and let рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң be a bijective continuous map. Then the following are equivalent:

(1) рқ‘“ is a homeomorphism;

(2) рқ‘“ is an open map;

(3) рқ‘“ is a closed map.

Proof. We will show that (1) is equivalent to (2) and that (2) is equivalent to (3). Since рқ‘“ is a bijection, we have ( вҲ’1 )вҲ’1 рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҳ ) = рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҳ )

for any subset рқ‘Ҳ of рқ‘Ӣ. In particular, if рқ‘Ҳ is an open subset of рқ‘Ӣ and if we assume that рқ‘“ is a homeo- morphism then рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҳ ) must be open in рқ‘Ң as рқ‘“ вҲ’1 is continuous. Hence, рқ‘“ is an open map. Furthermore, if we assume that рқ‘“ is open instead of being a homeomorphism then рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҳ ) is open in рқ‘Ң for рқ‘Ҳ an open subset of рқ‘Ӣ. Hence, рқ‘“ вҲ’1 вҲ¶ рқ‘Ң вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ is continuous. Thus рқ‘“ is a homeomorphism. Hence, (1) is equivalent to (2). Assume that рқ‘“ is an open map. Since рқ‘“ is a bijection, we have

рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ӣ в§ө рқ‘Ҳ ) = рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҳ рқ‘җ ) = рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҳ )рқ‘җ = рқ‘Ң в§ө рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҳ ).

Thus рқ‘“ is closed: рқ‘Ӣ в§ө рқ‘Ҳ is closed and рқ‘Ң в§ө рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҳ ) is closed. The opposite implication follows by a completely analogous argument. Hence, (2) is equivalent to (3). This completes the proof.

The following lemma establishes a relation between open and closed maps, and quotient maps.

40 5.3. Quotient spaces

nodes.title: Lemma 5.22
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ң be topological spaces, and let рқңӢ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң be a surjective contin- uous map.

(1) If рқңӢ is in addition open then it is a quotient map.

(2) If рқңӢ is in addition closed then it is a quotient map.

Proof. Assume that рқңӢ is in addition open. Let рқ‘ү be a subset of рқ‘Ң . If рқ‘ү is open in рқ‘Ң then рқңӢ вҲ’1 (рқ‘ү ) is open in рқ‘Ӣ by assumption of continuity of рқңӢ. If рқңӢ вҲ’1 (рқ‘ү ) is open in рқ‘Ӣ then since рқңӢ is surjective, we have

рқңӢ(рқңӢ вҲ’1 (рқ‘ү )) = рқ‘ү

which is open in рқ‘Ң since we have assumed that рқңӢ is an open map. Hence, (1) holds. Now assume that рқңӢ is also closed in addition to being a surjective continuous map. Let рқ‘Ҡ be a subset of рқ‘Ң . If рқ‘Ҡ is closed in рқ‘Ң then рқңӢ вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҡ ) is closed in рқ‘Ӣ by assumption of continuity of рқңӢ. If рқңӢ вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҡ ) is closed in рқ‘Ӣ then since рқңӢ is surjective, we have

рқңӢ(рқңӢ вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҡ )) = рқ‘Ҡ

which is closed in рқ‘Ң since we have assumed that рқңӢ is a closed map. Hence, (2) holds.

nodes.title: Example 5.23
nodes.originalText:
Let в„қ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Consider [0, 1] as a subspace of в„қ and рқ‘Ҷ 1 as a subspace of в„қ2 where в„қ2 is also given the standard topology. Let рқңӢ вҲ¶ [0, 1] вҶ’ рқ‘Ҷ 1 be the map given by рқңӢ(рқ‘Ў) = (cos(2рқңӢрқ‘Ў), sin(2рқңӢрқ‘Ў)). Then, clearly, рқңӢ is a surjective continuous map. We can show that рқңӢ is also closed (to do this it helps to have defined compactness). Thus by Lemma 5.22 рқңӢ is a quotient map. Note that рқңӢ is not open as рқңӢ([0, рқ‘Ў)) is not open for 0 < рқ‘Ў < 1 (where we are using the fact that [0, рқ‘Ў) is open in [0, 1] for 0 < рқ‘Ў < 1). Let вҲј be the equivalence relation on [0, 1] given by рқ‘  вҲј рқ‘Ў if and only if рқңӢ(рқ‘ ) = рқңӢ(рқ‘Ў), and let

рқ‘қ вҲ¶ [0, 1] вҶ’ [0, 1]вҲ•вҲј

be the map given by рқ‘қ(рқ‘Ў) = [рқ‘Ў]. Then, clearly, рқ‘қ is a surjective continuous map. The induced bijective map

рқң‘ вҲ¶ [0, 1]вҲ•вҲј вҶ’ рқ‘Ҷ 1

given by рқң‘([рқ‘Ў]) = рқңӢ(рқ‘Ў) is then a homeomorphism from [0, 1]вҲ•вҲј with the quotient topology induced by рқ‘қ to рқ‘Ҷ 1 with the quotient topology induced by рқңӢ. See Figure 5.6.

nodes.title: Example 5.24
nodes.originalText:
Consider [0, 1] Г— [0, 1] as a subspace of в„қ Г— в„қ = в„қ2 where в„қ2 is given the standard topology, and рқ‘Ү 2 = рқ‘Ҷ 1 Г— рқ‘Ҷ 1 as a subspace of в„қ2 Г— в„қ2 = в„қ4 where в„қ4 is also given the standard topology. Let рқңӢ вҲ¶ [0, 1] Г— [0, 1] вҶ’ рқ‘Ҷ 1 Г— рқ‘Ҷ 1 be the map given by

рқңӢ(рқ‘ , рқ‘Ў) = (cos(2рқңӢрқ‘ ), sin(2рқңӢрқ‘ ), cos(2рқңӢрқ‘Ў), sin(2рқңӢрқ‘Ў)).

Clearly, рқңӢ is a surjective continuous function. As in the previous example, we can show that рқңӢ is closed, and so, by Lemma 5.22 it is a quotient map. Let вҲј be the equivalence relation on [0, 1] Г— [0, 1] given by

(рқ‘ , 0) вҲј (рқ‘ , 1) and (0, рқ‘Ў) вҲј (1, рқ‘Ў),

and let рқ‘қ вҲ¶ [0, 1] Г— [0, 1] вҶ’ ([0, 1] Г— [0, 1])вҲ•вҲј be the map given by рқ‘қ(рқ‘ , рқ‘Ў) = [рқ‘ , рқ‘Ў]. Then, clearly, рқ‘қ is a surjective continuous map. The induced bijective map

рқң‘ вҲ¶ ([0, 1] Г— [0, 1])вҲ•вҲј вҶ’ рқ‘Ҷ 1 Г— рқ‘Ҷ 1

given by рқң‘([рқ‘ , рқ‘Ў]) = рқңӢ(рқ‘ , рқ‘Ў) is then a homeomorphism from ([0, 1] Г— [0, 1])вҲ•вҲј with the quotient topology induced by рқ‘қ to рқ‘Ҷ 1 Г— рқ‘Ҷ 1 with the quotient topology induced by рқңӢ. The equivalence classes are then the sets

{(0, 0), (0, 1), (1, 0), (1, 1)}

(the four vertices of the square are identified),

{(рқ‘ , 0), (рқ‘ , 1)} and {(0, рқ‘Ў), (1, рқ‘Ў)}

(opposing edges of the square are identified), and

{(рқ‘ , рқ‘Ў)}

for рқ‘ , рқ‘Ў вҲҲ (0, 1). See Figure 5.5.

42 5.3. Quotient spaces

nodes.title: Example 5.25
nodes.originalText:
The real projective space is the quotient space

в„қPрқ‘ӣ = рқ‘Ҷ рқ‘ӣ вҲ•вҲј

where рқ‘Ҷ рқ‘ӣ is the рқ‘ӣ-sphere and вҲј is the equivalence relation given by рқ‘Ҙ вҲј рқ‘Ұ if and only if рқ‘Ҙ = Вұрқ‘Ұ, i.e., [рқ‘Ҙ] = {рқ‘Ҙ, вҲ’рқ‘Ҙ}. We say that рқ‘Ҙ and вҲ’рқ‘Ҙ are antipodal points. The topology on в„қPрқ‘ӣ is defined by the quotient map рқңӢ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ҷ рқ‘ӣ вҶ’ в„қPрқ‘ӣ given by рқңӢ(рқ‘Ҙ) = [рқ‘Ҙ].

We end this section with an alternative description of the quotient topology.

nodes.title: Theorem 5.26
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space, let рқҗҙ be a set, and let рқңӢ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқҗҙ be a surjective map. The quotient topology is the only topology on рқҗҙ with the following universal property: for every topological space рқ‘Ң and every map рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқҗҙ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң , рқ‘“ is continuous if and only if рқ‘“ в—ҰрқңӢ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң is continuous.

рқ‘Ӣ рқ‘“ в—ҰрқңӢ рқңӢ

рқҗҙ рқ‘Ң рқ‘“

Proof. We follow the arguments for the proofs of Theorem 5.6 and Theorem 5.13 and adapt them to our current setting. We first prove that the quotient topology оүҖ рқңӢ has the universal property that for every topological space рқ‘Ң and every map рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқҗҙ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң , рқ‘“ is continuous if and only if рқ‘“ в—ҰрқңӢ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң is continuous. Let рқҗҙ be given the quotient topology induced by рқңӢ, and assume that рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқҗҙ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң is continuous. By definition of the quotient topology рқңӢ вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҳ ) is open in рқ‘Ӣ if and only if рқ‘Ҳ is open in рқҗҙ. Hence, рқңӢ is continuous. Thus by Theorem 3.16 рқ‘“ в—ҰрқңӢ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң is continuous. Now assume that рқ‘“ в—ҰрқңӢ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң is continuous. Let рқ‘ү be an open set in рқ‘Ң . Since рқңӢ is a quotient map and

(рқ‘“ в—ҰрқңӢ)вҲ’1 (рқ‘ү ) = рқңӢ вҲ’1 (рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘ү ))

is open in рқ‘Ӣ by assumption of continuity of рқ‘“ в—ҰрқңӢ, it follows that рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘ү ) is open in рқҗҙ. Thus рқ‘“ is continuous. Let оүҖ вҖІ be a topology on рқҗҙ with the universal property that for every topological space рқ‘Ң and every map рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқҗҙ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң , рқ‘“ is continuous if and only if рқ‘“ в—ҰрқңӢ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң is continuous. We must show that оүҖ вҖІ = оүҖ рқңӢ. Let оүҖ be the topology on рқ‘Ӣ, and let рқҗҙ be given the topology оүҖ вҖІ . First let рқ‘Ң = рқҗҙ with the quotient topology induced by рқ‘“ в—ҰрқңӢ. Then for рқ‘“ = id вҲ¶ (рқҗҙ, оүҖ вҖІ ) вҶ’ (рқҗҙ, оүҖ рқңӢ ), we have

id в—ҰрқңӢ = рқңӢ вҲ¶ (рқ‘Ӣ, оүҖ ) вҶ’ (рқҗҙ, оүҖ рқңӢ )

which is continuous. Hence, by the universal property id is continuous.

Chapter 5. Constructing topological spaces 43

(рқ‘Ӣ, оүҖ ) id в—ҰрқңӢ рқңӢ

(рқҗҙ, оүҖ вҖІ ) (рқҗҙ, оүҖ рқңӢ ) id

Thus any рқ‘ү вҲҲ оүҖ рқңӢ must also be an element of оүҖ вҖІ , and so, оүҖ рқңӢ вҠҶ оүҖ вҖІ . Secondly let рқ‘Ң = рқҗҙ with оүҖ вҖІ as its topology. Then, clearly, рқ‘“ = id вҲ¶ (рқҗҙ, оүҖ вҖІ ) вҶ’ (рқҗҙ, оүҖ вҖІ ) is continu- ous. Thus by the universal property it follows that

id в—ҰрқңӢ = рқңӢ вҲ¶ (рқ‘Ӣ, оүҖ ) вҶ’ (рқҗҙ, оүҖ вҖІ )

is continuous. (рқ‘Ӣ, оүҖ ) id в—ҰрқңӢ рқңӢ

(рқҗҙ, оүҖ вҖІ ) (рқҗҙ, оүҖ вҖІ ) id

Since the quotient topology induced by рқңӢ is the finest topology on рқҗҙ such that рқңӢ is a continuous map, cf. Lemma 5.17, it follows that оүҖ вҖІ вҠҶ оүҖ рқңӢ . Hence, оүҖ вҖІ = оүҖ рқңӢ .

5.4 Exercises

Exercise 5.1 Let в„қ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Show that any subspace of the form (рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ), i.e., an open interval, is homeomorphic to в„қ.

Exercise 5.2 Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space and let рқ‘Ң be a subspace of рқ‘Ӣ. If рқҗҙ is a subset of рқ‘Ң , show that the subspace topology on рқҗҙ inherited from рқ‘Ң is equal to the subspace topology on рқҗҙ inherited from рқ‘Ӣ.

Exercise 5.3Let в„қ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology, and consider the set of rational numbers в„ҡ as a subspace of в„қ. Show that the subset { вҲҡ } | вҲҡ рқҗҙ= рқ‘ҘвҲҲв„ҡ|вҲ’ 5<рқ‘Ҙ< 5 | of в„ҡ is both open and closed in в„ҡ.

Exercise 5.4 Let рқ‘Ӣ = {рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ, рқ‘җ, рқ‘‘} be given the topology оүҖрқ‘Ӣ = {вҲ…, {рқ‘Һ}, {рқ‘Һ, рқ‘җ, рқ‘‘}, {рқ‘җ, рқ‘‘}, рқ‘Ӣ}, and let рқ‘Ң = {1, 2, 3} be given the topology оүҖрқ‘Ң = {вҲ…, {1}, {1, 3}, рқ‘Ң }. Find a basis for the product topology on рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң .

Exercise 5.5 Let рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ң be topological spaces, and let рқҗҙ and рқҗө be subsets of рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ң , respectively. Show that the topology on рқҗҙ Г— рқҗө as a subspace of the product рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң is equal to the product topology on рқҗҙ Г— рқҗө where рқҗҙ and рқҗө are given the subspace topology inherited from рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ң , respectively.

44 5.4. Exercises

Exercise 5.6 Let рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ң be topological spaces. Show that the product topology is the coarsest topology on рқ‘Ӣ Г—рқ‘Ң for which both of the projection maps pr 1 вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ Г—рқ‘Ң вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ and pr 2 вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ Г—рқ‘Ң вҶ’ рқ‘Ң are continuous.

Exercise 5.7 Let рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ң be two topological spaces, and let рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң be given the product topology. Show that if рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң is a continuous map, the subspace

рқҗә = {(рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң вҲЈ рқ‘Ұ = рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҙ)}

of рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң , is homeomorphic to рқ‘Ӣ.

Exercise 5.8 Let в„қ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Let

рқңӢвҲ¶ в„қ вҶ’ в„Ө

be the map given by { рқ‘Ҙ рқ‘ҘвҲҲв„Ө рқңӢ(рқ‘Ҙ) = рқ‘ӣ рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ (рқ‘ӣ вҲ’ 1, рқ‘ӣ + 1), and рқ‘ӣ is an odd integer.

Show that the quotient topology on в„Ө induced by рқңӢ is equal to the digital line topology, cf. Exercise 4.6.

6. Topological properties

6.1 Connected spaces One of the fundamental results of calculus is the intermediate value theorem. The theorem says that for a continuous map рқ‘“ вҲ¶ [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ] вҶ’ в„қ and for a real number рқ‘ҹ between рқ‘“ (рқ‘Һ) and рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҹ) there is a real number рқ‘җ вҲҲ [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ] such that рқ‘“ (рқ‘җ) = рқ‘ҹ. See Figure 6.1. From this result we can deduce that the graph of a continuous map (in this setting) is connected.

рқ‘Ұ

рқ‘Ұ = рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҙ)

рқ‘ҹ

рқ‘“ (рқ‘Һ) рқ‘Ҙ рқ‘Һ рқ‘җ рқ‘Ҹ

Figure 6.1: The intermediate value theorem.

nodes.title: Connected space
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space. A separation of рқ‘Ӣ is a pair of non-empty subsets рқ‘Ҳ and рқ‘ү that are open in рқ‘Ӣ, disjoint and whose union equal рқ‘Ӣ. We say that рқ‘Ӣ is connected if there are no separations of рқ‘Ӣ. Otherwise it is disconnected.

The property of being connected is a topological property as it is formulated entirely in terms of open subsets. In other words, if рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ң are homeomorphic topological spaces and рқ‘Ӣ is connected then so is рқ‘Ң .

nodes.title: Example 6.2
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be the set {рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ, рқ‘җ, рқ‘‘, рқ‘’}. If we equip рқ‘Ӣ with the topology оүҖ1 = {вҲ…, {рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ}, {рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ, рқ‘җ}, {рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ, рқ‘‘, рқ‘’}, {рқ‘‘, рқ‘’}, рқ‘Ӣ} then it is disconnected; the pair рқ‘Ҳ = {рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ, рқ‘җ} and рқ‘ү = {рқ‘‘, рқ‘’} is a separation of рқ‘Ӣ in this topology. However, if we equip рқ‘Ӣ with the topology оүҖ2 = {вҲ…, {рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ, рқ‘җ}, {рқ‘җ}, {рқ‘җ, рқ‘‘, рқ‘’}, рқ‘Ӣ} then it is connected; there are no separations of рқ‘Ӣ in this topology.

46 6.1. Connected spaces

nodes.title: Example 6.3
nodes.originalText: Let рқ‘Ӣ be an indiscrete space. Then рқ‘Ӣ is connected as there are no separations of рқ‘Ӣ, i.e., there are no non-empty open subsets of рқ‘Ӣ who are disjoint and whose union equal рқ‘Ӣ.

nodes.title: Example 6.4
nodes.originalText: Let рқ‘Ӣ be a discrete space containing two or more points. Then рқ‘Ӣ is disconnected. Let рқ‘қ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ and let рқ‘Ҳ = {рқ‘қ} and рқ‘ү = рқ‘Ҳ рқ‘җ = рқ‘Ӣ в§ө {рқ‘қ}. Then the pair рқ‘Ҳ and рқ‘ү is a separation of рқ‘Ӣ.

nodes.title: Example 6.5
nodes.originalText:
Let в„қ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology, and let рқ‘Ӣ = [0, 1) вҲӘ (1, 2) be a subspace of в„қ. Since рқ‘Ҳ = [0, 1) is open in рқ‘Ӣ (but not in в„қ) and рқ‘ү = (1, 2) is open рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘Ҳ вҲ© рқ‘ү = вҲ… and рқ‘Ҳ вҲӘ рқ‘ү = рқ‘Ӣ, they form a separation of рқ‘Ӣ. Thus рқ‘Ӣ is disconnected.

In the above examples the spaces that are connected all share the property that the only subsets that are both open and closed in рқ‘Ӣ are вҲ… and рқ‘Ӣ. Likewise, the disconnected spaces all share the property that there are non-empty proper subsets of рқ‘Ӣ that are both open and closed in рқ‘Ӣ.

nodes.title: Theorem 6.6
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space. Then рқ‘Ӣ is connected if and only if the are no non-empty proper subsets of рқ‘Ӣ that are both open and closed in рқ‘Ӣ.

Proof. We prove the equivalent statement that рқ‘Ӣ is disconnected if and only if there are non-empty proper subsets of рқ‘Ӣ that are both open and closed in рқ‘Ӣ. Assume рқ‘Ӣ is disconnected, i.e., that there is a separation of рқ‘Ӣ. Let рқ‘Ҳ and рқ‘ү be a separation of рқ‘Ӣ. Thus рқ‘Ҳ is open in рқ‘Ӣ. Since рқ‘Ҳ вҲ© рқ‘ү = вҲ… and рқ‘Ҳ вҲӘ рқ‘ү = рқ‘Ӣ, we have рқ‘Ҳ рқ‘җ = рқ‘Ӣ в§ө рқ‘Ҳ = рқ‘ү . Thus рқ‘Ҳ рқ‘җ is open in рқ‘Ӣ, and so, рқ‘Ҳ is closed in рқ‘Ӣ. Hence, рқ‘Ҳ is both open and closed in рқ‘Ӣ. Likewise, рқ‘ү is both open and closed in рқ‘Ӣ. Assume that the non-empty proper subset рқ‘Ҳ of рқ‘Ӣ is both open and closed in рқ‘Ӣ. Let рқ‘ү = рқ‘Ҳ рқ‘җ = рқ‘Ӣ в§ө рқ‘Ҳ . Then рқ‘ү is open in рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘Ҳ вҲ© рқ‘ү = вҲ… and рқ‘Ҳ вҲӘ рқ‘ү = рқ‘Ӣ. Hence, the pair рқ‘Ҳ and рқ‘ү is a separation of рқ‘Ӣ. Thus рқ‘Ӣ is disconnected.

nodes.title: Theorem 6.7
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a connected space, рқ‘Ң be a topological space, and let рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң be a surjective continuous map. Then рқ‘Ң is connected.

Proof. We prove the equivalent statement that if рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң is a surjective continuous map and рқ‘Ң is disconnected then рқ‘Ӣ is disconnected. Assume that рқ‘Ң is disconnected, i.e., there is a separation of рқ‘Ң . Let the pair рқ‘Ҳ and рқ‘ү be a separation of рқ‘Ң . Then рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҳ ) and рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘ү ) are non-empty subsets of рқ‘Ӣ which are open in рқ‘Ӣ as рқ‘“ is a surjective continuous map. Furthermore,

рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҳ ) вҲ© рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘ү ) = рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҳ вҲ© рқ‘ү ) = вҲ… and рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҳ ) вҲӘ рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘ү ) = рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҳ вҲӘ рқ‘ү ) = рқ‘Ӣ.

Hence, the pair рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҳ ) and рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘ү ) is a separation of рқ‘Ӣ. Thus рқ‘Ӣ is disconnected.

A subset рқҗҙ of a topological space рқ‘Қ is connected if рқҗҙ is connected in the subspace topology. Thus the theorem can be extended to saying that the continuous image of a connected space is connected, i.e., assuming рқ‘Ӣ is a connected space and рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң is a continuous map then рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ӣ) is connected in рқ‘Ң .

Chapter 6. Topological properties 47

We may also describe connectedness by way of the following theorem.

nodes.title: Theorem 6.8
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space. Then рқ‘Ӣ is connected if and only if every contin- uous map form рқ‘Ӣ to a discrete space, with at least two points, is constant.

Proof. We prove the equivalent statement that рқ‘Ӣ is disconnected if and only if there is a non-constant continuous map from рқ‘Ӣ to a discrete space (with at least two points). Assume that рқ‘Ӣ is disconnected. Let рқ‘Ҳ be a non-empty proper subset of рқ‘Ӣ that is both open and closed in рқ‘Ӣ. Let рқ‘Ң = {рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ} be given the discrete topology. The map рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң that sends рқ‘Ҳ to рқ‘Һ and рқ‘Ҳ рқ‘җ = рқ‘Ӣ в§ө рқ‘Ҳ to рқ‘Ҹ is continuous and not constant. Let рқ‘Ң be a discrete space with at least two points. Assume that рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң is a non-constant continuous map. Then for each рқ‘Ұ вҲҲ рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ӣ), i.e., рқ‘Ұ = рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҙ) for some рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘“ вҲ’1 ({рқ‘Ұ}) is a non-empty proper subset of рқ‘Ӣ that is both open and closed in рқ‘Ӣ. Thus by Theorem 6.6, рқ‘Ӣ is disconnected.

We will prove that the (finite) product of connected spaces is again connected. To prove this we will need the following two results.

nodes.title: Lemma 6.9
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a disconnected space with separation рқ‘Ҳ and рқ‘ү , and let рқҗҙ be a connected subspace of рқ‘Ӣ. Then рқҗҙ вҠҶ рқ‘Ҳ or рқҗҙ вҠҶ рқ‘ү .

Proof. Since рқ‘Ҳ and рқ‘ү are open in рқ‘Ӣ, the intersections рқҗҙ вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ and рқҗҙ вҲ© рқ‘ү are both open in рқҗҙ (in the subspace topology). Furthermore, the complement of рқҗҙвҲ©рқ‘Ҳ in рқҗҙ is equal to рқҗҙвҲ©рқ‘ү as рқ‘Ҳ рқ‘җ = рқ‘Ӣ в§өрқ‘Ҳ = рқ‘ү . Hence, рқҗҙ вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ is also closed in рқҗҙ. Thus by Theorem 6.6, рқҗҙ вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ is either empty or all of рқҗҙ as рқҗҙ is connected. If рқҗҙ вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ = вҲ… then рқҗҙ вҠҶ рқ‘ү . If рқҗҙ вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ = рқҗҙ then рқҗҙ вҠҶ рқ‘Ҳ .

nodes.title: Theorem 6.10
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space, and let {рқҗҙрқңҶ }рқңҶвҲҲОӣ be a collection of connected вӢӮ вӢғ subspaces of рқ‘Ӣ such that рқңҶвҲҲОӣ рқҗҙрқңҶ is non-empty. Then рқңҶвҲҲОӣ рқҗҙрқңҶ is connected.

вӢғ Proof. Let рқ‘Ң = рқңҶвҲҲОӣ рқҗҙрқңҶ . Suppose that рқ‘Ң is disconnected, i.e., that there is a separation of рқ‘Ң . Let рқ‘Ҳ and рқ‘ү be a separation of рқ‘Ң . We will show that this leads to a contradiction, and so, рқ‘Ң must be connected. вӢӮ Let рқ‘қ вҲҲ рқңҶвҲҲОӣ рқҗҙрқңҶ . Then either рқ‘қ вҲҲ рқ‘Ҳ or рқ‘қ вҲҲ рқ‘ү . Assume without loss of generality that рқ‘қ вҲҲ рқ‘Ҳ . By Lemma 6.9 it follows that for each рқңҶ вҲҲ Оӣ either рқҗҙрқңҶ вҠҶ рқ‘Ҳ or рқҗҙрқңҶ вҠҶ рқ‘ү . Since we have assumed that рқ‘қ вҲҲ рқ‘Ҳ we must have рқҗҙрқңҶ вҠҶ рқ‘Ҳ for all рқңҶ вҲҲ Оӣ. Thus рқ‘Ң вҠҶ рқ‘Ҳ . But this implies that рқ‘ү is empty, and hence, contradicts that рқ‘Ҳ and рқ‘ү is a separation of рқ‘Ң . Thus рқ‘Ң is connected.

We can prove that the (finite) product of connected spaces is again connected.

nodes.title: Theorem 6.11
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ1 , рқ‘Ӣ2 , вҖҰ , рқ‘Ӣрқ‘ӣ be connected spaces. Then the product space рқ‘Ӣ1 Г— рқ‘Ӣ2 Г— вӢҜ Г— рқ‘Ӣрқ‘ӣ is connected.

Proof. We prove the statement for the product of two connected spaces. The general result then follows by an induction argument. Let рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ң be two connected spaces. We must prove that рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң is connected. Since for each рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ the subspace {рқ‘Ҙ} Г— рқ‘Ң of рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң is homeomorphic to рқ‘Ң , it follows that {рқ‘Ҙ} Г— рқ‘Ң is connected. Similarly, for each рқ‘Ұ вҲҲ рқ‘Ң the subspace рқ‘Ӣ Г— {рқ‘Ұ} is homeomorphic to рқ‘Ӣ, and hence, рқ‘Ӣ Г— {рқ‘Ұ} is

48 6.1. Connected spaces

connected. Thus by Theorem 6.10 it follows that for each рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ and each рқ‘Ұ вҲҲ рқ‘Ң the subspace ({рқ‘Ҙ} Г— рқ‘Ң ) вҲӘ (рқ‘Ӣ Г— {рқ‘Ұ}) is connected as it is the union of two connected spaces whose intersection is ({рқ‘Ҙ} Г— рқ‘Ң ) вҲ© (рқ‘Ӣ Г— {рқ‘Ұ}) = {(рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ)} вү  вҲ…. Fix рқ‘Ҙ0 вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ and let рқҗҙрқ‘Ұ = ({рқ‘Ҙ0 } Г— рқ‘Ң ) вҲӘ (рқ‘Ӣ Г— {рқ‘Ұ}). Then for each рқ‘Ұ вҲҲ рқ‘Ң the subspace рқҗҙрқ‘Ұ of рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң is connected as it is the union of two connected spaces whose intersection is equal to {(рқ‘Ҙ0 , рқ‘Ұ)}. Hence, вӢғ by Theorem 6.10 it follows that рқ‘ҰвҲҲрқ‘Ң рқҗҙрқ‘Ұ is connected as it is the union of connected spaces whose intersection in non-empty. Since вӢғ рқҗҙрқ‘Ұ = рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң , рқ‘ҰвҲҲрқ‘Ң

it follows that рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң is connected.

вҲҸ The theorem can be extended to hold for arbitrary products, рқңҶвҲҲОӣ рқ‘ӢрқңҶ , if we equip the product with the вҲҸ product topology. If we equip рқңҶвҲҲОӣ рқ‘ӢрқңҶ with the box topology the statement is no longer true.

An important example of a connected space is the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology.

nodes.title: The real numbers are connected
nodes.originalText:
Let в„қ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Then в„қ is connected.

We will use the fact that the real numbers satisfy the following two properties.

(1) Every subset of в„қ that is bounded above has a least upper bound. This is known as the least upper bound property.

(2) If рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ вҲҲ в„қ with рқ‘Ҙ < рқ‘Ұ then there is a real number рқ‘§ such that рқ‘Ҙ < рқ‘§ < рқ‘Ұ.

Proof. Assume that в„қ is disconnected, i.e., that there is a separation of в„қ. Let рқ‘Ҳ and рқ‘ү be a separation of в„қ, and choose рқ‘Һ вҲҲ рқ‘Ҳ and рқ‘Ҹ вҲҲ рқ‘ү . We may assume without loss of generality that рқ‘Һ < рқ‘Ҹ. Let рқҗҙ = [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ] вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ and рқҗө = [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ] вҲ© рқ‘ү . Then the pair рқҗҙ and рқҗө is a separation of [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ], with рқ‘Һ вҲҲ рқҗҙ and рқ‘Ҹ вҲҲ рқҗө. Also note that рқҗҙ is bounded above by рқ‘Ҹ. Hence, by the least upper bound property рқҗҙ has a least upper bound; рқ‘җ = sup рқҗҙ. Thus рқ‘Һ в©Ҫ рқ‘җ в©Ҫ рқ‘Ҹ. We will show that рқ‘җ belongs neither to рқҗҙ nor to рқҗө thus contradicting the fact that рқҗҙ and рқҗө is a separation of [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ]. Assume that рқ‘җ вҲҲ рқҗө. Since рқ‘Һ вҲү рқҗө and рқҗө is open in [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ], it follows that there is a real number рқ‘‘ such that рқ‘Һ < рқ‘‘ < рқ‘җ and (рқ‘‘, рқ‘җ] вҠҶ рқҗө. This implies that рқ‘‘ is an upper bound of рқҗҙ and that рқ‘‘ is less than the least upper bound рқ‘җ. That is a contradiction, and so, рқ‘җ вҲү рқҗө. Now assume that рқ‘җ вҲҲ рқҗҙ. Since рқҗҙ is open in [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ] and рқ‘Ҹ вҲү рқҗҙ, there is a real number рқ‘‘ such that [рқ‘җ, рқ‘‘) вҠҶ рқҗҙ. For any рқ‘’ вҲҲ (рқ‘җ, рқ‘‘) it follows that рқ‘’ вҲҲ рқҗҙ and рқ‘’ > рқ‘җ. That is a contradiction to the fact that рқ‘җ is an upper bound of рқҗҙ. Thus рқ‘җ вҲү рқҗҙ. Hence, рқ‘җ вҲү рқҗҙ and рқ‘җ вҲү рқҗө. This is a contradiction to the fact that рқ‘җ вҲҲ [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ] and that рқҗҙ and рқҗө is a separation of [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ]. Thus в„қ must be connected.

As an immediate consequence of Theorem 6.12, we get that open intervals of the form (рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ), (вҲ’вҲһ, рқ‘Ҹ) and (рқ‘Һ, вҲһ) are all connected as they are all homeomorphic to в„қ (with the standard topology). We can also show that every closed interval [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ] is connected. Furthermore, by Theorem 6.11 and Theorem 6.12 в„қрқ‘ӣ is a connected space.

Chapter 6. Topological properties 49

nodes.title: Generalized intermediate value theorem
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a connected space, and let рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ в„қ be a continuous map where в„қ is given the standard topology. If рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ and if рқ‘ҹ is a real number that lies between рқ‘“ (рқ‘Һ) and рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҹ), there is a рқ‘җ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ such that рқ‘“ (рқ‘җ) = рқ‘ҹ.

Proof. Assume that рқ‘ҹ вҲү рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ӣ). We will show that this contradicts the assumption that рқ‘Ӣ is connected. By the assumption that рқ‘ҹ вҲү рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ӣ), i.e., рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ӣ) вҠҶ в„қ в§ө {рқ‘ҹ} = (вҲ’вҲһ, рқ‘ҹ) вҲӘ (рқ‘ҹ, вҲһ), we have a separation of рқ‘Ӣ: рқ‘Ҳ = рқ‘“ вҲ’1 ((вҲ’вҲһ, рқ‘ҹ)) and рқ‘ү = рқ‘“ вҲ’1 ((рқ‘ҹ, вҲһ))

are disjoint non-empty open subsets of рқ‘Ӣ whose union equals рқ‘Ӣ. Thus they are a separation of рқ‘Ӣ. This contradicts the assumption that рқ‘Ӣ is connected. Hence, рқ‘ҹ вҲҲ рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ӣ). In other words, there is a рқ‘җ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ such that рқ‘“ (рқ‘җ) = рқ‘ҹ.

We end this section with a discussion of path-connectivity.

nodes.title: Path-connected space
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space, and let рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ. A path from рқ‘Ҙ to рқ‘Ұ is a continuous map рқ‘“ вҲ¶ [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ] вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ such that рқ‘“ (рқ‘Һ) = рқ‘Ҙ and рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҹ) = рқ‘Ұ where [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ] is a subspace of в„қ with the standard topology. We say that рқ‘Ӣ is path-connected if every pair of points of рқ‘Ӣ can be joined by a path in рқ‘Ӣ.

The property of being path-connected is a topological property as it is completely described using the el- ements of рқ‘Ӣ and its open subsets. In other words, if рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ң are homeomorphic topological spaces and рқ‘Ӣ is path connected then so is рқ‘Ң .

nodes.title: Example 6.15
nodes.originalText: Let в„қ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Then в„қ is path-connected as for any two points, рқ‘қ, рқ‘һ вҲҲ в„қ, there is a path from рқ‘қ to рқ‘һ in в„қ, e.g., the path given by рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ў) = (1 вҲ’ рқ‘Ў)рқ‘қ + рқ‘Ўрқ‘һ where рқ‘Ў вҲҲ [0, 1].

nodes.title: Example 6.16
nodes.originalText:
For all рқ‘ӣ в©ҫ 2, в„қрқ‘ӣ with the standard topology is path-connected, and so is в„қрқ‘ӣ в§ө {рқ‘қ} for each рқ‘қ вҲҲ в„қрқ‘ӣ . For рқ‘ӣ = 1, в„қ в§ө {рқ‘қ} is neither connected nor path-connected.

The next theorem states that path-connectedness implies connectedness. While the converse is not true in general, it is true that open subsets of в„қрқ‘ӣ that are connected (where в„қрқ‘ӣ is given the standard topology) are also path-connected. See [7, Proposition 12.25] for a proof of this fact.

nodes.title: Path-connectedness implies connectedness
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a path-connected space. Then рқ‘Ӣ is connected.

Proof. Assume that рқ‘Ӣ is path-connected but that рқ‘Ӣ is disconnected, i.e., there is a separation of рқ‘Ӣ. Let рқ‘Ҳ and рқ‘ү be a separation of рқ‘Ӣ. Let рқ‘“ вҲ¶ [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ] вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ be a path from рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ҳ to рқ‘Ұ вҲҲ рқ‘ү . Then рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ҳ ) and рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘ү ) are disjoint non-empty open subsets of [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ] whose union is equal to [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ]. Thus they are a separation of [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ]. This is a contradiction to the fact that [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ] is a connected space.

50 6.2. Hausdorff spaces

nodes.title: The topologistвҖҷs sine curve
nodes.originalText:
The topologistвҖҷs sine curve is the subspace {( ( )) | } 1 | 0 < рқ‘Ҙ в©Ҫ 1 вҲӘ {(0, рқ‘Ұ) вҲЈ вҲ’1 в©Ҫ рқ‘Ұ в©Ҫ 1} рқ‘Ҷ= рқ‘Ҙ, sin | рқ‘Ҙ | of в„қ2 with the standard topology. It can be shown that рқ‘Ҷ is connected but not path-connected. For a proof of this fact, see [4, pp. 156вҖ“157]. See Figure 6.2.

рқ‘Ұ рқ‘Ҷ

рқ‘Ҙ

вҲ’1

Figure 6.2: The topologistвҖҷs sine curve.

6.2 Hausdorff spaces A common feature that we typically want a topological space to have is the ability to separate the individual points. This is commonly referred to as separation axioms. We will in this section focus on the most common separation axiom.

nodes.title: Definition 6.19
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space. We say that рқ‘Ӣ is Hausdorff if for each pair of points рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ, with рқ‘Ҙ вү  рқ‘Ұ, there are disjoint neighborhoods рқ‘Ҳ and рқ‘ү of рқ‘Ҙ and рқ‘Ұ, respectively. In other words, for each pair of distinct points рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ there are open subsets рқ‘Ҳ and рқ‘ү of рқ‘Ӣ with рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ҳ and рқ‘Ұ вҲҲ рқ‘ү where рқ‘Ҳ вҲ© рқ‘ү = вҲ….

The property to be a Hausdorff space is completely described using the elements of рқ‘Ӣ and its open subsets, and so, it is a topological property. In other words, if рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ң are homeomorphic topological spaces and рқ‘Ӣ is Hausdorff then so is рқ‘Ң . To see this let рқ‘Ӣ be a Hausdorff space and let рқ‘Ұ1 and рқ‘Ұ2 be distinct points in рқ‘Ң . If рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң is a homeomorphism then рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ұ1 ) and рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ұ2 ) are distinct points in рқ‘Ӣ. Since рқ‘Ӣ is Hausdorff this means that there are disjoint neighborhoods рқ‘Ҳ1 and рқ‘Ҳ2 of рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ұ1 ) and рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘Ұ2 ), respectively. Thus рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҳ1 ) and рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҳ2 ) are disjoint neighborhoods of рқ‘Ұ1 and рқ‘Ұ2 , respectively. Hence, рқ‘Ң is Hausdorff.

nodes.title: Example 6.20
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be the set {рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ, рқ‘җ}. If we equip рқ‘Ӣ with the discrete topology then рқ‘Ӣ is Hausdorff since for all pairs of distinct points рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ, the open subsets {рқ‘Ҙ} and {рқ‘Ұ} are neighborhoods of рқ‘Ҙ and рқ‘Ұ, respectively, and {рқ‘Ҙ} вҲ© {рқ‘Ұ} = вҲ…. However, if we equip рқ‘Ӣ with the topology {вҲ…, {рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ}, {рқ‘Ҹ}, рқ‘Ӣ} it is not Hausdorff; the only neighborhood of рқ‘җ is рқ‘Ҳ = рқ‘Ӣ, and no neighborhood рқ‘ү of either рқ‘Һ or рқ‘Ҹ can be disjoint from рқ‘Ӣ.

Chapter 6. Topological properties 51

nodes.title: Example 6.21
nodes.originalText: Let рқ‘Ӣ be a set, and let оүҖ1 and оүҖ2 be two topologies on рқ‘Ӣ. If оүҖ2 is finer than оүҖ1 and рқ‘Ӣ equipped with оүҖ1 as its topology is Hausdorff then рқ‘Ӣ equipped with оүҖ2 as its topology is also Hausdorff.

nodes.title: Theorem 6.22
nodes.originalText:
Every metric space is Hausdorff.

Proof. Let (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘‘) be a metric space, and let рқ‘Ҙ and рқ‘Ұ be two distinct points in рқ‘Ӣ. We must show that there are disjoint neighborhoods рқ‘Ҳ and рқ‘ү of рқ‘Ҙ and рқ‘Ұ, respectively. Let рқӣҝ = рқ‘‘(рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ). Then рқӣҝ > 0. Let рқ‘Ҳ = рқ–Ў(рқ‘Ҙ; рқӣҝвҲ•2) and рқ‘ү = рқ–Ў(рқ‘Ұ; рқӣҝвҲ•2) be neighborhoods of рқ‘Ҙ and рқ‘Ұ, respectively. Then by M3, рқ‘Ҳ вҲ© рқ‘ү = вҲ….

nodes.title: Example 6.23
nodes.originalText:
For all integers рқ‘ӣ в©ҫ 1, the metric space (в„қрқ‘ӣ , рқ‘‘) is Hausdorff. In particular, в„қрқ‘ӣ with the standard topology is Hausdorff.

All Hausdorff spaces share the property that finite subsets are closed which is an immediate con- sequence of the following theorem.

nodes.title: Theorem 6.24
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a Hausdorff space. Then for each рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ the subset {рқ‘Ҙ} of рқ‘Ӣ is closed in рқ‘Ӣ.

Proof. Let рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ with рқ‘Ҙ вү  рқ‘Ұ. Since рқ‘Ӣ is Hausdorff, we have neighborhoods рқ‘Ҳ and рқ‘ү of рқ‘Ҙ and рқ‘Ұ, respectively, such that рқ‘Ҳ вҲ© рқ‘ү = вҲ…. Then рқ‘Ҙ вҲү рқ‘ү . In other words, рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘ү рқ‘җ = рқ‘Ӣ в§ө рқ‘ү . Since рқ‘ү is open, рқ‘ү рқ‘җ is closed in рқ‘Ӣ. Thus {рқ‘Ҙ} вҠҶ рқ‘ү рқ‘җ , and hence, рқ‘Ұ вҲү {рқ‘Ҙ}. Hence, {рқ‘Ҙ} = {рқ‘Ҙ}. Thus {рқ‘Ҙ} is closed in рқ‘Ӣ.

There are examples of topological spaces who are not Hausdorff but have the property that finite subsets are closed. One such example is the set of real numbers equipped with the cofinite topology. We have seen that the (finite) product of connected spaces is connected, cf. Theorem 6.11. The same statement holds for Hausdorff spaces, i.e., the (finite) product of Hausdorff spaces is Hausdorff.

nodes.title: Theorem 6.25
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ1 , рқ‘Ӣ2 , вҖҰ , рқ‘Ӣрқ‘ӣ be Hausdorff spaces. Then the product space рқ‘Ӣ1 Г— рқ‘Ӣ2 Г— вӢҜ Г— рқ‘Ӣрқ‘ӣ is Hausdorff.

Proof. We prove the statement for two Hausdorff spaces. The general result then follows from an induction argument. Let (рқ‘Ҙ1 , рқ‘Ұ1 ) and (рқ‘Ҙ2 , рқ‘Ұ2 ) be two distinct points in рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң , i.e., рқ‘Ҙ1 вү  рқ‘Ҙ2 or рқ‘Ұ1 вү  рқ‘Ұ2 . If рқ‘Ҙ1 вү  рқ‘Ҙ2 there must be neighborhoods рқ‘Ҳрқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘үрқ‘Ӣ in рқ‘Ӣ of рқ‘Ҙ1 and рқ‘Ҙ2 , respectively, such that рқ‘Ҳрқ‘Ӣ вҲ© рқ‘үрқ‘Ӣ = вҲ… as рқ‘Ӣ is assumed to be Hausdorff. Then рқ‘Ҳрқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң and рқ‘үрқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң are neighborhoods of (рқ‘Ҙ1 , рқ‘Ұ1 ) and (рқ‘Ҙ2 , рқ‘Ұ2 ), respectively, where (рқ‘Ҳрқ‘Ӣ Г—рқ‘Ң )вҲ©(рқ‘үрқ‘Ӣ Г—рқ‘Ң ) = вҲ…. Similarly, if рқ‘Ұ1 вү  рқ‘Ұ2 there must be neighborhoods рқ‘Ҳрқ‘Ң and рқ‘үрқ‘Ң in рқ‘Ң of рқ‘Ұ1 and рқ‘Ұ2 , respectively, such that рқ‘Ҳрқ‘Ң вҲ©рқ‘үрқ‘Ң = вҲ… as рқ‘Ң is assumed to be Hausdorff. Then рқ‘Ӣ Г—рқ‘Ҳрқ‘Ң and рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘үрқ‘Ң are neighborhoods of (рқ‘Ҙ1 , рқ‘Ұ1 ) and (рқ‘Ҙ2 , рқ‘Ұ2 ), respectively, where (рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ҳрқ‘Ң ) вҲ© (рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘үрқ‘Ң ) = вҲ…. Hence, рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң is Hausdorff.

вҲҸ The theorem be extended to hold for arbitrary products, рқңҶвҲҲОӣ рқ‘ӢрқңҶ , if we equip the product with either the product topology or the box topology.

52 6.3. Compact spaces

We end this section with a result that helps us decide whether or not a topological space is Haus- dorff.

nodes.title: Theorem 6.26
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space. Then рқ‘Ӣ is Hausdorff if and only if the diagonal О” = {(рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ҙ) вҲЈ рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ} is closed in the product space рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ӣ.

Proof. Assume that рқ‘Ӣ is a Hausdorff space. Then for any two distinct points рқ‘Ҙ and рқ‘Ұ in рқ‘Ӣ, i.e., (рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) вҲҲ О”рқ‘җ = (рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ӣ) в§ө О”, there are disjoint neighborhoods рқ‘Ҳ and рқ‘ү of рқ‘Ҙ and рқ‘Ұ, respectively. Thus рқ‘Ҳ Г— рқ‘ү is open in рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ӣ and (рқ‘Ҳ Г— рқ‘ү ) вҲ© О” = вҲ…, i.e., рқ‘Ҳ Г— рқ‘ү вҠҶ О”рқ‘җ . Hence, by Theorem 3.10, it follows that О”рқ‘җ is open in рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ӣ, and so, О” is closed in рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ӣ. Now assume that О” is closed in the product space рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ӣ. Then for any point (рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ӣ with рқ‘Ҙ вү  рқ‘Ұ, i.e., (рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) вҲҲ О”рқ‘җ , there is a basis element рқ‘Ҳ Г— рқ‘ү for the product topology on рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ӣ such that (рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) вҲҲ рқ‘Ҳ Г— рқ‘ү вҠҶ О”рқ‘җ . Since рқ‘Ҳ Г— рқ‘ү вҠҶ О”рқ‘җ , we have рқ‘Ҳ вҲ© рқ‘ү = вҲ…. Thus рқ‘Ҳ and рқ‘ү are neighborhoods of рқ‘Ҙ and рқ‘Ұ, respectively, such that рқ‘Ҳ вҲ© рқ‘ү = вҲ…. Hence, рқ‘Ӣ is Hausdorff.

6.3 Compact spaces In Section 6.1 we saw how the intermediate value theorem might be generalized to connected spaces. In this section we will see how the extreme value theorem may be generalized to compact spaces. The extreme value theorem says that for a continuous map рқ‘“ вҲ¶ [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ] вҶ’ в„қ there are points рқ‘ҡ, рқ‘Җ вҲҲ [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ] such that рқ‘“ (рқ‘ҡ) в©Ҫ рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҙ) в©Ҫ рқ‘“ (рқ‘Җ) for all рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ]. See Figure 6.3.

рқ‘Ұ

рқ‘“ (рқ‘Җ)

рқ‘Ұ = рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҙ)

рқ‘“ (рқ‘ҡ) рқ‘Ҙ рқ‘Һ рқ‘Җ рқ‘ҡ рқ‘Ҹ

Figure 6.3: The extreme value theorem.

nodes.title: Cover of a space
nodes.originalText: Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space, and let оҲӯ be a collection вӢғ of subsets of рқ‘Ӣ. We say that оҲӯ is a cover of рқ‘Ӣ, or covering of рқ‘Ӣ if рқ‘Ӣ = рқҗҙвҲҲоҲӯ рқҗҙ. If рқҗҙ is also open in рқ‘Ӣ for each рқҗҙ вҲҲ оҲӯ, we say that оҲӯ is an open cover of рқ‘Ӣ, or open covering of рқ‘Ӣ. We say that оҲӯвҖІ is a subcover of оҲӯ if оҲӯвҖІ is another cover of рқ‘Ӣ that satisfies оҲӯвҖІ вҠҶ оҲӯ.

nodes.title: Example 6.28
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space, and let оҲ® be a basis for the topology on рқ‘Ӣ. Then оҲ® is an open cover of рқ‘Ӣ. Similarly, if оҲҝ is a subbasis for the topology on рқ‘Ӣ, then оҲҝ is an open cover of рқ‘Ӣ.

Chapter 6. Topological properties 53

nodes.title: Compact spaces
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space. We say that рқ‘Ӣ is compact if every open cover оҲӯ of рқ‘Ӣ contains a finite subcover.

The property of being compact is a topological property as it is formulated entirely in terms of the collection of open sets. In other words, if рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ң are homeomorphic topological spaces and рқ‘Ӣ is compact then so is рқ‘Ң .

nodes.title: Example 6.30
nodes.originalText: Let рқ‘Ӣ be a finite topological space. Then рқ‘Ӣ is compact as there are only finitely many different open subsets рқҗҙ of рқ‘Ӣ, and so, any collection covering рқ‘Ӣ must necessarily be finite.

nodes.title: Example 6.31
nodes.originalText: Let рқ‘Ӣ be an indiscrete space. Then рқ‘Ӣ is compact as the only open covers are the collections {рқ‘Ӣ} and {вҲ…, рқ‘Ӣ} which are finite.

nodes.title: Example 6.32
nodes.originalText: Let в„қ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Since the open cover оҲӯ = {(рқ‘ӣ вҲ’ 1, рқ‘ӣ + 1) вҲЈ рқ‘ӣ вҲҲ в„Ө}, does not admit a finite subcover, в„қ is not compact.

nodes.title: Compact subspaces
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space, and let рқҗҙ be a subset of рқ‘Ӣ. We say that рқҗҙ is compact if рқҗҙ is compact in the subspace topology.

If рқҗҙ is a subspace of рқ‘Ӣ, a collection оҲӯ of subsets of рқ‘Ӣ is a cover of рқҗҙ if the union of elements of оҲӯ contains рқҗҙ.

nodes.title: Lemma 6.34
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space, and let рқҗҙ be a subspace of рқ‘Ӣ. Then рқҗҙ is compact if and only if every cover of рқҗҙ by open subsets of рқ‘Ӣ contains a finite subcollection that covers рқҗҙ.

Proof. Assume that рқҗҙ is compact. Let оҲҜ be a cover of рқҗҙ by open subsets of рқ‘Ӣ. Then the collection

оҲҜ вҖІ = {рқҗҙ вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ вҲЈ рқ‘Ҳ вҲҲ оҲҜ}

is an open cover of рқҗҙ. Since рқҗҙ is compact there must be a finite subcover {рқҗҙ вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ1 , рқҗҙ вҲ© рқ‘Ҳ2 , вҖҰ , рқҗҙ вҲ© рқ‘Ҳрқ‘ӣ } of оҲҜ вҖІ . Hence, {рқ‘Ҳ1 , рқ‘Ҳ2 , вҖҰ , рқ‘Ҳрқ‘ӣ } is a finite subcollection of оҲҜ that covers рқҗҙ. Now assume that every cover of рқҗҙ by open subsets of рқ‘Ӣ contains a finite subcollection that covers рқҗҙ. Let оҲҜ = {рқ‘үрқңҶ }рқңҶвҲҲОӣ be a cover of рқҗҙ by open subsets of рқҗҙ. Hence, by definition of the subspace topology, cf. Definition 5.1, we have for each рқңҶ вҲҲ Оӣ that рқ‘үрқңҶ = рқҗҙ вҲ© рқ‘ҲрқңҶ where рқ‘ҲрқңҶ is an open subset of рқ‘Ӣ. Thus the collection оҲҜ вҖІ = {рқ‘ҲрқңҶ }рқңҶвҲҲОӣ is a cover of рқҗҙ by open subsets of рқ‘Ӣ. Then, by assumption, there must be a finite subcollection {рқ‘ҲрқңҶ1 , рқ‘ҲрқңҶ2 , вҖҰ , рқ‘ҲрқңҶрқ‘ӣ } that covers рқҗҙ. Hence, {рқ‘үрқңҶ1 , рқ‘үрқңҶ2 , вҖҰ , рқ‘үрқңҶрқ‘ӣ } is a finite subcover of оҲҜ. Thus every cover of рқҗҙ by open subsets of рқҗҙ has a finite subcover, and so, рқҗҙ is compact.

The following two theorems indicates that being compact and being closed are closely related properties.

54 6.3. Compact spaces

nodes.title: Theorem 6.35
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a compact space, and let рқҗҙ be a closed subset of рқ‘Ӣ. Then рқҗҙ is compact.

вӢғ Proof. Let оҲҜ be a cover of рқҗҙ, i.e., рқҗҙ вҠҶ рқҗ¶вҲҲоҲҜ рқҗ¶, by open subsets of рқ‘Ӣ. Since рқҗҙ is closed in рқ‘Ӣ, рқҗҙрқ‘җ = рқ‘Ӣ в§ө рқҗҙ is open in рқ‘Ӣ. Thus оҲӯ = оҲҜ вҲӘ {рқҗҙрқ‘җ } is an open cover of рқ‘Ӣ. Since рқ‘Ӣ is compact there must be a finite subcover оҲӯвҖІ вҠҶ оҲӯ of рқ‘Ӣ. If оҲӯвҖІ contains рқҗҙрқ‘җ , let оҲӯвҖІвҖІ = оҲӯвҖІ в§ө {рқҗҙрқ‘җ }. Then оҲӯвҖІвҖІ is a finite subcover of оҲҜ that covers рқҗҙ. If оҲӯвҖІ does not contain рқҗҙрқ‘җ then оҲӯвҖІ is a finite subcover of оҲҜ that covers рқҗҙ. Either way there is a finite subcover of оҲҜ that covers рқҗҙ. Thus рқҗҙ is compact.

nodes.title: Theorem 6.36
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a Hausdorff space, and let рқҗҫ be a subset of рқ‘Ӣ which is compact. Then рқҗҫ is closed in рқ‘Ӣ.

Proof. We show that рқҗҫ рқ‘җ = рқ‘Ӣ в§ө рқҗҫ is open in рқ‘Ӣ. Let рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗҫ рқ‘җ . Then for each рқ‘Ұ вҲҲ рқҗҫ there are neighborhoods рқ‘Ҳрқ‘Ұ and рқ‘үрқ‘Ұ of рқ‘Ҙ and рқ‘Ұ, respectively, such that рқ‘Ҳрқ‘Ұ вҲ© рқ‘үрқ‘Ұ = вҲ…, since рқ‘Ӣ is assumed to be Hausdorff and рқ‘Ҙ вү  рқ‘Ұ. The collection оҲҜ = {рқ‘үрқ‘Ұ вҲЈ рқ‘Ұ вҲҲ рқҗҫ} of open subsets of рқ‘Ӣ covers рқҗҫ since вӢғ рқҗҫвҠҶ рқ‘үрқ‘Ұ . рқ‘ҰвҲҲрқҗҫ

Since рқҗҫ is assumed to be compact there must be a finite subcollection {рқ‘үрқ‘Ұ1 , рқ‘үрқ‘Ұ2 , вҖҰ , рқ‘үрқ‘Ұрқ‘ӣ } that covers рқҗҫ. Let рқ‘ү = рқ‘үрқ‘Ұ1 вҲӘ рқ‘үрқ‘Ұ2 вҲӘ вӢҜ вҲӘ рқ‘үрқ‘Ұрқ‘ӣ , and let рқ‘Ҳ = рқ‘Ҳрқ‘Ұ1 вҲ© рқ‘Ҳрқ‘Ұ2 вҲ© вӢҜ вҲ© рқ‘Ҳрқ‘Ұрқ‘ӣ . Then рқ‘Ҳ is open in рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ҳ and рқ‘Ҳ вҲ© рқ‘ү = вҲ…. Furthermore, рқ‘Ҳ вҠҶ рқ‘Ӣ в§ө рқ‘ү вҠҶ рқ‘Ӣ в§ө рқҗҫ = рқҗҫ рқ‘җ . Hence, рқҗҫ рқ‘җ is open in рқ‘Ӣ.

nodes.title: Theorem 6.37
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a compact space, рқ‘Ң a topological space and let рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң be a surjective continuous map. Then рқ‘Ң is compact.

Proof. Let оҲҜ = {рқ‘ҲрқңҶ }рқңҶвҲҲОӣ be an open cover of рқ‘Ң . Then оҲӯ = {рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘ҲрқңҶ )}рқңҶвҲҲОӣ is an open cover of рқ‘Ӣ. Since рқ‘Ӣ is compact there must be a finite subcover оҲӯвҖІ = {рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘ҲрқңҶ1 ), рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘ҲрқңҶ2 ), вҖҰ , рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқ‘ҲрқңҶрқ‘ӣ )} of оҲӯ. Then оҲҜ вҖІ = {рқ‘ҲрқңҶ1 , рқ‘ҲрқңҶ2 , вҖҰ , рқ‘ҲрқңҶрқ‘ӣ } is a finite subcover of оҲҜ. Hence, рқ‘Ң is compact.

The theorem can be extended to saying that the continuous image of a compact space is compact, i.e., assuming рқ‘Ӣ is a compact space and рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң is a continuous map then рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ӣ) is compact.

We will prove that the (finite) product of compact spaces is compact. To prove this we need the following result.

nodes.title: Tube lemma
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space, and let рқ‘Ң be a compact space. If рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ҳ is an open set in the product space рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң containing {рқ‘Ҙ} Г— рқ‘Ң , then there is a neighborhood рқ‘Ҡ of рқ‘Ҙ in рқ‘Ӣ such that рқ‘Ҡ Г— рқ‘Ң вҠҶ рқ‘Ҳ .

The set рқ‘Ҡ Г— рқ‘Ң is often called a tube about {рқ‘Ҙ} Г— рқ‘Ң . See Figure 6.4.

Chapter 6. Topological properties 55

рқ‘Ң рқ‘ӢГ—рқ‘Ң рқ‘Ҳ

рқ‘Ҡ рқ‘Ҙ рқ‘Ӣ

Figure 6.4: A tube about {рқ‘Ҙ} Г— рқ‘Ң .

Proof. As рқ‘Ҳ is open in рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң and (рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) вҲҲ {рқ‘Ҙ} Г— рқ‘Ң вҠҶ рқ‘Ҳ for all рқ‘Ұ вҲҲ рқ‘Ң , there is a basis element рқ‘Ҡрқ‘Ұ Г—рқ‘үрқ‘Ұ вҠҶ рқ‘Ҳ for the product topology on рқ‘Ӣ Г—рқ‘Ң such that (рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) вҲҲ рқ‘Ҡрқ‘Ұ Г—рқ‘үрқ‘Ұ . The collection {рқ‘үрқ‘Ұ }рқ‘ҰвҲҲрқ‘Ң is an open cover of рқ‘Ң . Since рқ‘Ң is compact there must be a finite subcover of {рқ‘үрқ‘Ұ }рқ‘ҰвҲҲрқ‘Ң , say, {рқ‘үрқ‘Ұ1 , рқ‘үрқ‘Ұ2 , вҖҰ , рқ‘үрқ‘Ұрқ‘ӣ }. Let рқ‘ӣ вӢӮ рқ‘Ҡ = рқ‘Ҡрқ‘Ұрқ‘– . рқ‘–=1

Then рқ‘Ҡ is open in рқ‘Ӣ, and it must contain рқ‘Ҙ. Clearly, рқ‘ӣ ( ) вӢғ рқ‘Ҡ Г—рқ‘Ң вҠҶ рқ‘Ҡрқ‘Ұрқ‘– Г— рқ‘үрқ‘Ұрқ‘– вҠҶ рқ‘Ҳ . рқ‘–=1

Thus {рқ‘Ҙ} Г— рқ‘Ң вҠҶ рқ‘Ҡ Г— рқ‘Ң and рқ‘Ҡ Г— рқ‘Ң вҠҶ рқ‘Ҳ .

The lemma is not true if we remove the assumption that рқ‘Ң is compact; the open set

рқ‘Ҳ = {(рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ) вҲҲ в„қ Г— в„қ вҲЈ |рқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ұ| < 1} вҠҶ в„қ Г— в„қ

does contain {0} Г— в„қ but does not contain any tube рқ‘Ҡ Г— в„қ containing {0} Г— в„қ. (Here we have assumed that в„қ is given the standard topology, and hence, it is not compact, cf. Example 6.32.)

nodes.title: Theorem 6.39
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ1 , рқ‘Ӣ2 , вҖҰ , рқ‘Ӣрқ‘ӣ be compact spaces. Then the product space рқ‘Ӣ1 Г— рқ‘Ӣ2 Г— вӢҜ Г— рқ‘Ӣрқ‘ӣ is compact.

Proof. We prove the statement for the product of two compact spaces. The general result then follows by an inductive argument. Let рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ң be compact spaces. Let оҲӯ be an open cover of рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң . We must show that there is a finite subcover оҲӯвҖІ of оҲӯ. For each рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ, {рқ‘Ҙ} Г— рқ‘Ң is compact as it is homeomorphic to рқ‘Ң which is assumed to be compact. Thus there is a finite subcollection оҲӯрқ‘Ҙ of оҲӯ that covers {рқ‘Ҙ} Г— рқ‘Ң . Let вӢғ рқ‘Ҳрқ‘Ҙ = рқҗҙрқ‘Ҙ вҲҲоҲӯрқ‘Ҙ рқҗҙрқ‘Ҙ . Then рқ‘Ҳрқ‘Ҙ is open in рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң and contains {рқ‘Ҙ} Г— рқ‘Ң . Thus by Lemma 6.38 for each рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ there is a neighborhood рқ‘Ҡрқ‘Ҙ вҠҶ рқ‘Ӣ such that рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ҡрқ‘Ҙ and рқ‘Ҡрқ‘Ҙ Г— рқ‘Ң вҠҶ рқ‘Ҳрқ‘Ҙ . Furthermore, оҲӯрқ‘Ҙ covers рқ‘Ҡрқ‘Ҙ Г— рқ‘Ң . Now let рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ vary. The collection {рқ‘Ҡрқ‘Ҙ }рқ‘ҘвҲҲрқ‘Ӣ is then an open cover of рқ‘Ӣ. Since рқ‘Ӣ is compact, there must be a finite subcover {рқ‘Ҡрқ‘Ҙ1 , рқ‘Ҡрқ‘Ҙ2 , вҖҰ , рқ‘Ҡрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣ } of {рқ‘Ҡрқ‘Ҙ }рқ‘ҘвҲҲрқ‘Ӣ . For each 1 в©Ҫ рқ‘– в©Ҫ рқ‘ӣ the subspace

56 6.3. Compact spaces

рқ‘Ҡрқ‘Ҙрқ‘– Г— рқ‘Ң is covered by the finite subcollection оҲӯрқ‘Ҙрқ‘– of оҲӯ. Hence,

рқ‘ӣ вӢғ рқ‘ӢГ—рқ‘Ң = рқ‘Ҡрқ‘Ҙ рқ‘– Г— рқ‘Ң рқ‘–=1 вӢғрқ‘ӣ is covered by the subcollection оҲӯвҖІ = рқ‘–=1 оҲӯрқ‘Ҙрқ‘– of оҲӯ. Thus рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқ‘Ң is compact.

The theorem can be extended to hold for arbitrary products of compact spaces if we equip the product with the product topology. This is known as TychonoffвҖҷs theorem. It is a deep result whose proof uses several original ideas. If we equip the product with the box topology the statement is no longer true. See, e.g., [4, В§37] for a discussion and proof of TychonoffвҖҷs theorem.

We have already seen that the real line (with the standard topology) is not compact, cf. Exam- ple 6.32. The next theorem states that all closed intervals of the real line are compact.

nodes.title: Theorem 6.40
nodes.originalText:
Let в„қ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Then every closed interval [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ] in в„қ is compact.

Proof. Let оҲӯ = {рқ‘ҲрқңҶ }рқңҶвҲҲОӣ be a cover of [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ] by open subsets of в„қ, and let

рқ‘Ҷ = {рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ] вҲЈ [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҙ] is covered by a finite subcollection of оҲӯ}.

Note that рқ‘Ҷ is bounded above by рқ‘Ҹ. Since рқ‘Һ вҲҲ рқ‘ҲрқңҶ for some рқңҶ вҲҲ Оӣ, the singleton {рқ‘ҲрқңҶ } is a finite subcollection of оҲӯ that covers [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Һ] = {рқ‘Һ}. Hence, рқ‘Ҷ is non-empty and it is bounded above. Thus рқ‘Ҷ has a least upper bound; рқ‘җ = sup рқ‘Ҷ. Clearly, рқ‘Һ в©Ҫ рқ‘җ в©Ҫ рқ‘Ҹ. We will show that рқ‘җ вҲҲ рқ‘Ҷ. The result follows if we can also show that рқ‘җ = рқ‘Ҹ. Choose рқңҶвҖІ вҲҲ Оӣ with рқ‘җ вҲҲ рқ‘ҲрқңҶвҖІ . Since рқ‘ҲрқңҶвҖІ is open in в„қ there is a real number рқң– > 0 such that (рқ‘җ вҲ’ рқң–, рқ‘җ + рқң–) вҠҶ рқ‘ҲрқңҶвҖІ . Moreover, there is an рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ҷ such that рқ‘җ вҲ’ рқң– < рқ‘Ҙ, and so, by definition of рқ‘Ҷ there is a finite subcollection вӢғ {рқ‘ҲрқңҶ1 , рқ‘ҲрқңҶ2 , вҖҰ , рқ‘ҲрқңҶрқ‘ӣ } of оҲӯ such that [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҙ] вҠҶ рқ‘ӣрқ‘–=1 рқ‘ҲрқңҶрқ‘– . Furthermore, [рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘җ] вҠҶ рқ‘ҲрқңҶвҖІ . Thus [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘җ] = [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҙ] вҲӘ [рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘җ] is covered by the finite subcollection {рқ‘ҲрқңҶ1 , рқ‘ҲрқңҶ2 , вҖҰ , рқ‘ҲрқңҶрқ‘ӣ , рқ‘ҲрқңҶвҖІ } of оҲӯ. Hence, рқ‘җ вҲҲ рқ‘Ҷ. We now show that рқ‘җ = рқ‘Ҹ. Assume that рқ‘җ < рқ‘Ҹ. Then there must be a рқ‘Ұ вҲҲ [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ] such that рқ‘җ < рқ‘Ұ < рқ‘җ + рқң–. Thus [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ұ] is covered by the subcollection {рқ‘ҲрқңҶ1 , рқ‘ҲрқңҶ2 , вҖҰ , рқ‘ҲрқңҶрқ‘ӣ , рқ‘ҲрқңҶвҖІ } of оҲӯ such that рқ‘Ұ вҲҲ рқ‘Ҷ. This is a contradiction of the fact that рқ‘җ is an upper bound. Hence, рқ‘җ = рқ‘Ҹ.

In order to state and prove the HeineвҖ“Borel theorem we need the following definition.

nodes.title: Bounded subsets
nodes.originalText:
Let (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘‘) be a metric space, and let рқҗҙ be a subset of рқ‘Ӣ. We say that рқҗҙ is bounded if there is an рқ‘Җ вҲҲ в„қ such that рқ‘‘(рқ‘Һ1 , рқ‘Һ2 ) в©Ҫ рқ‘Җ for all рқ‘Һ1 , рқ‘Һ2 вҲҲ рқҗҙ.

Equivalently, we may say that a subset рқҗҙ of a metric space (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘‘) is bounded if there is a рқҗҫ вҲҲ в„қ and рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ such that рқ‘‘(рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҙ) в©Ҫ рқҗҫ for all рқ‘Һ вҲҲ рқҗҙ. In particular, this means that a subset of в„қрқ‘ӣ equipped with the Euclidean metric is bounded if it is contained in some open ball of finite radius centered at the origin.

nodes.title: HeineвҖ“Borel
nodes.originalText:
Let в„қрқ‘ӣ be given the (Euclidean) metric topology and the Eu- clidean metric. A subset рқҗҙ of в„қрқ‘ӣ is compact if and only if it is closed and bounded.

Chapter 6. Topological properties 57

Proof. Assume that рқҗҙ is compact. By Theorem 6.22, в„қрқ‘ӣ is Hausdorff, and so, by Theorem 6.36 рқҗҙ is closed in в„қрқ‘ӣ . We must show that рқҗҙ is also bounded. Let оҲӯ = {рқ–Ў(0; рқ‘ӣ) вҲЈ рқ‘ӣ вҲҲ в„Ө+ }, i.e., a collection of open balls centered at the origin in в„қрқ‘ӣ . Then оҲӯ is a cover of рқҗҙ by open subsets of в„қрқ‘ӣ . Since рқҗҙ is compact there must be a finite subcollection of оҲӯ that covers рқҗҙ. Thus there is an рқ‘Ғ вҲҲ в„Ө+ such that рқҗҙ вҠҶ рқ–Ў(0; рқ‘Ғ). Hence, for all рқ‘Һ1 , рқ‘Һ2 вҲҲ рқҗҙ we have рқ‘‘(рқ‘Һ1 , рқ‘Һ2 ) < 2рқ‘Ғ. Thus рқҗҙ is bounded. Now assume that рқҗҙ is bounded and closed in в„қрқ‘ӣ . Let рқ‘қ = (рқ‘қ1 , рқ‘қ2 , вҖҰ , рқ‘қрқ‘ӣ ) вҲҲ рқҗҙ. Since рқҗҙ is bounded there is an рқ‘Җ вҲҲ в„қ such that рқ‘‘(рқ‘Һ1 , рқ‘Һ2 ) в©Ҫ рқ‘Җ for all рқ‘Һ1 , рқ‘Һ2 вҲҲ рқҗҙ. Then рқҗҙ is contained in the product space рқ‘ғ = [рқ‘қ1 вҲ’ рқ‘Җ, рқ‘қ1 + рқ‘Җ] Г— [рқ‘қ2 вҲ’ рқ‘Җ, рқ‘қ2 + рқ‘Җ] Г— вӢҜ Г— [рқ‘қрқ‘ӣ вҲ’ рқ‘Җ, рқ‘қрқ‘ӣ + рқ‘Җ]

which by Theorem 6.40 and Theorem 6.39 is compact. Since рқҗҙ is closed in в„қрқ‘ӣ and a subset of рқ‘ғ , it follows by extending Theorem 6.35 to our setting that рқҗҙ is compact. Specifically, let оҲҜ be a cover of рқҗҙ by open subsets of в„қрқ‘ӣ . Since рқҗҙ is closed in в„қрқ‘ӣ , рқҗҙрқ‘җ = в„қрқ‘ӣ в§ө рқҗҙ is open in в„қрқ‘ӣ . Thus

оҲӯ = оҲҜ вҲӘ {рқҗҙрқ‘җ }

is an open cover of в„қрқ‘ӣ , and thus it is also an open cover of рқ‘ғ in the subspace topology. Since рқ‘ғ is compact there must be a finite subcover of оҲӯ that covers рқ‘ғ . This implies that there is a finite subcover of оҲҜ that covers рқҗҙ. Thus рқҗҙ is compact.

An immediate consequence of Theorem 6.42 is that рқ‘Ҷ рқ‘ӣ considered as a subspace of в„қрқ‘ӣ+1 is com- pact, and hence, that the torus рқ‘Ү 2 = рқ‘Ҷ 1 Г— рқ‘Ҷ 1 is compact. We end this section with a proof of the generalized extreme value theorem.

nodes.title: Generalized extreme value theorem
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a compact space, and let рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ в„қ be a continuous map where в„қ is given the standard topology. Then there are рқ‘ҡ, рқ‘Җ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ such that рқ‘“ (рқ‘ҡ) в©Ҫ рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҙ) в©Ҫ рқ‘“ (рқ‘Җ) for all рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ.

Proof. By Theorem 6.37, рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ӣ) is compact. We must show that рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ӣ) contains its supremum and its infimum. If it does, then by setting рқ‘“ (рқ‘ҡ) = inf рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ӣ) and рқ‘“ (рқ‘Җ) = sup рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ӣ) the theorem follows. We prove that рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ӣ) contains its supremum. The proof for the infimum is similar. Since рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ӣ) is compact, it is closed and bounded by Theorem 6.42. In particular, рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ӣ) is bounded above. Hence, the set рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ӣ) has a least upper bound; рқ‘  = sup рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ӣ). Thus рқ‘қ в©Ҫ рқ‘  for all рқ‘қ вҲҲ рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ӣ). We must show that рқ‘  вҲҲ рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ӣ). Assume that рқ‘  вҲү рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ӣ). Since рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ӣ) is closed, i.e., рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ӣ)рқ‘җ = в„қв§өрқ‘“ (рқ‘Ӣ) is open, it follows that there is a real number рқң– > 0 such that (рқ‘  вҲ’ рқң–, рқ‘  + рқң–) вҠҶ рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ӣ)рқ‘җ , and so,

(рқ‘  вҲ’ рқң–, рқ‘  + рқң–) вҲ© рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ӣ) = вҲ….

Hence, there is a real number рқ‘Ұ such that рқ‘Ұ is an upper bound of рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ӣ) and рқ‘  вҲ’ рқң– < рқ‘Ұ < рқ‘ . This is a contradiction to the fact that рқ‘  is the least upper bound of рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ӣ). Hence, рқ‘  = sup рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ӣ) вҲҲ рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ӣ).

58 6.4. Exercises

6.4 Exercises

Exercise 6.1 Let рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ң be topological spaces. Show that if рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң is surjective continuous map and рқ‘Ӣ is path-connected then рқ‘Ң is also path-connected.

We say that a subset рқҗҙ of a topological space рқ‘Ӣ is path-connected if рқҗҙ is path-connected in the subspace topology. Hence, the previous exercise may be extended to saying that the continuous image of a path-connected space is path-connected.

Exercise 6.2 Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space, and let рқҗҙ вҠҶ рқҗө вҠҶ рқҗҙ be subspaces of рқ‘Ӣ.

(a) Show that if рқҗҙ is connected then so is рқҗө.

(b) Show that [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ), (рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ], [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ], (вҲ’вҲһ, рқ‘Ҹ] and [рқ‘Һ, вҲһ) are all connected spaces when considered as subspaces of в„қ with the standard topology.

Exercise 6.3 Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space, and consider рқҗј = [0, 1] as a subspace of в„қ where в„қ is given the standard topology. Furthermore, let the cone on рқ‘Ӣ be quotient space рқҗ¶рқ‘Ӣ = рқ‘ӢГ—рқҗјвҲ•вҲј, where вҲј is the equivalence relation on the product space рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқҗј given by (рқ‘Ҙ, 0) вҲј (рқ‘ҘвҖІ , 0) for all рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘ҘвҖІ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ. Show that рқҗ¶рқ‘Ӣ is path-connected.

Exercise 6.4 Let рқ‘Ӣ be a Hausdorff space, and let рқҗҙ be a subspace of рқ‘Ӣ. Show that рқҗҙ is Hausdorff.

Exercise 6.5 Let рқ‘Ӣ be an infinite set with the cofinite topology.

(a) Show that рқ‘Ӣ is compact.

(b) Show that any subset of рқ‘Ӣ is compact.

Exercise 6.6 Let рқ‘Ӣ be a compact space, and let рқ‘Ң be a Hausdorff space. Furthermore, let рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң be a continuous map.

(a) Show that рқ‘“ is a closed map.

(b) Show that if рқ‘“ is a surjective continuous map, then рқ‘“ is a quotient map.

(c) Show that if рқ‘“ a bijective continuous map, then рқ‘“ is a homeomorphism.

(d) Show that рқ‘“ is proper, i.e., for each subset рқҗҫ of рқ‘Ң that is compact the preimage рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқҗҫ) is compact.

Exercise 6.7 Show that the surface of the cube centered at the origin,

рқҗ¶ = {(рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ, рқ‘§) вҲҲ в„қ3 вҲЈ max{|рқ‘Ҙ|, |рқ‘Ұ|, |рқ‘§|} = 1},

and the 2-sphere, { } |вҲҡ рқ‘Ҷ 2 = (рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ұ, рқ‘§) вҲҲ в„қ3 | рқ‘Ҙ2 + рқ‘Ұ2 + рқ‘§2 = 1 , |

Chapter 6. Topological properties 59

are homeomorphic where they are both considered to be subspaces of в„қ3 with the standard topology.

Exercise 6.8 Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space, and let рқҗҙ1 .рқҗҙ2 , вҖҰ , рқҗҙрқ‘ӣ be subspaces of рқ‘Ӣ each of which is compact. Show that рқ‘ӣ вӢғ рқҗҙрқ‘– рқ‘–=1

is compact.

7. The fundamental group

7.1 Homotopy of paths The topological properties we have discussed so far do not help us to distinguish between, e.g., the 2-sphere рқ‘Ҷ 2 and the torus рқ‘Ү 2 = рқ‘Ҷ 1 Г— рқ‘Ҷ 1 . In order to prove that рқ‘Ҷ 2 and рқ‘Ү 2 are not topologically equivalent, i.e., рқ‘Ҷ 2 вүҮ рқ‘Ү 2 , we need new properties and new techniques. A such property is that of simple connectedness. A path-connected space рқ‘Ӣ is, roughly speaking, simply connected if every closed curve in рқ‘Ӣ can be shrunk to a point in рқ‘Ӣ. The 2-sphere рқ‘Ҷ 2 is simply connected as any closed curve on рқ‘Ҷ 2 can be shrunk to a point in рқ‘Ҷ 2 . On the other hand, the torus рқ‘Ү 2 is not simply connected as there are closed curves which cannot be shrunk to a point in рқ‘Ү 2 . See Figure 7.1. To be simply connected is a topological property. Hence, if рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ң are homeomorphic topological spaces and one of them is simply connected then so is the other. We can express that a path connected space is simply connected by saying that its fundamental group is trivial. In particular, if рқ‘Ӣ вү… рқ‘Ң then their fundamental groups are isomorphic. We will show that the fundamental group of рқ‘Ҷ 2 is not isomorphic to the fundamental group of рқ‘Ү 2 . Hence, рқ‘Ҷ 2 вүҮ рқ‘Ү 2 . Computing fundamental groups provides us with more information than just that of being simply connected.

Figure 7.1: The 2-sphere рқ‘Ҷ 2 is simply connected while the torus рқ‘Ү 2 = рқ‘Ҷ 1 Г—рқ‘Ҷ 1 is not simply connected.

In order to define the fundamental group of a topological space, we will need the concept of ho- motopy (of paths).

nodes.title: Homotopy
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ң be two topological spaces, and let рқ‘“0 , рқ‘“1 вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң be two continuous maps. Furthermore, let в„қ be the set of real numbers with the standard topology, рқҗј = [0, 1] be a subspace of в„қ, and let рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқҗј be given the product topology. We say that рқ‘“0 is homotopic to рқ‘“1 , written рқ‘“0 вүғ рқ‘“1 , if there is a continuous map

рқҗ»вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ Г—рқҗј вҶ’ рқ‘Ң

such that рқҗ»(рқ‘Ҙ, 0) = рқ‘“0 (рқ‘Ҙ) and рқҗ»(рқ‘Ҙ, 1) = рқ‘“1 (рқ‘Ҙ) for all рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ. The map рқҗ» is called a homotopy between рқ‘“0 and рқ‘“1 . If рқ‘“0 вүғ рқ‘“1 and рқ‘“1 is a constant map, we say that рқ‘“0 is nullhomotopic.

Chapter 7. The fundamental group 61

We can think of a homotopy as a continuous one-parameter family {рқҗ»|рқ‘ӢГ—{рқ‘Ў} }рқ‘ЎвҲҲрқҗј of continuous maps from рқ‘Ӣ to рқ‘Ң . See Figure 7.2.

рқ‘“1 рқҗј рқ‘Ң рқ‘Ў рқҗ»

рқ‘Ӣ

рқ‘“0

Figure 7.2: A homotopy from рқ‘“0 to рқ‘“1 .

nodes.title: Straight-line homotopy
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space. Then any two continu- ous maps рқ‘“0 , рқ‘“1 вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ в„қрқ‘ӣ , where в„қрқ‘ӣ is given the standard topology, are homotopic. The map рқҗ» вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқҗј вҶ’ в„қрқ‘ӣ given by

рқҗ»(рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ў) = (1 вҲ’ рқ‘Ў)рқ‘“0 (рқ‘Ҙ) + рқ‘Ўрқ‘“1 (рқ‘Ҙ).

for all рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ and all рқ‘Ў вҲҲ рқҗј = [0, 1] is a homotopy between рқ‘“0 and рқ‘“1 . We call it the straight-line homotopy as it deforms for each рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ the point рқ‘“0 (рқ‘Ҙ) to the point рқ‘“1 (рқ‘Ҙ) along the straight-line segment between them. In particular, any continuous map рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ в„қрқ‘ӣ is homotopic to the constant map рқ‘җ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ в„қрқ‘ӣ given by рқ‘җ(рқ‘Ҙ) = 0 for рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ. Hence, any continuous map into в„қрқ‘ӣ is nullhomotopic.

We will need the following lemma to prove that вүғ is an equivalence relation on the set of all continuous maps between a topological space рқ‘Ӣ and a topological space рқ‘Ң .

nodes.title: Pasting lemma
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ = рқҗҙ вҲӘ рқҗө be a topological space, where рқҗҙ and рқҗө are closed in рқ‘Ӣ. Furthermore, let рқ‘Ң be a topological space, and assume that рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқҗҙ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң and рқ‘” вҲ¶ рқҗө вҶ’ рқ‘Ң are continuous maps. If рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҙ) = рқ‘”(рқ‘Ҙ) for all рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗҙ вҲ© рқҗө, then the map в„Һ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң given by { рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҙ) рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗҙ в„Һ(рқ‘Ҙ) = рқ‘”(рқ‘Ҙ) рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқҗө is continuous.

Proof. We will prove that the preimage of a closed subset of рқ‘Ң under в„Һ is closed in рқ‘Ӣ. Let рқҗ¶ be a closed subset of рқ‘Ң . Then в„ҺвҲ’1 (рқҗ¶) = рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқҗ¶) вҲӘ рқ‘” вҲ’1 (рқҗ¶). Since рқ‘“ is continuous, it follows that рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқҗ¶) is closed in рқҗҙ, and so, it is closed in рқ‘Ӣ. Similarly, рқ‘” вҲ’1 (рқҗ¶) is closed in рқҗө, and so, it is closed in рқ‘Ӣ. Hence, в„ҺвҲ’1 (рқҗ¶) = рқ‘“ вҲ’1 (рқҗ¶) вҲӘ рқ‘” вҲ’1 (рқҗ¶)

62 7.1. Homotopy of paths

is closed in рқ‘Ӣ as it is the union of two closed subsets of рқ‘Ӣ. Thus в„Һ is continuous.

nodes.title: Theorem 7.4
nodes.originalText:
The relation вүғ is an equivalence relation on the set of all continuous maps from a topological space рқ‘Ӣ to a topological space рқ‘Ң .

Proof. We must show that вүғ is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive. For any continuous map рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң there is a homotopy рқҗ» вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқҗј вҶ’ рқ‘Ң given by рқҗ»(рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ў) = рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҙ) for all рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ and all рқ‘Ў вҲҲ рқҗј. Then рқҗ»(рқ‘Ҙ, 0) = рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҙ) and рқҗ»(рқ‘Ҙ, 1) = рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҙ) for all рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ. Hence, рқ‘“ вүғ рқ‘“ . Thus вүғ is reflexive. Let рқ‘“0 вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң and рқ‘“1 вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң be two continuous maps, and let рқҗ» вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқҗј вҶ’ рқ‘Ң be a homotopy between them. Then рқҗ» вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқҗј вҶ’ рқ‘Ң given by рқҗ»(рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ў) = рқҗ»(рқ‘Ҙ, 1 вҲ’ рқ‘Ў) is a homotopy between рқ‘“1 and рқ‘“0 . Hence, рқ‘“0 вүғ рқ‘“1 implies рқ‘“1 вүғ рқ‘“0 . Thus вүғ is symmetric. To show that вүғ is transitive, we must show that if рқ‘“0 вүғ рқ‘“1 and рқ‘“1 вүғ рқ‘“2 , then рқ‘“0 вүғ рқ‘“2 . Let рқҗ»1 вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқҗј вҶ’ рқ‘Ң be a homotopy from рқ‘“0 to рқ‘“1 , and let рқҗ»2 вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқҗј вҶ’ рқ‘Ң be a homotopy from рқ‘“1 to рқ‘“2 . Then there is a map рқҗ»1 вҲ— рқҗ»2 вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқҗј вҶ’ рқ‘Ң given by { рқҗ»1 (рқ‘Ҙ, 2рқ‘Ў) 0 в©Ҫ рқ‘Ў в©Ҫ 1вҲ•2 (рқҗ»1 вҲ— рқҗ»2 )(рқ‘Ҙ, рқ‘Ў) = рқҗ»2 (рқ‘Ҙ, 2рқ‘Ў вҲ’ 1) 1вҲ•2 в©Ҫ рқ‘Ў в©Ҫ 1. It is continuous for all рқ‘Ў вҲҲ рқҗј = [0, 1] by Lemma 7.3 since рқ‘Ӣ Г— [0, 1вҲ•2] and рқ‘Ӣ Г— [1вҲ•2, 1] are closed subsets of рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқҗј whose union is equal to рқ‘Ӣ Г— рқҗј, and рқҗ»1 (рқ‘Ҙ, 1) = рқҗ»2 (рқ‘Ҙ, 0) for all рқ‘Ҙ вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ. Hence, рқҗ»1 вҲ— рқҗ»2 is a homotopy from рқ‘“0 to рқ‘“2 . Thus вүғ is transitive.

nodes.title: Homotopy classes
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘Ң be topological spaces, and let рқҗ¶(рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘Ң ) be the set of continuous maps from рқ‘Ӣ to рқ‘Ң . The homotopy classes in рқҗ¶(рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘Ң ) are the equivalence classes under the relation вүғ. We write [рқ‘“ ] for the homotopy class of рқ‘“ вҲҲ рқҗ¶(рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘Ң ), i.e.,

[рқ‘“ ] = {рқ‘” вҲҲ рқҗ¶(рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘Ң ) вҲЈ рқ‘“ вүғ рқ‘”},

and we write [рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘Ң ] for the set of homotopy classes of continuous maps from рқ‘Ӣ to рқ‘Ң , i.e., [рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘Ң ] = рқҗ¶(рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘Ң )вҲ•вүғ.

If we can connect two points рқ‘Ҙ and рқ‘Ұ in a topological space рқ‘Ӣ by a path, i.e., a continuous map рқ‘“ вҲ¶ [рқ‘Һ, рқ‘Ҹ] вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ such that рқ‘“ (рқ‘Һ) = рқ‘Ҙ and рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҹ) = рқ‘Ұ, cf. Definition 6.14, we say that they lie in the same path component. Specifically, the relation вҲјрқ‘қ given by рқ‘Ҙ вҲјрқ‘қ рқ‘Ұ if and only if there is a path from рқ‘Ҙ to рқ‘Ұ in рқ‘Ӣ, is an equivalence relation on рқ‘Ӣ. We say that an equivalence class in рқ‘Ӣ under вҲјрқ‘қ is a path component. We write рқңӢ0 (рқ‘Ӣ) for the set of path components of рқ‘Ӣ. Note that рқңӢ0 (рқ‘Ӣ) consists of only one element if and only if рқ‘Ӣ is path-connected.

nodes.title: Example 7.6
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ = {рқ‘Ҙ0 } and рқ‘Ң be topological spaces. Then the continuous maps рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң correspond to the points рқ‘“ (рқ‘Ҙ0 ) = рқ‘Ұ вҲҲ рқ‘Ң . Two continuous maps рқ‘“0 , рқ‘“1 вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң are homotopic if and only if there is a path from рқ‘Ұ0 = рқ‘“0 (рқ‘Ҙ0 ) to рқ‘Ұ1 = рқ‘“1 (рқ‘Ҙ0 ). Hence, the homotopy classes of continuous maps from рқ‘Ӣ to рқ‘Ң correspond to the path components of рқ‘Ң , and [рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘Ң ] corresponds to рқңӢ0 (рқ‘Ң ).

We are particularly interested in the case where we have homotopies between paths in a topological space рқ‘Ӣ. For simplicity, let all paths be continuous maps from рқҗј = [0, 1] to рқ‘Ӣ.

for all рқ‘Ў вҲҲ рқҗј. We call рқҗ» a path homotopy from рқ‘“ to рқ‘”.

nodes.title: Theorem 7.10
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space, and let рқ‘Ҙ0 , рқ‘Ҙ1 вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ. Then the relation вүғрқ‘қ is an equivalence relation on the set of all paths from рқ‘Ҙ0 to рқ‘Ҙ1 in рқ‘Ӣ.

Proof. The result follows by proving that the homotopies constructed in the proof of Theorem 7.4 when applied to paths are path homotopies. Let рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқҗј вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ be a path from рқ‘Ҙ0 to рқ‘Ҙ1 . Then the constant homotopy рқҗ» вҲ¶ рқҗј Г— рқҗј вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ given by

рқҗ»(рқ‘ , рқ‘Ў) = рқ‘“ (рқ‘ )

Assume that рқҗ» вҲ¶ рқҗј Г— рқҗј вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ is a path homotopy between paths рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқҗј вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ and рқ‘” вҲ¶ рқҗј вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ from рқ‘Ҙ0 to рқ‘Ҙ1 . Then the homotopy рқҗ» вҲ¶ рқҗј Г— рқҗј вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ given by

рқҗ»(рқ‘ , рқ‘Ў) = рқҗ»(рқ‘ , 1 вҲ’ рқ‘Ў)

is a path homotopy from рқ‘” to рқ‘“ : рқҗ»(рқ‘ , 0) = рқҗ»(рқ‘ , 1) = рқ‘”(рқ‘ ) and рқҗ»(рқ‘ , 1) = рқҗ»(рқ‘ , 0) = рқ‘“ (рқ‘ ) for all рқ‘  вҲҲ рқҗј, and рқҗ»(0, рқ‘Ў) = рқҗ»(0, 1 вҲ’ рқ‘Ў) = рқ‘Ҙ0 and рқҗ»(1, рқ‘Ў) = рқҗ»(1, 1 вҲ’ рқ‘Ў) = рқ‘Ҙ1 for all рқ‘Ў вҲҲ рқҗј. Then the homotopy рқҗ»1 вҲ— рқҗ»2 вҲ¶ рқҗј Г— рқҗј вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ given by { рқҗ»1 (рқ‘ , 2рқ‘Ў) 0 в©Ҫ рқ‘Ў в©Ҫ 1вҲ•2 (рқҗ»1 вҲ— рқҗ»2 )(рқ‘ , рқ‘Ў) = рқҗ»2 (рқ‘ , 2рқ‘Ў вҲ’ 1) 1вҲ•2 в©Ҫ рқ‘Ў в©Ҫ 1

is a path homotopy from рқ‘“0 to рқ‘“2 : (рқҗ»1 вҲ— рқҗ»2 )(рқ‘ , 0) = рқҗ»1 (рқ‘ , 0) = рқ‘“0 (рқ‘ ) and (рқҗ»1 вҲ— рқҗ»2 )(рқ‘ , 1) = рқҗ»2 (рқ‘ , 1) = рқ‘“2 (рқ‘ ) for all рқ‘  вҲҲ рқҗј, and (рқҗ»1 вҲ— рқҗ»2 )(0, рқ‘Ў) = рқ‘Ҙ0 and (рқҗ»1 вҲ— рқҗ»2 )(1, рқ‘Ў) = рқ‘Ҙ1 for all рқ‘Ў вҲҲ рқҗј.

Chapter 7. The fundamental group 65

nodes.title: Path homotopy classes
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space, and let рқ‘Ҙ0 , рқ‘Ҙ1 вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ. If рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқҗј вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ is a path from рқ‘Ҙ0 to рқ‘Ҙ1 , we write [рқ‘“ ] for its path homotopy class, i.e.,

[рқ‘“ ] = {рқ‘” вҲ¶ рқҗј вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ вҲЈ рқ‘” is a path from рқ‘Ҙ0 to рқ‘Ҙ1 and рқ‘“ вүғрқ‘қ рқ‘”}.

The fundamental group of a topological space рқ‘Ӣ with basepoint рқ‘Ҙ0 is, as a set, the set of path homotopy classes of paths from рқ‘Ҙ0 to рқ‘Ҙ0 , i.e., the set of path homotopy classes of loops based at рқ‘Ҙ0 . The group structure of the fundamental group is derived from the following product.

nodes.title: Product of paths
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space, and let рқ‘Ҙ0 , рқ‘Ҙ1 , рқ‘Ҙ2 вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ. If рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқҗј вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ is a path from рқ‘Ҙ0 to рқ‘Ҙ1 , and рқ‘” вҲ¶ рқҗј вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ is a path from рқ‘Ҙ1 to рқ‘Ҙ2 , we define the product of рқ‘“ and рқ‘” as the path рқ‘“ вҲ— рқ‘” вҲ¶ рқҗј вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ from рқ‘Ҙ0 to рқ‘Ҙ2 given by { рқ‘“ (2рқ‘ ) 0 в©Ҫ рқ‘  в©Ҫ 1вҲ•2 (рқ‘“ вҲ— рқ‘”)(рқ‘ ) = рқ‘”(2рқ‘  вҲ’ 1) 1вҲ•2 в©Ҫ рқ‘  в©Ҫ 1.

Note that the map рқ‘“ вҲ— рқ‘” is well-defined and continuous by Lemma 7.3. Furthermore, as (рқ‘“ вҲ— рқ‘”)(0) = рқ‘“ (0) = рқ‘Ҙ0 and (рқ‘“ вҲ— рқ‘”)(1) = рқ‘”(1) = рқ‘Ҙ2 , рқ‘“ вҲ— рқ‘” is a path from рқ‘Ҙ0 to рқ‘Ҙ2 . See Figure 7.5.

nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space, and let рқ‘Ҙ0 , рқ‘Ҙ1 , рқ‘Ҙ2 вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ. If рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқҗј вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ is a path from рқ‘Ҙ0 to рқ‘Ҙ1 and рқ‘” вҲ¶ рқҗј вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ is a path from рқ‘Ҙ1 to рқ‘Ҙ2 , then the product рқ‘“ вҲ— рқ‘” induces a well-defined operation on path homotopy classes given by

[рқ‘“ ] вҲ— [рқ‘”] = [рқ‘“ вҲ— рқ‘”].

Then the map рқҗ»1 вҲ— рқҗ»2 вҲ¶ рқҗј Г— рқҗј вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ given by { рқҗ»1 (2рқ‘ , рқ‘Ў) 0 в©Ҫ рқ‘  в©Ҫ 1вҲ•2 (рқҗ»1 вҲ— рқҗ»2 )(рқ‘ , рқ‘Ў) = рқҗ»2 (2рқ‘  вҲ’ 1, рқ‘Ў) 1вҲ•2 в©Ҫ рқ‘  в©Ҫ 1

66 7.1. Homotopy of paths

is a path homotopy from рқ‘“ вҲ— рқ‘” to рқ‘“ вҖІ вҲ— рқ‘” вҖІ : it is well-defined since рқҗ»1 (1, рқ‘Ў) = рқ‘Ҙ1 = рқҗ»2 (0, рқ‘Ў) for all рқ‘Ў вҲҲ рқҗј and it is continuous by Lemma 7.3, and

nodes.title: Theorem 7.14
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a topological space. Then the product of paths, вҲ—, has the following properties on the set of path homotopy classes in рқ‘Ӣ.

Associativity Let рқ‘Ҙ0 , рқ‘Ҙ1 , рқ‘Ҙ2 and рқ‘Ҙ3 be points in рқ‘Ӣ. If рқ‘“0 вҲ¶ рқҗј вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ is a path from рқ‘Ҙ0 to рқ‘Ҙ1 , рқ‘“1 вҲ¶ рқҗј вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ is a path from рқ‘Ҙ1 to рқ‘Ҙ2 , and рқ‘“2 вҲ¶ рқҗј вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ is a path from рқ‘Ҙ2 to рқ‘Ҙ3 , then

([рқ‘“0 ] вҲ— [рқ‘“1 ]) вҲ— [рқ‘“2 ] = [рқ‘“0 ] вҲ— ([рқ‘“1 ] вҲ— [рқ‘“2 ]).

7.2 Definition and elementary properties of the fundamental group The set of path homotopy classes of paths in a topological space рқ‘Ӣ does not form a group under the product of paths as the product of two path homotopy classes is not always defined. However, such a product is defined if we restrict to paths that begin and end at some fixed point рқ‘Ҙ0 . This is the fundamental group of рқ‘Ӣ with basepoint рқ‘Ҙ0 . We say that the ordered pair (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘Ҙ0 ) is a based space (sometimes also called a pointed space).

nodes.title: The fundamental group
nodes.originalText:
Let (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘Ҙ0 ) be a based space. A path рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқҗј вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ from рқ‘Ҙ0 to рқ‘Ҙ0 is called a loop in рқ‘Ӣ based at рқ‘Ҙ0 . Let

рқңӢ1 (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘Ҙ0 ) = {[рқ‘“ ] вҲЈ рқ‘“ is a loop in рқ‘Ӣ based at рқ‘Ҙ0 }

be the set of path homotopy classes of loops in рқ‘Ӣ based at рқ‘Ҙ0 . We say that рқңӢ1 (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘Ҙ0 ) is the fundamental group of рқ‘Ӣ based at рқ‘Ҙ0 .

From the definition it is clear that рқңӢ1 (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘Ҙ0 ) depends only on the path component of рқ‘Ӣ that contains рқ‘Ҙ0 . The fundamental group does not provide us with any information about the rest of рқ‘Ӣ. As the notation suggests, the fundamental group is sometimes also referred to as the first homotopy group of рқ‘Ӣ. In fact, there are groups рқңӢрқ‘ӣ (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘Ҙ0 ) for all рқ‘ӣ вҲҲ в„Ө+ . These groups are part of a subject called homotopy theory.

If we restrict Theorem 7.14 to the case where all paths are loops in рқ‘Ӣ based at рқ‘Ҙ0 , we get the following result.

nodes.title: Theorem 7.16
nodes.originalText:
Let (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘Ҙ0 ) be a based space. Then the fundamental group рқңӢ1 (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘Ҙ0 ) of рқ‘Ӣ based at рқ‘Ҙ0 is, in fact, a group with product of paths, вҲ—, as its binary operation. The identity element рқ‘’ is equal to the path homotopy class of the constant path at рқ‘Ҙ0 , рқ‘’ = [рқ‘җрқ‘Ҙ0 ], and the inverse of [рқ‘“ ] is [рқ‘“ ]вҲ’1 = [рқ‘“ ] where рқ‘“ is the reverse path of рқ‘“ .

nodes.title: Example 7.17
nodes.originalText: Let в„қ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. If рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқҗј вҶ’ в„қ is a loop in в„қ based at 0, by Example 7.2 we know that рқ‘“ is path homotopic to the constant path at 0. Thus рқңӢ1 (в„қ, 0) is the trivial group, i.e., рқңӢ1 (в„қ, 0) = {рқ‘’}. In general, рқңӢ1 (в„қрқ‘ӣ , 0) is the trivial group, where в„қрқ‘ӣ is given the standard topology. We will often denote the trivial group as simply 0.

nodes.title: Example 7.18
nodes.originalText: Let (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘Ҙ0 ) be a based space where рқ‘Ӣ is a discrete space. Then рқңӢ1 (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘Ҙ0 ) = 0 for all рқ‘Ҙ0 вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ as the path component containing рқ‘Ҙ0 consists of only one element, namely рқ‘Ҙ0 .

In light of Theorem 7.19 it might be tempting to simply speak of the fundamental group of a path-connected space рқ‘Ӣ, and thus leave out any mention of the basepoint. However, while рқңӢ1 (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘Ҙ0 ) вү… рқңӢ1 (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘Ҙ1 ) for any two points рқ‘Ҙ0 and рқ‘Ҙ1 in рқ‘Ӣ, the isomorphism may depend on the path from рқ‘Ҙ0 to рқ‘Ҙ1 . It can be shown that the isomorphism of рқңӢ1 (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘Ҙ0 ) with рқңӢ1 (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘Ҙ1 ) for a path-connected space рқ‘Ӣ is independent of path if and only if the fundamental group is abelian.

72 7.2. Definition and elementary properties of the fundamental group

nodes.title: Simply connected spaces
nodes.originalText:
Let рқ‘Ӣ be a path-connected space. We say that рқ‘Ӣ is simply connected if рқңӢ1 (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘Ҙ0 ) is the trivial group for some рқ‘Ҙ0 вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ, and hence, for all рқ‘Ҙ0 вҲҲ рқ‘Ӣ.

By Example 7.17, we get that в„қрқ‘ӣ is simply connected where в„қрқ‘ӣ is given the standard topology. We shall later see that the circle рқ‘Ҷ 1 and the torus рқ‘Ү 2 = рқ‘Ҷ 1 Г— рқ‘Ҷ 1 are two examples of non-simply connected spaces.

nodes.title: Based maps
nodes.originalText:
Let (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘Ҙ0 ) and (рқ‘Ң , рқ‘Ұ0 ) be based spaces. A based map в„Һ вҲ¶ (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘Ҙ0 ) вҶ’ (рқ‘Ң , рқ‘Ұ0 ) is a continuous map в„Һ вҲ¶ рқ‘Ӣ вҶ’ рқ‘Ң such that в„Һ(рқ‘Ҙ0 ) = рқ‘Ұ0 .

Let (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘Ҙ0 ) and (рқ‘Ң , рқ‘Ұ0 ) be based spaces, and let в„Һ вҲ¶ (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘Ҙ0 ) вҶ’ (рқ‘Ң , рқ‘Ұ0 ) be a based map. If рқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқҗј вҶ’ рқ‘Ӣ is a loop in рқ‘Ӣ based at рқ‘Ҙ0 , the composition в„Һв—Ұрқ‘“ вҲ¶ рқҗј вҶ’ рқ‘Ң is a loop in рқ‘Ң based at рқ‘Ұ0 . This leads to a map from рқңӢ1 (рқ‘Ӣ, рқ‘Ҙ0 ) to рқңӢ1 (рқ‘Ң , рқ‘Ұ0 ).

nodes.originalText:

and so { в„Һ(рқ‘“ (2рқ‘ )) 0 в©Ҫ рқ‘  в©Ҫ 1вҲ•2 (в„Һв—Ұ(рқ‘“ вҲ— рқ‘”))(рқ‘ ) = ((в„Һв—Ұрқ‘“ ) вҲ— (в„Һв—Ұрқ‘”))(рқ‘ ) = в„Һ(рқ‘”(2рқ‘  вҲ’ 1)) 1вҲ•2 в©Ҫ рқ‘  в©Ҫ 1. nodes.originalText:

(в„Һ2 в—Ұв„Һ1 )вҲ— = (в„Һ2 )вҲ— в—Ұ(в„Һ1 )вҲ— .
